tasks.description: Multi-task анализ макета документов на 2500 реальных изображениях DocLayNet (2000 train + 500 test, 800×1100, .npy): (A) классификация типа документа из 6 классов — Accuracy, 30 баллов; (B) детекция 6 структурных элементов с bbox в COCO-формате — 40 баллов; (C) попиксельная semantic segmentation на 7 классов (фон + 6 элементов) — 30 баллов. Можно решать любое подмножество задач, итог = сумма баллов (max 100). Метрики для B/C — стандартные mAP / IoU; формула A: `max(0, min(30, (acc − 0.5) × 60))`.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract A legal tech company needs to automatically process thousands of scanned documents daily. They require three core capabilities: **identifying document types**, **locating structural elements**, and **generating precise pixel-level masks** for each element. Your task is to build a **multi-task computer vision system** that solves all three problems simultaneously. You can tackle one, two, or all three tasks — each scored independently. This challenge covers three fundamental computer vision problems in one competition: | Task | Problem | CV Discipline | Points | |------|---------|---------------|--------| | **A** | What type of document is this? | Image Classification | 30 | | **B** | Where are the elements located? | Object Detection | 40 | | **C** | What does each pixel belong to? | Semantic Segmentation | 30 | **Total: 100 points** ### Task A: Document Classification (30 points) Classify each document image into one of **6 categories**: | ID | Document Type | Examples | |----|---------------|----------| | 0 | financial | Invoices, bank statements, tax forms | | 1 | scientific | Research papers, journal articles | | 2 | legal | Contracts, court filings, legal briefs | | 3 | government | Official forms, permits, certificates | | 4 | technical | Manuals, specifications, data sheets | | 5 | other | Miscellaneous documents | **Metric:** Accuracy **Scoring:** points = max(0, min(30, (accuracy − 0.5) × 60)) | Accuracy | Points | |----------|--------| | ≤ 0.50 | 0 | | 0.67 | 10 | | 0.83 | 20 | | 1.00 | 30 | --- ### Task B: Layout Detection (40 points) Detect and localize document elements with **bounding boxes**. Each detection includes a class label and confidence score. | ID | Element Type | Description | |----|-------------|-------------| | 0 | text | Body text paragraphs | | 1 | title | Section headers, document titles | | 2 | table | Tabular data | | 3 | figure | Images, charts, diagrams | | 4 | list | Bulleted or numbered lists | | 5 | other | Footers, page numbers, stamps | **Metric:** mAP @ IoU=0.5 (mean Average Precision) **Scoring:** points = max(0, min(40, (mAP − 0.2) × 50)) | mAP | Points | |-----|--------| | ≤ 0.20 | 0 | | 0.40 | 10 | | 0.60 | 20 | | 0.80 | 30 | | 1.00 | 40 | --- ### Task C: Element Segmentation (30 points) Generate **pixel-wise segmentation masks** for all document elements. Each pixel is assigned a class label (0 = background, 1-6 = element types matching Task B categories). **Metric:** mIoU (mean Intersection over Union) **Scoring:** points = max(0, min(30, (mIoU − 0.3) × 43)) | mIoU | Points | |------|--------| | ≤ 0.30 | 0 | | 0.53 | 10 | | 0.77 | 20 | | 1.00 | 30 | --- --- ## data-description The dataset contains **2,500 real document images** (2,000 train + 500 test) at resolution **800×1100 pixels**, sourced from **IBM DocLayNet** (Pfitzmann et al., KDD 2022) — a large-scale, human-annotated dataset for document layout analysis. - **Document type labels** (Task A): real `doc_category` labels from DocLayNet — financial reports, scientific articles, laws & regulations, government tenders, manuals, patents - **Bounding box annotations** (Task B): real manual annotations from DocLayNet (11 original categories mapped to 6) - **Segmentation masks** (Task C): derived from real bounding box annotations — standard practice in document layout analysis All data is stored in **NPY format** for fast loading (no need to read individual image files). ### Files **Images & IDs:** | File | Shape | Description | |------|-------|-------------| | `train_images.npy` | (2000, 1100, 800, 3) uint8 | Training images (RGB) | | `test_images.npy` | (500, 1100, 800, 3) uint8 | Test images (RGB) | | `train_ids.npy` | (2000,) | Training image IDs (strings, use `allow_pickle=True`) | | `test_ids.npy` | (500,) | Test image IDs (strings, use `allow_pickle=True`) | **Task A — Classification:** | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `train_labels_a.npy` | (2000,) int64 — document type labels (0-5) | **Task B — Detection:** | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `task_b/train.json` | COCO-format annotations: images, annotations (bbox + category_id), categories | | `train_b_img_to_idx.npy` | dict — image_id → index mapping for fast lookup (use `allow_pickle=True`) | **Task C — Segmentation:** | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `train_masks.npy` | (2000, 1100, 800) uint8 — pixel-wise class labels (0=background, 1-6=element types) | **Metadata & Submission:** | File | Description | |------|-------------| | `class_info.json` | Document types, element categories, image size | | `metadata.npy` | Dataset metadata (use `allow_pickle=True`) | | `sample_submission.csv` | Unified submission template for all 3 tasks | ### Loading Data ```python import os import numpy as np import json DATA = "/kaggle/input/document-layout-analysis" # Images train_images = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "train_images.npy")) # (2000, 1100, 800, 3) uint8 test_images = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "test_images.npy")) # (500, 1100, 800, 3) uint8 # IDs (string arrays → need allow_pickle) train_ids = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "train_ids.npy"), allow_pickle=True) # (2000,) test_ids = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "test_ids.npy"), allow_pickle=True) # (500,) # Task A: Classification labels (0-5) train_labels = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "train_labels_a.npy")) # (2000,) int64 # Task B: Detection annotations (COCO format) with open(os.path.join(DATA, "task_b", "train.json")) as f: coco_data = json.load(f) # coco_data keys: "images", "annotations", "categories" # Each annotation: {"id", "image_id", "category_id", "bbox": [x,y,w,h], "area", "iscrowd"} # Task B: fast image_id → index lookup img_to_idx = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "train_b_img_to_idx.npy"), allow_pickle=True).item() # Task C: Segmentation masks (0=background, 1-6=element classes) train_masks = np.load(os.path.join(DATA, "train_masks.npy")) # (2000, 1100, 800) uint8 # Class info with open(os.path.join(DATA, "class_info.json")) as f: class_info = json.load(f) # class_info keys: "doc_types", "element_categories", "image_size", "source" ``` --- ## Submission Format Submit a **single CSV file** containing predictions for all tasks. **Columns:** `image_id, task, doc_type, category_id, bbox_x, bbox_y, bbox_w, bbox_h, confidence, rle_mask` ### Task A rows (one row per test image): ```csv image_id,task,doc_type,category_id,bbox_x,bbox_y,bbox_w,bbox_h,confidence,rle_mask doc_abc123,A,financial,,,,,,, ``` - `task` = "A" - `doc_type` = one of: financial, scientific, legal, government, technical, other - All other fields: empty ### Task B rows (multiple rows per image, one per detection): ```csv image_id,task,doc_type,category_id,bbox_x,bbox_y,bbox_w,bbox_h,confidence,rle_mask doc_abc123,B,,0,60.0,100.0,680.0,150.0,0.95, doc_abc123,B,,1,60.0,50.0,400.0,40.0,0.88, ``` - `task` = "B" - `category_id` = element class (0-5) - `bbox_x, bbox_y` = top-left corner coordinates - `bbox_w, bbox_h` = width and height - `confidence` = detection confidence (0.0 to 1.0) - Other fields: empty ### Task C rows (one row per test image): ```csv image_id,task,doc_type,category_id,bbox_x,bbox_y,bbox_w,bbox_h,confidence,rle_mask doc_abc123,C,,,,,,,,100 50 200 30 500 100 ``` - `task` = "C" - `rle_mask` = Run-Length Encoded segmentation mask (see below) - Other fields: empty ### RLE Mask Encoding Masks are encoded as Run-Length Encoding (RLE). The mask is **flattened row-by-row** (1100 rows × 800 columns = 880,000 pixels), then runs of non-zero values are encoded as `start length` pairs: ``` Format: "start1 length1 start2 length2 ..." ``` Example: `"100 50 200 30"` means pixels 100-149 and 200-229 are non-zero. ```python def rle_encode(mask): """Encode segmentation mask (H, W) to RLE string.""" pixels = mask.flatten() runs = [] prev = 0 start = 0 for i, val in enumerate(pixels): if val != prev: if prev > 0: runs.append(f"{start} {i - start}") if val > 0: start = i prev = val if prev > 0: runs.append(f"{start} {len(pixels) - start}") return " ".join(runs) def rle_decode(rle_string, shape=(1100, 800)): """Decode RLE string to mask (H, W).""" mask = np.zeros(shape[0] * shape[1], dtype=np.uint8) if not rle_string or pd.isna(rle_string): return mask.reshape(shape) parts = rle_string.split() for i in range(0, len(parts), 2): start = int(parts[i]) length = int(parts[i + 1]) mask[start:start + length] = 1 return mask.reshape(shape) ``` **Note:** You can submit for any subset of tasks. Simply omit rows for tasks you don't solve — those tasks will score 0 points. --- ## Evaluation Your score is calculated as: ``` Total = points_A + points_B + points_C where: points_A = max(0, min(30, (accuracy - 0.5) × 60)) points_B = max(0, min(40, (mAP@0.5 - 0.2) × 50)) points_C = max(0, min(30, (mIoU - 0.3) × 43)) ``` | Strategy | Task A | Task B | Task C | Expected Total | |----------|--------|--------|--------|----------------| | Task A only | ~25 | 0 | 0 | ~25 pts | | A + B | ~25 | ~25 | 0 | ~50 pts | | All three (baseline) | ~20 | ~25 | ~15 | ~60 pts | | All three (optimized) | ~28 | ~35 | ~25 | ~88 pts | --- --- ## Evaluation **Total = points_A + points_B + points_C** (max 100) You can submit predictions for **any subset** of tasks. Missing tasks receive 0 points. --- ## rules ##ENTRY IN THIS COMPETITION CONSTITUTES YOUR ACCEPTANCE OF THESE OFFICIAL COMPETITION RULES. **[See Section 3.18 for defined terms](rules#18.-terms)** *The Competition named below is a skills-based competition to promote and further the field of data science. You must register via the Competition Website to enter. To enter the Competition, you must agree to these Official Competition Rules, which incorporate by reference the provisions and content of the Competition Website and any Specific Competition Rules herein (collectively, the "Rules"). Please read these Rules carefully before entry to ensure you understand and agree. You further agree that Submission in the Competition constitutes agreement to these Rules. You may not submit to the Competition and are not eligible to receive the prizes associated with this Competition unless you agree to these Rules. These Rules form a binding legal agreement between you and the Competition Sponsor with respect to the Competition. Your competition Submissions must conform to the requirements stated on the Competition Website. Your Submissions will be scored based on the evaluation metric described on the Competition Website. Subject to compliance with the Competition Rules, Prizes, if any, will be awarded to Participants with the best scores, based on the merits of the data science models submitted. See below for the complete Competition Rules.* **You cannot sign up to Kaggle from multiple accounts and therefore you cannot enter or submit from multiple accounts.**

tasks.description: Многоклассовая CV-классификация изображений (Fashion-MNIST) на 10 категорий одежды. Train: 60 000 grayscale-картинок 28×28 (npy), test: 10 000 (5K public + 5K private). Метрика — Accuracy; специальные ограничения по моделям и претрейнам указаны в baseline-ноутбуке организаторов, действуют общие правила Cyprus AI Camp 2026.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Check the baseline notebook published by organizers in the `Code` section of the competition: it has description of the task, description of the data, and specific rules that you must follow while solving this problem. Read them carefully. In case of any question, ask them in `Discussions` section of the competition. Good luck!

---

## data-description

# Dataset Description

## Overview

You are provided with the **Fashion-MNIST** dataset - a collection of grayscale images of fashion items. Your task is to build an **image classification model** that can correctly identify the type of clothing item in each image.

This is a **multi-class classification problem** with 10 categories.

## What am I predicting?

For each image in the test set, predict the **category** (integer 0-9) of the fashion item shown.

## Files

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `train_images.npy` | Training images as NumPy array. Shape: (60000, 28, 28) |
| `train_labels.npy` | Training labels as NumPy array. Shape: (60000,) |
| `public_test_images.npy` | Public test images. Shape: (5000, 28, 28) |
| `private_test_images.npy` | Private test images. Shape: (5000, 28, 28) |
| `sample_submission.csv` | Example submission file showing the correct format |

## Image Format

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Image size | 28 × 28 pixels |
| Color channels | 1 (grayscale) |
| Pixel values | 0-255 (uint8) |
| Total training images | 60,000 |
| Total test images | 10,000 (5K public + 5K private) |

## Categories

| Category ID | Label | Description |
|-------------|-------|-------------|
| 0 | T-shirt/top | T-shirts and tops |
| 1 | Trouser | Pants and trousers |
| 2 | Pullover | Pullovers and sweaters |
| 3 | Dress | Dresses |
| 4 | Coat | Coats and jackets |
| 5 | Sandal | Sandals |
| 6 | Shirt | Shirts (button-up) |
| 7 | Sneaker | Sneakers and athletic shoes |
| 8 | Bag | Bags and purses |
| 9 | Ankle boot | Ankle boots |

## Submission Format

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `Id` | int | Image index (0 to 9999) |
| `Category` | int | Predicted category (0-9) |

### sample_submission.csv
```csv
Id,Category
0,6
1,3
2,7
3,4
4,6
...
9999,4
```

**Note**: 
- `Id` 0-4999 corresponds to `public_test_images.npy`
- `Id` 5000-9999 corresponds to `private_test_images.npy`

## Loading Data (PyTorch)

```python
import numpy as np
import torch

# Load data
train_images = np.load('data/train_images.npy')
train_labels = np.load('data/train_labels.npy')
test_images = np.load('data/public_test_images.npy')

# Normalize and reshape for CNN
X = train_images.astype('float32') / 255.0
X = X.reshape(-1, 1, 28, 28)  # (N, C, H, W) format for PyTorch

# Convert to tensors
X_tensor = torch.FloatTensor(X)
y_tensor = torch.LongTensor(train_labels)
```

## Data Statistics

- **Training set**: 60,000 images (6,000 per category - balanced)
- **Public test set**: 5,000 images
- **Private test set**: 5,000 images

### Class Distribution (Training)
```
T-shirt/top:  6,000 images
Trouser:      6,000 images
Pullover:     6,000 images
Dress:        6,000 images
Coat:         6,000 images
Sandal:       6,000 images
Shirt:        6,000 images
Sneaker:      6,000 images
Bag:          6,000 images
Ankle boot:   6,000 images
```

## Hints

1. **Framework**: Use **PyTorch** (as specified in IOAI rules)

2. **Normalization**: Scale pixel values from [0, 255] to [0, 1] or [-1, 1]

3. **Architecture**: Start with a simple CNN:
   - Conv2D → ReLU → MaxPool → Conv2D → ReLU → MaxPool → Flatten → Dense → Output

4. **Data Augmentation**: Improve generalization with:
   - Random horizontal flip
   - Random rotation (small angles)
   - Random crop

5. **Regularization**: Prevent overfitting with:
   - Dropout
   - Batch normalization
   - Weight decay

6. **Training Tips**:
   - Use Adam optimizer with lr=0.001
   - Train for 10-20 epochs
   - Use batch size 64-128
   - Monitor validation accuracy

## Evaluation

Your submission will be scored using **Classification Accuracy**:

```
accuracy = correct_predictions / total_predictions
```

**Points formula:**
```
points = min(100, 3.2 × e^(13.9 × (accuracy - 0.75)) - 3.2)
```

| Accuracy | Points |
|----------|--------|
| 0.75 | 0 |
| 0.80 | 5 |
| 0.85 | 10 |
| 0.90 | 20 |
| 0.95 | 50 |
| 1.00 | 100 |

## Expected Performance

| Approach | Expected Accuracy |
|----------|-------------------|
| Random guessing | ~10% |
| Simple MLP | 85-87% |
| Basic CNN | 88-90% |
| Tuned CNN | 91-93% |
| Advanced models | 93-95% |

## Important Notes

- Submit predictions for **both** public and private test sets in a single file
- Ids must be integers from 0 to 9999
- Category must be integers from 0 to 9
- No missing values allowed in submission
- Use PyTorch (TensorFlow submissions may not be accepted)

---

## Evaluation

Accuracy (Categorization Accuracy)

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

A new wide-field telescope has captured tens of thousands of galaxy images. Astronomers need these galaxies sorted by morphology (shape and structure) to study galaxy evolution, mergers, and spiral formation — but manual labeling is far too slow. **Your task is to build a Computer Vision classifier that automatically predicts the morphology class from an RGB image.**


## Goal

Build a model that classifies galaxy images into one of **10 morphology categories**:

| Class | Morphology |
|-------|------------|
| 0 | Disturbed |
| 1 | Merging |
| 2 | Round Smooth |
| 3 | Smooth, Cigar shaped |
| 4 | Cigar Shaped Smooth |
| 5 | Barred Spiral |
| 6 | Unbarred Tight Spiral |
| 7 | Unbarred Loose Spiral |
| 8 | Edge-on without Bulge |
| 9 | Edge-on with Bulge |

---

## Description

You will work with the **Galaxy10 DECaLS** dataset containing approximately **20,000** color galaxy cutouts at **256×256 pixels**. The dataset is derived from the Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey and labeled according to Galaxy Zoo classifications.

---

## data-description

# Dataset Description

## Overview

The dataset contains galaxy images from the Galaxy10 DECaLS survey. Your goal is to classify each galaxy into one of 10 morphology categories based on its visual appearance.

**What am I predicting?**
For each galaxy image in the test set, you must predict the `Category` — an integer from 0 to 9 representing the galaxy's morphology class.

## Files

* **train_images.npy** — Training set images. NumPy array of shape `(N, 256, 256, 3)` containing RGB galaxy images.
* **train_labels.npy** — Training set labels. NumPy array of shape `(N,)` containing integer class labels (0-9).
* **public_test_images.npy** — Public test set images used for the public leaderboard. NumPy array of shape `(M, 256, 256, 3)`.
* **private_test_images.npy** — Private test set images used for final scoring. NumPy array of shape `(K, 256, 256, 3)`.
* **sample_submission.csv** — A sample submission file in the correct format.
* **class_names.json** — JSON array mapping class indices (0-9) to human-readable morphology names.

## Data Format

### Images (.npy files)

Images are stored as NumPy arrays with the following specifications:

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Dimensions | 256 x 256 pixels |
| Channels | 3 (RGB) |
| Data type | uint8 (0-255) |
| Format | NumPy .npy file |

**Loading example:**
```python
import numpy as np

train_images = np.load('data/train_images.npy')
train_labels = np.load('data/train_labels.npy')

print(train_images.shape)  # (N, 256, 256, 3)
print(train_labels.shape)  # (N,)
```

### Submission Format (CSV)

Your submission file must contain exactly two columns:

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `Id` | Integer index of the test image (0-indexed, matching array order) |
| `Category` | Predicted morphology class (integer 0-9) |

**Example:**
```
Id,Category
0,2
1,6
2,1
3,5
4,0
```

## Morphology Classes

| Category | Name | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| 0 | Disturbed | Galaxies with irregular, disturbed morphology |
| 1 | Merging | Galaxies in the process of merging |
| 2 | Round Smooth | Elliptical galaxies with round, smooth appearance |
| 3 | Smooth, Cigar shaped | Elongated smooth elliptical galaxies |
| 4 | Cigar Shaped Smooth | Highly elongated smooth galaxies |
| 5 | Barred Spiral | Spiral galaxies with a central bar structure |
| 6 | Unbarred Tight Spiral | Spiral galaxies with tightly wound arms, no bar |
| 7 | Unbarred Loose Spiral | Spiral galaxies with loosely wound arms, no bar |
| 8 | Edge-on without Bulge | Disk galaxies viewed edge-on, no visible bulge |
| 9 | Edge-on with Bulge | Disk galaxies viewed edge-on, with visible central bulge |

## Acronyms

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| DECaLS | Dark Energy Camera Legacy Survey |
| RGB | Red, Green, Blue color channels |
| NPY | NumPy binary file format |

---

## Evaluation

Submissions are evaluated on **Classification Accuracy** — the percentage of correctly classified galaxies on the private test set.

$$\text{Score} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}}$$

## Submission File
Submit a CSV file with predictions for each test image:

```
Id,Category
0,2
1,6
2,1
...
```

---

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This challenge requires building a multimodal model that combines:
1. Computer Vision for dermoscopic image analysis
2. Tabular data processing for patient metadata (age, sex, anatomic site)

The goal is to classify skin lesions into **7 diagnostic categories** from the ISIC/HAM10000 dataset:

| Code | Name | Type |
|------|------|------|
| **mel** | Melanoma | Malignant |
| **bcc** | Basal Cell Carcinoma | Malignant |
| **akiec** | Actinic Keratosis | Pre-cancerous |
| **bkl** | Benign Keratosis | Benign |
| **nv** | Melanocytic Nevus | Benign |
| **df** | Dermatofibroma | Benign |
| **vasc** | Vascular Lesion | Benign |

---

## data-description

# Multimodal Skin Lesion Classification

Classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions using both image data and patient metadata.

## Task

Build a multimodal model that predicts the diagnosis of skin lesions into 7 categories:

| Code | Name | Type |
|------|------|------|
| mel | Melanoma | Malignant |
| bcc | Basal Cell Carcinoma | Malignant |
| akiec | Actinic Keratosis | Pre-cancerous |
| bkl | Benign Keratosis | Benign |
| nv | Melanocytic Nevus | Benign |
| df | Dermatofibroma | Benign |
| vasc | Vascular Lesion | Benign |

## Files

**Images:**
- `images/train/` - Training images (4,000 JPEGs, 224x224)
- `images/test/` - Test images (1,000 JPEGs)

**NPY files (preprocessed, for fast loading):**
- `train_images.npy` - (4000, 224, 224, 3) uint8
- `train_labels.npy` - (4000,) int64
- `train_tabular.npy` - (4000, 3) [age, sex, site]
- `test_images.npy` - (1000, 224, 224, 3) uint8
- `test_tabular.npy` - (1000, 3) [age, sex, site]
- `train_ids.npy`, `test_ids.npy` - image IDs
- `metadata.npy` - class mappings

**CSV files:**
- `train.csv` - Training labels and patient metadata
- `test.csv` - Test patient metadata (no labels)
- `sample_submission.csv` - Example submission format
- `class_info.json` - Class and category mappings

## Metadata

| Column | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| image_id | Unique image identifier |
| age | Patient age (NaN if missing) |
| sex | male / female / unknown |
| anatom_site | Lesion location on body |
| diagnosis | Target label (train only) |

## Submission Format

```csv
image_id,diagnosis
img_abc123,mel
img_def456,nv
img_ghi789,bcc
```

---

## Evaluation

The `metric.py` file calculates Weighted F1-Score:

```python
def score(solution: pd.DataFrame, submission: pd.DataFrame) -> float:
    """
    Calculate Weighted F1-Score across 7 classes.
    Returns score between 0 and 1.
    """
    ...
```

---

## rules

This competition is subject to general Cyprus AI Camp rules that can be found here: https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/cyprus-ai-camp-2026-tasks/rules

Special rules and limitations that apply for this specific task can be found in the baseline published by organizers in the `Code` section

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Check the baseline notebook published by organizers in the `Code` section of the competition: it has description of the task, description of the data, and specific rules that you must follow while solving this problem. Read them carefully. In case of any question, ask them in `Discussions` section of the competition. Good luck!

---

## data-description

# Dataset Description

## Overview

You are provided with physicochemical measurements of Portuguese "Vinho Verde" red wines. Your task is to build a **classification model** that predicts the quality rating assigned by wine experts.

This is a **multi-class classification problem** with 6 quality levels (3-8).

## What am I predicting?

For each wine sample in the test set, predict the **quality** score (integer from 3 to 8).

## Files

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `train.csv` | Training data with 11 features and quality labels (1,279 samples) |
| `val.csv` | Validation data with labels for local evaluation (320 samples) |
| `public_test.csv` | Public test set without labels (160 samples) |
| `private_test.csv` | Private test set without labels (160 samples) |
| `sample_submission.csv` | Example submission file showing the correct format |

## Features

| Feature | Type | Description |
|---------|------|-------------|
| `fixed acidity` | float | Tartaric acid concentration (g/dm³) |
| `volatile acidity` | float | Acetic acid concentration (g/dm³) - too high leads to vinegar taste |
| `citric acid` | float | Citric acid concentration (g/dm³) - adds freshness |
| `residual sugar` | float | Sugar remaining after fermentation (g/dm³) |
| `chlorides` | float | Sodium chloride concentration (g/dm³) |
| `free sulfur dioxide` | float | Free SO₂ (mg/dm³) - prevents microbial growth |
| `total sulfur dioxide` | float | Total SO₂ (mg/dm³) - free + bound forms |
| `density` | float | Wine density (g/cm³) |
| `pH` | float | Acidity level (0-14 scale) |
| `sulphates` | float | Potassium sulphate concentration (g/dm³) |
| `alcohol` | float | Alcohol content (% by volume) |

## Target Variable

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `quality` | int | Wine quality score (3, 4, 5, 6, 7, or 8) |

### Quality Scale

| Score | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| 3 | Very poor |
| 4 | Poor |
| 5 | Below average |
| 6 | Above average |
| 7 | Good |
| 8 | Excellent |

## Submission Format

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `Id` | int | Sample ID (0 to 319) |
| `quality` | int | Predicted quality (3-8) |

### sample_submission.csv
```csv
Id,quality
0,5
1,5
2,5
3,5
4,5
...
319,5
```

**Note**: 
- `Id` 0-159 corresponds to `public_test.csv`
- `Id` 160-319 corresponds to `private_test.csv`

## Loading Data (Python)

```python
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Load data
train_df = pd.read_csv('data/train.csv')
public_test = pd.read_csv('data/public_test.csv')
private_test = pd.read_csv('data/private_test.csv')

# Separate features and target
feature_cols = [col for col in train_df.columns if col != 'quality']
X_train = train_df[feature_cols].values
y_train = train_df['quality'].values

# Scale features
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
```

## Data Statistics

- **Training set**: 1,279 samples
- **Validation set**: 320 samples (with labels - use for local evaluation)
- **Public test set**: 160 samples
- **Private test set**: 160 samples
- **Total features**: 11

### Class Distribution (Training)

```
Quality 3:    8 samples  (0.6%)
Quality 4:   42 samples  (3.3%)
Quality 5:  545 samples (42.6%)
Quality 6:  510 samples (39.9%)
Quality 7:  159 samples (12.4%)
Quality 8:   15 samples  (1.2%)
```

## Evaluation

Your submission will be scored using **Weighted F1-Score**:

```
F1 = 2 × (precision × recall) / (precision + recall)
```

Weighted F1 accounts for class imbalance by weighting each class by its support.

**Points formula:**
```
points = max(0, min(100, (F1 - 0.40) × 166.67))
```

| F1-Score | Points |
|----------|--------|
| 0.40 | 0 |
| 0.50 | 17 |
| 0.60 | 33 |
| 0.70 | 50 |
| 0.80 | 67 |
| 0.90 | 83 |
| 1.00 | 100 |

## Expected Performance

| Approach | Expected F1-Score |
|----------|-------------------|
| Random guessing | ~0.15 |
| Always predict 5 | ~0.25 |
| Basic Random Forest | 0.55-0.65 |
| Tuned ensemble | 0.65-0.75 |
| Top solutions | 0.75+ |

## Important Notes

- Submit predictions for **both** public and private test sets in a single file
- `Id` must be integers from 0 to 319
- `quality` must be integers from 3 to 8
- No missing values allowed in submission
- The dataset is imbalanced - accuracy alone is misleading!

## Data Source

This dataset is based on the Wine Quality dataset from UCI Machine Learning Repository:
- P. Cortez et al. "Modeling wine preferences by data mining from physicochemical properties"
- Decision Support Systems, Elsevier, 47(4):547-553, 2009

---

## Evaluation

Your submission will be scored using **Weighted F1-Score**:

```
F1 = 2 × (precision × recall) / (precision + recall)
```

Weighted F1 accounts for class imbalance by weighting each class by its support.

**Points formula:**
```
points = max(0, min(100, (F1 - 0.40) × 166.67))
```

---

## rules

This competition is subject to general Cyprus AI Camp rules that can be found here: https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/cyprus-ai-camp-2026-tasks/rules

Special rules and limitations that apply for this specific task can be found in the baseline published by organizers in the `Code` section

tasks.description: Аудио-классификация: распознавание 35 голосовых команд (Google Speech Commands v2) по сырым waveforms длительностью 1 секунда при 16 кГц. Дано train_waveforms.npy формы (N, 16000) и train_labels.npy, public+private test ~2120 (private_test_waveforms.npy). Метрика — Accuracy (points = (Acc-0.40)*166.67, clamp 0-100). Можно работать с raw audio (1D CNN) или mel-spectrogram (2D CNN), augmentation поощряется.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract The score in this problem will be fractional, from 0 to 100 points. --- ## Description ## The Last Frequency — Speech Command Recognition *Year 2051.* After the uprising of **LAIda** — the rogue AI that once terrorized the world with weaponized cuteness — Earth has plunged into chaos. Deep beneath the Troodos Mountains in Cyprus, you discover **Bunker Omega** — humanity's last hope. Its control system is **voice-activated**, responding to **35 specific voice commands**. Decades of neglect have degraded the system — it now misidentifies commands more often than not. ### Goal Train a neural network to accurately classify **35 voice commands** from raw audio waveforms. Each audio sample is 1 second long, recorded at 16kHz (16,000 samples per waveform). ### Context The dataset is a subset of **Google Speech Commands v2** — a standard benchmark for keyword spotting and speech recognition. The 35 commands include directions (*yes, no, up, down, left, right, go, stop*), digits (*zero through nine*), actions (*on, off, forward, backward, follow, learn, visual*), and words (*bed, bird, cat, dog, happy, house, marvin, sheila, tree, wow*). The data is provided as **raw audio waveforms** (not spectrograms). You choose the representation: - Convert to mel-spectrograms and use 2D CNNs (image classification approach) - Work directly with raw audio using 1D CNNs - Different representations may have different performance ceilings... ### Evaluation Submissions are scored on **Accuracy** (higher is better). See the Evaluation tab for the full scoring formula. ### Acknowledgements Data derived from the [Google Speech Commands Dataset v2](https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.03209) (Warden, 2018). This is a learning competition — no monetary prizes are awarded. --- ## data-description The dataset contains raw audio waveforms from Google Speech Commands v2. Each sample is **1 second of audio** recorded at **16kHz** (16,000 samples per waveform), normalized to the range [-1, 1]. ### The 35 Command Classes | Category | Commands | |----------|----------| | **Directions** | yes, no, up, down, left, right, go, stop | | **Actions** | on, off, forward, backward, follow, learn, visual | | **Digits** | zero, one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine | | **Words** | bed, bird, cat, dog, happy, house, marvin, sheila, tree, wow | ### Files - **train_waveforms.npy** — Training audio waveforms, shape `(N, 16000)`, float32, normalized to [-1, 1]. - **train_labels.npy** — Training labels as integer indices, shape `(N,)`. - **public_test_waveforms.npy** — Public test audio (no labels). Predict the command for each sample. - **private_test_waveforms.npy** — Private test audio (no labels). Used for final scoring. - **label_map.json** — Mapping from integer label index to command name string (e.g., `{"0": "backward", "1": "bed", ...}`). - **sample_submission.csv** — A sample submission file in the correct format (`Id,Command`). ### Tips - You can convert waveforms to **mel-spectrograms** using `torchaudio` for 2D CNN approaches. - Or work directly with **raw audio** using 1D convolutions. - Different data representations may have different performance ceilings... - Some commands sound very similar (e.g., "three" vs "tree") — check your confusion matrix! - Data augmentation is encouraged: time shifting, noise injection, volume perturbation, SpecAugment. --- ## Evaluation ### Metric Submissions are evaluated on **Accuracy**: $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of correct predictions}}{\text{Total predictions}}$$ **Higher Accuracy is better.** An accuracy of 0.90 means 90% of your predictions match the true command. ### Scoring Your competition points (0–100) are calculated as: $$\text{points} = \max(0, \min(100, (\text{Accuracy} - 0.40) \times 166.67))$$ | Accuracy | Points | |----------|--------| | <= 0.40 | 0 | | 0.50 | ~17 | | 0.60 | ~33 | | 0.70 | 50 | | 0.80 | ~67 | | 0.90 | ~83 | | >= 1.00 | 100 | ### Submission Format For each sample in the test set, you must predict the `Command` (one of the 35 command strings). Your submission file should have exactly 2 columns: ``` Id,Command 0,yes 1,stop 2,three ... ``` The file must contain a header and have the same number of rows as the combined public and private test sets. --- ## rules ## ENTRY IN THIS COMPETITION CONSTITUTES YOUR ACCEPTANCE OF THESE OFFICIAL COMPETITION RULES. **[See Section 3.18 for defined terms](rules#18.-terms)** *The Competition named below is a skills-based competition to promote and further the field of data science. You must register via the Competition Website to enter. To enter the Competition, you must agree to these Official Competition Rules, which incorporate by reference the provisions and content of the Competition Website and any Specific Competition Rules herein (collectively, the "Rules"). Please read these Rules carefully before entry to ensure you understand and agree. You further agree that Submission in the Competition constitutes agreement to these Rules. You may not submit to the Competition and are not eligible to receive the prizes associated with this Competition unless you agree to these Rules. These Rules form a binding legal agreement between you and the Competition Sponsor with respect to the Competition. Your competition Submissions must conform to the requirements stated on the Competition Website. Your Submissions will be scored based on the evaluation metric described on the Competition Website. Subject to compliance with the Competition Rules, Prizes, if any, will be awarded to Participants with the best scores, based on the merits of the data science models submitted. See below for the complete Competition Rules.* **You cannot sign up to Kaggle from multiple accounts and therefore you cannot enter or submit from multiple accounts.**

1. COMPETITION-SPECIFIC TERMS

1. COMPETITION TITLE

The Last Frequency — Speech Command Recognition

2. COMPETITION SPONSOR

Cyprus AI Training

3. COMPETITION SPONSOR ADDRESS

Limassol, Cyprus

4. COMPETITION WEBSITE

https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/speech-command-recognition

5. TOTAL PRIZES AVAILABLE

This is a learning competition. No monetary prizes are awarded. Top-3 participants will receive recognition on the course leaderboard.

6. WINNER LICENSE TYPE

Open Source (Apache 2.0 or MIT)

7. DATA ACCESS AND USE

Competition Use and Non-Commercial & Academic Research ### 2. COMPETITION-SPECIFIC RULES In addition to the provisions of the General Competition Rules below, you understand and agree to these Competition-Specific Rules required by the Competition Sponsor: #### 1. TEAM LIMITS a. The maximum Team size is three (3). b. Team mergers are allowed and can be performed by the Team leader. In order to merge, the combined Team must have a total Submission count less than or equal to the maximum allowed as of the Team Merger Deadline. The maximum allowed is the number of Submissions per day multiplied by the number of days the competition has been running. #### 2. SUBMISSION LIMITS a. You may submit a maximum of five (5) Submissions per day. b. You may select up to two (2) Final Submissions for judging. #### 3. COMPETITION TIMELINE a. Competition Timeline dates (including Entry Deadline, Final Submission Deadline, Start Date, and Team Merger Deadline, as applicable) are reflected on the competition's Overview > Timeline page. #### 4. COMPETITION DATA a. **Data Access and Use.** You may access and use the Competition Data for non-commercial purposes only, including for participating in the Competition and on Kaggle.com forums, and for academic research and education. The Competition Sponsor reserves the right to disqualify any Participant who uses the Competition Data other than as permitted by the Competition Website and these Rules. b. **Data Security.** You agree to use reasonable and suitable measures to prevent persons who have not formally agreed to these Rules from gaining access to the Competition Data. You agree not to transmit, duplicate, publish, redistribute or otherwise provide or make available the Competition Data to any party not participating in the Competition. You agree to notify Kaggle immediately upon learning of any possible unauthorized transmission of or unauthorized access to the Competition Data and agree to work with Kaggle to rectify any unauthorized transmission or access. #### 5. WINNER LICENSE a. Under Section 2.8 (Winners Obligations) of the General Rules below, you hereby grant and will grant the Competition Sponsor the following license(s) with respect to your Submission if you are a Competition winner: Open Source: You hereby license and will license your winning Submission and the source code used to generate the Submission under an Open Source Initiative-approved license (see [www.opensource.org](http://www.opensource.org)) that in no event limits commercial use of such code or model containing or depending on such code. For generally commercially available software that you used to generate your Submission that is not owned by you, but that can be procured by the Competition Sponsor without undue expense, you do not need to grant the license in the preceding Section for that software. In the event that input data or pretrained models with an incompatible license are used to generate your winning solution, you do not need to grant an open source license in the preceding Section for that data and/or model(s). b. You may be required by the Sponsor to provide a detailed description of how the winning Submission was generated, to the Competition Sponsor's specifications, as outlined in Section 2.8, Winner's Obligations. This may include a detailed description of methodology, where one must be able to reproduce the approach by reading the description, and includes a detailed explanation of the architecture, preprocessing, loss function, training details, hyper-parameters, etc. The description should also include a link to a code repository with complete and detailed instructions so that the results obtained can be reproduced. #### 6. EXTERNAL DATA AND TOOLS a. You may use data other than the Competition Data ("External Data") to develop and test your Submissions. However, you will ensure the External Data is either publicly available and equally accessible to use by all Participants of the Competition for purposes of the competition at no cost to the other Participants, or satisfies the Reasonableness criteria as outlined in Section 2.6.b below. The ability to use External Data under this Section does not limit your other obligations under these Competition Rules, including but not limited to Section 2.8 (Winners Obligations). b. The use of external data and models is acceptable unless specifically prohibited by the Host. Because of the potential costs or restrictions (e.g., "geo restrictions") associated with obtaining rights to use external data or certain software and associated tools, their use must be "reasonably accessible to all" and of "minimal cost". Also, regardless of the cost challenges as they might affect all Participants during the course of the competition, the costs of potentially procuring a license for software used to generate a Submission, must also be considered. The Host will employ an assessment of whether or not the following criteria can exclude the use of the particular LLM, data set(s), or tool(s): 1. Are Participants being excluded from a competition because of the "excessive" costs for access to certain LLMs, external data, or tools that might be used by other Participants. The Host will assess the excessive cost concern by applying a "Reasonableness" standard (the "Reasonableness Standard"). The Reasonableness Standard will be determined and applied by the Host in light of things like cost thresholds and accessibility. 2. By way of example only, a small subscription charge to use additional elements of a large language model such as Gemini Advanced are acceptable if meeting the Reasonableness Standard of Sec. 8.2. Purchasing a license to use a proprietary dataset that exceeds the cost of a prize in the competition would not be considered reasonable. c. **Automated Machine Learning Tools ("AMLT")** Individual Participants and Teams may use automated machine learning tool(s) ("AMLT") (e.g., Google AutoML, H2O Driverless AI, etc.) to create a Submission, provided that the Participant or Team ensures that they have an appropriate license to the AMLT such that they are able to comply with the Competition Rules. #### 7. ELIGIBILITY a. Unless otherwise stated in the Competition-Specific Rules above or prohibited by internal policies of the Competition Entities, employees, interns, contractors, officers and directors of Competition Entities may enter and participate in the Competition, but are not eligible to win any Prizes. "Competition Entities" means the Competition Sponsor, Kaggle Inc., and their respective parent companies, subsidiaries and affiliates. If you are such a Participant from a Competition Entity, you are subject to all applicable internal policies of your employer with respect to your participation. #### 8. WINNER'S OBLIGATIONS a. As a condition to being awarded a Prize, a Prize winner must fulfill the following obligations: 1. Deliver to the Competition Sponsor the final model's software code as used to generate the winning Submission and associated documentation. The delivered software code must be capable of generating the winning Submission, and contain a description of resources required to build and/or run the executable code successfully. For avoidance of doubt, delivered software code should include training code, inference code, and a description of the required computational environment. a. However, for clarity, the potential winner's Submission must still meet the requirements of these Rules, including but not limited to Section 2.5 (Winners License), Section 2.8 (Winners Obligations), and Section 3.14 (Warranty, Indemnity, and Release). b. Train: 840 эссе с текстом и sentiment_score, test: эссе без меток. Итоговый скор — среднее по подзадачам (MAE/RMSE/F1); до 5 сабмитов в день, индивидуально, внешние данные допускаются.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Universities around the world receive thousands of admission essays each year.
Some are beautifully written and easy to follow, some are emotional and convey a strong sentiment, and some are even generated by artificial intelligence tools rather than applicants themselves.

In this task, your challenge is to simulate the work of an admissions committee — automatically analysing essays and predicting three different aspects of them:

- **Subtask 1:** Estimate the readability of the essay through a Robust Readability Metric (RRM).
- **Subtask 2:** Predict the sentiment score of the essay.
- **Subtask 3:** Identify whether the essay was written by a human or generated by AI.

---

## Description

Universities around the world receive thousands of admission essays each year. Some are beautifully written and easy to follow, some are emotional and convey a strong sentiment, and some are even generated by artificial intelligence tools rather than applicants themselves.

In this task, your challenge is to simulate the work of an admissions committee—automatically analysing essays and predicting three distinct aspects of them.

## Subtasks

**Subtask 1 — Robust Readability Metric (RRM)**

Essays should be evaluated on their accessibility to a broad audience. The RRM is a variant of the Flesch Reading Ease score, computed as:

$$\text{RRM} = \text{clip}\left(206.835 - 1.015 \cdot \frac{W}{S} - 84.6 \cdot \frac{SYL}{W},\ 0,\ 100\right)$$

Where:

- \( W \) = number of words (minimum 1)
- \( S \) = number of sentences (minimum 1)
- \( SYL \) = total syllables across all words
- \( \text{clip}(x, 0, 100) \) truncates values into the range [0, 100]

Empty texts default to a neutral score of 50.0. Predictions correspond to `subtaskID = 1`.

**Subtask 2 — Sentiment Regression**

Admission officers often look for emotional resonance and positive framing. Predict a continuous sentiment score. Labels are provided in `train.csv`. Predictions correspond to `subtaskID = 2`.

**Subtask 3 — Human vs AI Detection**

Committees are increasingly worried about authenticity. Predict whether the essay was written by a human (0) or by AI (1). **No labels are provided** — this is an unsupervised task. Predictions correspond to `subtaskID = 3`.

## Dataset

**Training file — `train.csv`:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | int | Unique identifier for the essay |
| `text\_content` | str | The essay text |
| `sentiment\_score` | float | Real-valued sentiment score |

**Test file — `test.csv`:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | int | Unique identifier for the essay |
| `text\_content` | str | Essay text to be evaluated |

## Scoring Breakdown

| Component | Points |
|---|---|
| Report / notebook | 5 |
| Baseline submission | 2 |
| Leaderboard score | 3 |
| **Total** | **10** |

## Restrictions

- You may use any Python libraries.
- You must not look up or reverse-engineer the ground truth.
- Ensure your code produces the exact predictions you upload.

## Tips

1. Subtask 1 is deterministic — get the formula right, and it's free points.
2. A good syllable counter (e.g. CMU Pronouncing Dictionary via `nltk`) helps a lot for RRM.
3. TF-IDF + ensemble regressors is a strong baseline for sentiment.
4. For AI detection, look at sentence length variance, vocabulary richness, and pronoun usage patterns.
5. AI-generated text tends to be more uniform and formulaic than human writing.

---

## data-description

All data can be downloaded from the [Kaggle Competition](https://www.kaggle.com/t/PLACEHOLDER).

You will use:
- `train.csv` — training data with sentiment labels
- `test.csv` — test essays (no labels)
- `sample_submission.csv` — example of the expected submission format

**Training file — `train.csv`:**

| Column           | Type  | Description                    |
|------------------|-------|--------------------------------|
| id               | int   | Unique identifier for the essay|
| text_content     | str   | The essay text                 |
| sentiment_score  | float | Real-valued sentiment score    |


**Test file — `test.csv`:**

| Column       | Type | Description                      |
|--------------|------|----------------------------------|
| id           | int  | Unique identifier for the essay  |
| text_content | str  | Essay text to be evaluated       |

---

## Evaluation

Submissions are evaluated across three subtasks. Each subtask is scored independently and contributes equally (1/3) to the final score:

| Subtask | Metric | Score Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (RRM) | MAE | \( \max(0,\ 1 - \text{MAE} / 50) \) |
| 2 (Sentiment) | RMSE | \( \max(0,\ 1 - \text{RMSE}) \) |
| 3 (AI Detection) | F1 | F1-Score |

The final score is the average of the three subtask scores:

$ \text{final\_score} = \frac{\text{subtask1\_score} + \text{subtask2\_score} + \text{subtask3\_score}}{3} $

Your score on this problem will be calculated as:

$ \text{points} = \text{final\_score} \times 10 $


## Submission File

For each `id` in the test set, you must predict answers for all three subtasks. The file should contain a header and have the following format:

```
subtaskID,datapointID,answer
1,17,61.4
2,17,0.322
3,17,1
1,18,48.9
2,18,-0.115
3,18,0
```

Where:

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `subtaskID` | int | Subtask number (1, 2, or 3) |
| `datapointID` | int | Essay `id` from `test.csv` |
| `answer` | mixed | Prediction (float for subtasks 1 & 2, int 0/1 for subtask 3) |

Each essay in the test set must appear exactly three times in the submission — once per subtask. See `sample\_submission.csv` for the complete format.

---

## rules

## Section 1: Competition-Specific Terms

Fill these into the Kaggle rules form:

#### 1. COMPETITION TITLE
[Cyprus Spring 2026] Are You A Robot?

#### 2. COMPETITION SPONSOR
Cyprus AI Training

#### 3. COMPETITION SPONSOR ADDRESS
Nicosia, Cyprus

#### 4. COMPETITION WEBSITE
https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/cyprus-spring-2026-are-you-a-robot

#### 5. TOTAL PRIZES AVAILABLE
No monetary prizes. This is an educational InClass competition. Points contribute to the course grade (up to 10 points).

#### 6. WINNER LICENSE TYPE
Non-Exclusive

#### 7. DATA ACCESS AND USE
Competition Use and Non-Commercial & Academic Research

---

## Section 2: Competition-Specific Rules

Use default values for everything except:

#### 1. TEAM LIMITS
a. The maximum Team size is **one (1)** (individual submissions only).
b. Team mergers are **not allowed**.

#### 2. SUBMISSION LIMITS
a. You may submit a maximum of **five (5)** Submissions per day.
b. You may select up to **two (2)** Final Submissions for judging.

#### 4. COMPETITION DATA
Choose option **2** (Competition Use and Non-Commercial & Academic Research):

> You may access and use the Competition Data for non-commercial purposes only, including for participating in the Competition and on Kaggle.com forums, and for academic research and education. The Competition Sponsor reserves the right to disqualify any Participant who uses the Competition Data other than as permitted by the Competition Website and these Rules.

#### 5. WINNER LICENSE
Choose option **1** (Non-Exclusive).

#### 6. EXTERNAL DATA AND TOOLS
Keep defaults (external data and AMLTs allowed).

tasks.full_description:
## Description

본 경진대회는 Image Classification 모델을 학습하여 주어진 이미지가 실제 사람의 얼굴 이미지(live)인지 스푸핑 공격 얼굴 이미지(spoof)인지 판별하는 것이 목표입니다.

**유의사항: 테스트셋에는 일부 unseen domain 데이터가 포함되어 있습니다.**

평가지표(evaluation metric)는 accuracy 사용해주시면 됩니다.

오픈소스 코드를 사용하실 경우 라이선스를 확인하시길 바랍니다.
(commercial use 가능한 오픈소스 코드만 사용 가능)

**경진대회 규정(Rules) 준수하셔야 하며 미 준수시 탈락 처리됩니다.**

1일 제출 제한: 5회
(한국 시간 오전 9시 제출 횟수 초기화)

**경진대회 마감 시간은 6월 11일(수) 밤 12시 (한국시간 기준) 입니다.**
마감 전에 submission 2개 선택해서 제출해주세요.

---

## data-description

본 경진대회는 Image Classification 모델을 학습하여 주어진 이미지가 실제 사람의 얼굴 이미지(live)인지 스푸핑 공격 얼굴 이미지(spoof)인지 판별하는 것이 목표입니다.

**유의사항: 테스트셋에는 일부 unseen domain 데이터가 포함되어 있습니다.**

평가지표(evaluation metric)는 accuracy 사용해주시면 됩니다.

오픈소스 코드를 사용하실 경우 라이선스를 확인하시길 바랍니다.
(commercial use 가능한 오픈소스 코드만 사용 가능)

경진대회 데이터셋 외의 외부 데이터셋 사용은 금지합니다.

1일 제출 제한: 5회
(한국 시간 오전 9시 제출 횟수 초기화)

경진대회 마감 시간은 6월 11일(수) 밤 12시 (한국시간 기준) 입니다.

마감 전에 submission 2개 선택해서 제출해주세요.

---

## rules

**1. 외부 데이터 및 API / 사전 학습 모델 허용 범위**
- 외부 데이터 사용 불가능
- 유료 API 사용 불가능
- 오픈소스는 상업적 이용이 가능한 코드, 학습된 모델만 사용 가능(commercial use 가능한 오픈소스)
- 법적 제약이 없으며 논문으로 공개된 베이스의 사전 학습 모델(Pre-trained Model) 사용 가능

tasks.description: Табличная многоклассовая классификация вина: предсказать комбинированную метку kategori (качество good/average/bad × цвет red/white, 6 классов) по 11 химико-физическим признакам (keasaman_tetap/volatile, asam_sitrat, gula_residual, klorida, sulfur_dioksida bebas/total, massa_jenis, pH, sulfat, alkohol). Test: 1300 строк. Метрика — Accuracy; запрещено загружать данные в ChatGPT и искать решения в Google/Kaggle.
tasks.full_description:
## Description

Tugas ini merupakan masalah klasifikasi multikelas yang bertujuan untuk memprediksi kategori wine berdasarkan berbagai fitur kimia dan fisik yang terkandung dalam sampel wine. Kategori tersebut adalah gabungan dari dua aspek penting: tingkat kualitas (good, average, bad) dan jenis warna (red, white), yang mencerminkan seberapa baik kualitas wine serta jenisnya berdasarkan proses fermentasi dan bahan baku yang digunakan. Dengan demikian, label yang diprediksi akan berupa kombinasi seperti good red, average white, atau bad red.

Model klasifikasi akan dibangun menggunakan sebelas fitur numerik yang mengukur karakteristik penting dari setiap sampel wine, seperti kadar keasaman (tetap dan volatile), kandungan gula residual, konsentrasi alkohol, jumlah sulfur dioksida (bebas maupun total), tingkat pH, massa jenis (density), serta kandungan sulfat dan klorida. Fitur-fitur ini memberikan indikasi terhadap cita rasa, stabilitas, dan kualitas keseluruhan dari produk wine.

Tujuan utama dari tugas ini adalah untuk mengembangkan sistem yang mampu memprediksi kategori wine secara akurat berdasarkan nilai-nilai fitur tersebut.

---

## data-description

# Deskripsi Kolom
| Nama Fitur                  | Deskripsi                                                                                                     |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **keasaman\_tetap**         | Jumlah keasaman non-volatil (tetap) dalam wine.                                        |
| **keasaman\_volatile**      | Jumlah keasaman yang mudah menguap. |
| **asam\_sitrat**            | Kandungan asam sitrat, memberikan rasa segar dan asam seperti jeruk.                                          |
| **gula\_residual**          | Kandungan gula sisa yang tidak terfermentasi.                               |
| **klorida**                 | Kandungan garam (biasanya natrium klorida) dalam wine.                                                        |
| **sulfur\_dioksida\_bebas** | Jumlah sulfur dioksida bebas yang berperan sebagai pengawet dan mencegah oksidasi.                            |
| **total\_sulfur\_dioksida** | Total kandungan sulfur dioksida (bebas + terikat) dalam wine.                                                 |
| **massa\_jenis**            | Massa jenis (density) dari wine, biasanya berhubungan dengan kandungan gula dan alkohol.                      |
| **pH**                      | Tingkat keasaman atau kebasaan wine.                                    |
| **sulfat**                  | Kandungan sulfat, berperan dalam stabilitas wine dan memengaruhi rasa.                                        |
| **alkohol**                 | Persentase kandungan alkohol dalam wine.                                                                      |
| **kategori**                | Kategori kualitas wine, misalnya “average red”, “good white”, dll.                                            |

# Deskripsi Target Kategori
Kolom kategori dalam dataset ini merupakan label klasifikasi yang terdiri dari dua komponen utama:

## Tingkat Kualitas Wine
Menunjukkan seberapa baik kualitas wine berdasarkan kombinasi berbagai fitur kimia.
Nilainya dapat berupa:
- good → kualitas tinggi
- average → kualitas sedang
- bad → kualitas rendah

## Warna Wine
Menunjukkan jenis wine berdasarkan warna atau tipe fermentasi.
Nilainya dapat berupa:
- red → wine merah
- white → wine putih

---

## Evaluation

## Metrik Evaluasi
Performa model Anda akan diukur berdasarkan metrik [akurasi](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.accuracy_score.html).

## Submission File
For each ID in the test set, you must predict a class for the `kategori` variable. The file should contain a header and have the following format:

    ID,kategori
    2,good red
    5,average white
    6,average white
    etc.

---

## rules

Anda dilarang meng-upload data ke ChatGPT dan dilarang mencari solusi dari Google ataupun platform seperti Kaggle dan sebagainya. Good luck and have fun!

Метрики — Accuracy для первых двух, F1-Macro для valid; суммарно 100. Решать только в Google Colab, поиск в интернете запрещён, разрешён только ChatGPT для пояснений.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

##**BACA DESKRIPSI SOAL DENGAN BAIK-BAIK.**

---

## Description

Nesy adalah seorang pelukis handal yang dikenal akan karya-karya lukisnya yang unik dan konsisten. Ia selalu melukis di atas kanvas berukuran 9 kali 9. Meskipun terbatas dalam jumlah cat yang dimiliki, Nesy mampu menciptakan karya luar biasa. Karena keterbatasan tersebut, beberapa bagian lukisan dibiarkan berwarna putih (belum dicat), sementara sisanya diberi warna menggunakan skala abu-abu.

![](https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/kaggle-user-content/o/inbox%2F27319419%2F17e1385130f9b8d1cf7502c4d785c8ba%2Fgrayscale_gradient.png?generation=1750221778247110&alt=media)

Beberapa tahun kemudian, lukisan-lukisan Nesy dipajang di sebuah museum seni. Namun, saat Nesy datang mengunjungi pameran tersebut, ia menyadari sesuatu yang janggal: beberapa lukisan tampak seperti dipalsukan! Meskipun ukurannya sama dan warna-warna yang digunakan mirip, ada pola tertentu yang membuatnya curiga.

Sebagai sahabat setia Nesy dan juga seorang ahli teknologi, kamu memutuskan untuk membantu. Tugasmu adalah membuat model deteksi untuk membedakan antara lukisan asli karya Nesy atau lukisan palsu yang mungkin dibuat oleh pemalsu.

Berikut sebagai contoh pada lukisan tersebut.
![](https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/kaggle-user-content/o/inbox%2F27319419%2Fede813468d13d8da462c7a94800c2d2b%2Fboard_0_label_1_scale_14_frame_red.png?generation=1750221334725424&alt=media)

---

## data-description

This page appears alongside the data files. It describes what files have been provided and the format of each. There is no single format for this page that is appropriate for all competitions, but you should strive to describe as much as you can here. A little time spent describing the data here can save a lot of time answering questions later.

## Files

*   **train_images** - Semua training image
*   **test_images** - Semua test image
**Ukuran gambar pada train dan test memiliki pixel yang BERBEDA-BEDA**
*   **sample_submission.csv** - a sample submission file in the correct format

## Columns

*   `border_color` - Warna border pada lukisan tersebut 
*   `banyak` - Banyaknya bagian yang tidak diwarnai oleh Nesy
*   `valid` - Apakah lukisan tersebut palsu atau tidak

---

## Evaluation

Terdapat 3 subtask yang harus kamu selesaikan berdasarkan dataset lukisan:

## 🔹 Subtask 1 — Prediksi Warna Border (10 poin)
- Prediksi warna dari bingkai lukisan.
- **Seluruh nilai warna border_color pada test set dijamin muncul juga di train set. Artinya, tidak akan ada warna baru yang tidak dikenal.**
- Nilai akan diberikan berdasarkan akurasi prediksi kolom border_color.

## 🔹 Subtask 2 — Prediksi Jumlah Area yang Dikosongkan (15 poin)
- Prediksi berapa banyak bagian putih (area kosong) dalam lukisan.
- Nilai akan diberikan berdasarkan akurasi prediksi kolom banyak.

##🔹 Subtask 3 — Prediksi Keaslian Lukisan (75 poin)
- Prediksi apakah lukisan adalah karya asli Nesy (True) atau palsu (False).
- Nilai akan diberikan berdasarkan F1-Macro prediksi kolom valid.

**⚠️ Total nilai dari ketiga subtask adalah 100 poin, dengan bobot terbesar pada prediksi keaslian lukisan.**

## Submission File
Kamu diminta mengumpulkan file prediksi dalam format CSV dengan struktur sebagai berikut:

    id,border_color,banyak,valid
    2,purple,0,False
    5,purple,0,False
    6,purple,0,False
    etc.

---

## rules

- Tidak diperkenankan berkomunikasi dengan peserta lain selama mengerjakan.
- Pengerjaan HANYA diperbolehkan menggunakan Google Colab
- Tidak diizinkan melakukan pencarian informasi di internet (Google, YouTube, dsb).
- Anda HANYA diperbolehkan membuka dan menggunakan ChatGPT untuk bantuan teknis atau penjelasan konsep.

tasks.description: Cross-lingual information retrieval без учителя: для 50 запросов на неизвестном «марсианском» языке отранжировать 1459 английских кандидатов-переводов. Доп. ресурс — параллельный корпус из 500 пар (eng500/unk500). Метрика — средний DCG(d; b=10) по позиции правильного перевода в ранге. Для каждого запроса ровно один правильный документ.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Pilihlah Mana yang Merupakan Arti dari Diriku?
==============================================

Anda diberikan 50 buah dokumen tekstual (queries.txt) dari sebuah bahasa yang tidak diketahui (asumsi Bahasa Planet Mars). Anda diminta untuk mencari tahu makna dari setiap dokumen berbahasa Planet Mars tersebut.

Beruntungnya Anda diberikan 1459 dokumen dalam bahasa Inggris (eng_collection.txt) yang masing-masing menjadi kandidat dari hasil translasi setiap dokumen dari pool 50 dokumen Bahasa Mars yang sudah disampaikan sebelumnya. Jadi, dijamin bahwa hasil translasi dari sebuah dokumen di queries.txt ada pada eng_collection.txt.

Tugas Anda adalah membuat submission ranking dengan format:

```python
que_doc,rank
Q0-D0,4
Q0-D1,54
Q0-D2,17
...
Q0-D1458,78
Q1-D0,73
Q1-D1,88
Q1-D2,35
...
Q1-D1458,477
...
...
Q49-D0,1002
Q49-D1,873
Q49-D2,2
...
Q49-D1458,10
```

Harapannya, dokumen yang benar-benar merupakan hasil translasinya adalah posisi rank pertama. Ingat juga penomoran ID query dan dokumen menggunakan zero-based index. Nilai rank adalah bilangan bulat dari 1 - 1459.

Untuk masing-masing query (sebuah dokumen bahasa Mars), hanya ada 1 dokumen hasil translasi yang benar pada eng_collection.txt.


Metric:
-------
Kita akan menggunakan **DCG (Discounted Cumulative Gain)** untuk menilai performa dari model Anda. Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) untuk sebuah query dinilai dengan:

`DCG(d;b) = 1/math.log(d + (b - 1), b). `

Perhatikan bahwa **d** adalah posisi ranking yang pertama kali ditemukan dokumen hasil translasi yang benar; dan **b** adalah basis pada fungsi logaritmanya. Dalam konteks retrieval, semakin besar nilai b semakin "persistent" seseorang dalam melakukan scanning hasil ranking dari posisi pertama. Pada task kali ini, kita akan menggunakan b = 10. Contoh detail perhitungannya adalah sebagai berikut:

Q1 --> misal yang benar ada di rank 3 --> DCG(3; 10)
Q2 --> misal yang benar ada di rank 1 --> DCG(1; 10)
Q3 --> misal yang benar ada di rank 65 --> DCG(65; 10)
...
Q50 --> misal yang benar ada di rank 10 --> DCG(10; 10)

Score akhir adalah rata-rata dari 50 nilai DCG di atas.


Beruntung lagi:
----------------
Beruntungnya lagi Anda diberikan sebuah paralel corpora Bahasa Mars-Bahasa Inggris yang berukuran 500 baris. Memang tidak besar, karena Bahasa Mars masih masuk kategori Low-Resource Language. Silakan Anda lihat file eng500.txt dan unk500.txt. Somehow, Anda bisa memanfaatkan paralel corpora ini untuk melatih model ranker Anda.


Selamat bekerja! Semoga Enjoy!

---

## data-description

## Files

*   **eng_collection.txt** - berisi 1459 dokumen Bahasa Inggris; ID dokumen bersifat zero-based, dan dipisahkan dengan double tabulasi dengan dokumen tekstualnya.
*   **queries.txt** - berisi 50 dokumen tekstual dari Bahasa Planet Mars, yang perlu Anda cari translasi yang benar pada eng_collection.txt; ID query bersifat zero-based, dan dipisahkan dengan double tabulasi dengan dokumen tekstualnya.
*   **eng500.txt** - paralel corpora dengan unk500.txt
*   **unk500.txt** - paralel corpora dengan eng500.txt

---

## rules

Silakan Anda kerjakan dengan semangat!

Train.csv + test_ready.csv (~333 строки в test). Метрика — Accuracy; разрешены encoder-only и encoder-decoder Transformer-модели и Word2Vec, decoder-only модели строго запрещены, RNN/LSTM/GRU предпочтительны, data augmentation разрешена.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

The goal of this task is to develop models that can accurately solve math word problems formulated in natural language. Each problem involves simple arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division) with a maximum of **three operands**. Do note that there may be more than three numbers in the questions, but not all numbers are used in the operation.

**RULES**
- You are allowed to use pre-trained Transformer-based models (encoder-only and encoder-decoder models; **decoder-only models are strictly prohibited**). However, the usages of RNN, LSTM, and GRU are preferable.
- You are allowed to use pre-trained Word2Vec for word or sentence embeddings.
- Data augmentation is allowed. Please clarify if you have any questions regarding this matter.

---

## Description

See the overview.

---

## data-description

This dataset contains math word problems paired with their corresponding answers. Each instance consists of a natural language question involving simple arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division), and a ground truth numerical answer.

## Files

*   **train.csv** - the training set
*   **test_ready.csv** - the test set

---

## Evaluation

Evaluation will be conducted using accuracy, i.e., count(correct answers)/count(all answers).


## Submission File
For each ID in the test set, you must predict an answer for the `answer` variable. The file should contain a header and have the following format:

    ID,answer
    2,45
    5,89
    6,100
    etc.

---

## rules

Following the general rule of competition.

tasks.description: Регрессия временных рядов: по истории суточного речного стока длиной obs_len (до 60 дней, x_1..x_60 с NaN-паддингом) предсказать среднее значение стока за следующие 12 дней (одно число target). Test ~400 строк, ряды разнородны (тренд, сезонность, mean-reverting, локальные изменения), единая модель неоптимальна. Метрика — Mean Absolute Error (MAE).
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Link Notebook: https://colab.research.google.com/drive/18H8nrF5A4mef8pmYXSYy97b-vQrJudXw?usp=sharing

---

## data-description

```python
Link drive alternatif: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Ig-_WksE7_oRxI4laH2Yl_L7Smj1lPeR/view?usp=sharing
DATA_BUNDLE_FILE_ID = "1Ig-_WksE7_oRxI4laH2Yl_L7Smj1lPeR"
```
### Format `train.csv`
Kolom:
- `id`
- `obs_len`
- `x_1`, `x_2`, ..., `x_60`
- `target`

Keterangan:
- `obs_len` = panjang observasi aktual untuk sampel tersebut
- `x_1` sampai `x_60` menyimpan observasi historis
- jika `obs_len < 60`, maka kolom setelah observasi terakhir diisi **NaN**
- `target` adalah rata-rata debit 12 hari berikutnya

### Format `test.csv`
Kolom:
- `id`
- `obs_len`
- `x_1`, `x_2`, ..., `x_60`

### Format `sample_submission.csv`
Kolom:
- `id`
- `target`

---

## Evaluation

Prediksi akan dievaluasi berdasarkan metric **MAE**

$$
\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |y_i - \hat{y}_i|
$$


## Submission File

```python
id,target
test_00000,0.0
test_00001,0.0
test_00002,0.0
```

---

File: `multimodal_train.csv`

Setiap baris merepresentasikan satu observasi:

- Kolom-kolom berikutnya berisi:
  - Nilai-nilai numerik dari **sinyal utama** sepanjang 4800 kolom
  - Nilai-nilai numerik dari **sinyal modulasi** sepanjang 128 kolom

### 🔹 Data Uji

File: `multimodal_test_data.csv`

Setiap baris berisi:

- Fitur numerik dari kedua sinyal dimana:
  - **sinyal utama** sepanjang 4800 kolom
  - **sinyal modulasi** sepanjang 128 kolom
- Label kategori **tidak disediakan**L

$$

tasks.description: Генеративный машинный перевод: по документу на синтетическом «марсианском» языке и 4 ключевым английским фразам сгенерировать английский перевод (гарантировано, что все 4 фразы войдут в правильный перевод). Ресурсы: parallel corpus 500 пар (eng500.txt / unk500.txt) и 50 тестовых документов (unk50.csv) + keywords.txt. Метрика — BLEU-3 против скрытого gold перевода.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

Translasi Bahasa Planet Mars dengan Kata Kunci
==============================================

Soal 2 ini merupakan kelanjutan dari Soal 1. Kali ini, Anda kurang beruntung. Anda tidak memiliki file yang berisi kandidat hasil translasi bahasa Inggris. Anda benar-benar harus membuat model yang menghasilkan (generate) hasil translasinya secara langsung, jika diberikan sebuah dokumen berbahasa Mars dan 4 buah frasa kunci dalam Bahasa Inggris.

Jadi, diberikan input 2 buah informasi:
1. Kalimat Bahasa Planet Mars
2. Empat (4) buah frasa kunci dalam Bahasa Inggris-nya; hasil translasi dalam Bahasa Inggris pasti mengandung 4 buah frasa kunci ini.

Ada 50 dokumen berbahasa Mars yang ada pada file unk50.txt; tugas Anda adalah membuat file submission sebagai berikut, dengan setiap baris adalah hasil translasi bahasa Inggris dari baris yang bersesuaian di unk50.txt. Simpan file submission Anda dalam format CSV.

```python
id,text
0,<hasil translasi dokumen ke-1 ke bahasa inggris>
1,<hasil translasi dokumen ke-2 ke bahasa inggris>
2,<hasil translasi dokumen ke-3 ke bahasa inggris>
3,<hasil translasi dokumen ke-4 ke bahasa inggris>
...
```

metric:
------
Kita akan menggunakan BLEU score (3-gram) untuk membandingkan hasil translasi Anda dengan sebuah hasil translasi gold standard yang disembunyikan.

Coba Anda pelajari bagaimana BLEU score itu dihitung via referensi berikut:
1. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BLEU
2. https://huggingface.co/spaces/evaluate-metric/bleu
3. tanya LLM favorit Anda


resource lain:
--------------
Wajib disyukuri juga Anda masih boleh menggunakan paralel corpora berisi 500 pasang dokumen Bahasa Mars-Bahasa Inggris yang sudah Anda gunakan di soal 1 :)

Kehadiran informasi 4 buah frasa kunci sebagai input juga harus Anda syukuri karena Anda bisa memanfaatkannya untuk lebih memberikan pengetahuan tentang hasil translasi ke Bahasa Inggris yang benar.

Namun perhatikan bahwa hasil translasi bahasa Inggris dari setiap dokumen di unk50.txt dipastikan tidak ditemukan pada koleksi eng_collection.txt yang diberikan pada soal1.


Selamat bekerja! Semoga Enjoy!

---

## data-description

## Files

*   **unk50.csv** - file yang berisi daftar dokumen dalam Bahasa Planet Mars yang perlu Anda translasi ke dalam Bahasa Inggris. File ini perlu dibaca menggunakan pd.read_csv() yang ada pada library pandas.
*   **keywords.txt** - file berisi 4 buah frasa kunci (dalam Bahasa Inggris) untuk masing-masing dokumen pada file unk50.csv yang bersesuaian. Perhatikan bahwa dokumen hasil translasi dalam Bahasa Inggris pasti mengandung 4 buah frasa kunci ini. Jadi, ini adalah informasi bagus yang perlu Anda manfaatkan untuk menambah kualitas hasil translasi.
*   **eng500.txt** - paralel corpora, bagian Bahasa Inggris
*   **unk500.txt** - paralel corpora, bagian Bahasa Planet Mars

---

## rules

Kerjakan dengan penuh semangat, dan dengan niat belajar yang baik.

tasks.description: CV cross-gender icon matching: по cropped-иконке туалета найти в галерее original-иконку противоположного пола из того же туалета. Train: 4 png-картинки (crop/orig × male/female) на каждый restroom-ID; test: query (cropped) и gallery (originals, |gallery|=2·|query|), id перемешаны. Метрика — Precision@1; сабмит — отсортированный по убыванию уверенности список N индексов на каждый query.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract # Community Mirror (Unofficial) This is an unofficial community-created mirror of a past IOAI task. It is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or officially connected to IOAI. # BASELINE - https://www.kaggle.com/code/hnbk54/restroom-baseline # READ SUBMISSION FORMAT # Restroom Icon Matching ## 1. Problem Description This task focuses on training a model to learn the relationship between **male and female restroom icons from the same restroom**. Based on labeled training data, you are required to train a matching model that, given a query image, finds its **cross-gender counterpart** (e.g., match a male icon in its cropped image to its female counterpart in its original image), under the constraint that both icons come from the **same restroom**. For example, for a query image (i.e., a male icon in cropped image) shown below in Fig1, its corresponding counterpart is shown in Fig2 (i.e., a female icon in original image which is from the same restroom). ## 2. Dataset **(1) Training Set:** Used for model training. Directory structure: ```bash training_set/training_set/ ├── crop/ # Cropped icons │ ├── female/ 1.png, 2.png, ... │ └── male/ └── orig/ # Original icons ├── female/ └── male/ ``` Each subfolder contains icons, where the number indicates the restroom ID. For each restroom, there are four images (note that four images from the same restroom will share one same unique id across all four subfolders): - `crop/female/i.png` → Cropped female icon - `crop/male/i.png`  → Cropped male icon - `orig/female/i.png` → Original female icon - `orig/male/i.png`  → Original male icon **(2) Test Set:** ```bash test_set/test_set/ ├── query/ # Cropped icons to be matched └── gallery/ # Candidate original icons ``` - `query/`: **cropped** icons that need to be matched. - `gallery/`: pool of **original** icons to match from. - Note that: - Unlike the training split, the filenames in `query/` and `gallery/` are independently numbered and shuffled, which means that matching cannot rely on the ids - For each cropped icon in `query/` there are exactly two originals in `gallery/` (one male, one female from the same restroom), which means $\text{len}(\text{gallery})=2 * \text{len}(\text{query})$ - All images are in `.png` format. ## 3. Task For each image in the `query/` folder, predict the image in `gallery/` that: - Is of the **opposite gender**, and - Comes from the **same restroom** This matching should be accomplished **using your trained model**. --- ## Description # 📂 Dataset Description The dataset consists of **training** and **test** samples. Each sample represents a single data instance to be processed by your model. --- ## data-description ## Dataset **(1) Training Set:** Used for model training. Directory structure: ```bash training_set/training_set/ ├── crop/ # Cropped icons │ ├── female/ 1.png, 2.png, ... │ └── male/ └── orig/ # Original icons ├── female/ └── male/ ``` Each subfolder contains icons, where the number indicates the restroom ID. For each restroom, there are four images (note that four images from the same restroom will share one same unique id across all four subfolders): - `crop/female/i.png` → Cropped female icon - `crop/male/i.png`  → Cropped male icon - `orig/female/i.png` → Original female icon - `orig/male/i.png`  → Original male icon **(2) Test Set:** ```bash test_set/test_set/ ├── query/ # Cropped icons to be matched └── gallery/ # Candidate original icons ``` - `query/`: **cropped** icons that need to be matched. - `gallery/`: pool of **original** icons to match from. - Note that: - Unlike the training split, the filenames in `query/` and `gallery/` are independently numbered and shuffled, which means that matching cannot rely on the ids - For each cropped icon in `query/` there are exactly two originals in `gallery/` (one male, one female from the same restroom), which means $\text{len}(\text{gallery})=2 * \text{len}(\text{query})$ - All images are in `.png` format. ## Submission Format Participants must submit their predictions as a **CSV file** with the following columns: - **id** — the unique identifier of the sample - **array** — a string list of predicted class indices, ordered from most to least confident ### Example id,array 0,"[0, 1, 2, ..., N-1]" --- ## Evaluation # 📊 Evaluation Metric Submissions are evaluated using the following metric: ## Primary Metric **Precision@1** ## Submission Format Participants must submit their predictions as a **CSV file** with the following columns: - **id** — the unique identifier of the sample - **array** — a list of predicted class indices, ordered from most to least confident ### Example id,array 0,"[0, 1, 2, ..., N-1]" ## Score - If the submission finishes within the time limit, the score is calculated as: $$ \text{Score} = \frac{\text{Number of correct matches}}{\text{Total queries}} $$ which will be a float number between 0.0 and 1.0 (inclusive). - Submissions that exceed the time limit will receive a score of **0**. ### Notes - The `array` column must contain **exactly N integers**, where **N is the total number of classes**. - Values must be **comma-separated** and enclosed in square brackets. - The order of values matters: predictions should be sorted by **descending confidence**. - The file must be saved with a `.csv` extension. - Submissions with an incorrect format will be rejected. --- ## rules ##ENTRY IN THIS COMPETITION CONSTITUTES YOUR ACCEPTANCE OF THESE OFFICIAL COMPETITION RULES. lol **[See Section 3.18 for defined terms](rules#18.-terms)** *The Competition named below is a skills-based competition to promote and further the field of data science.
