## abstract

Гарри Поттер и Сломанный Катбуст

---

## data-description

hello dear. Are you looking for a catboost? 🙂

---

## rules

These are general rules for NEOAI, which may be updated before the competition or have specific differences for certain NEOAI tasks. Note that if you compete in offline/online venue that is organized by someone in your country, they may override these rules - so ask for clarifications. The rules below apply for all russian kids participating at NEOAI on the main venue offline:

Note that organizers from your country may add additional limitations for the rules or make them similar to on-site rules, which makes sense if they would like to use the results of NEOAI to select participants who form teams for IOAI.

# Participation rules:
0. On the leaderboard of every competition, name yourself as "[Country] Name Surname" (e.g.: "[Russia] Alexander Guschin")
1. Reading/posting on "Discussions" tab is prohibited on the main venue - you should raise your hand and the organizer will come to you. On all other venues or online the question should be asked on "Discussions" tab of the relevant competition.
2. By default, the use of pre-trained models and additional data is not allowed, unless otherwise specified in the task statement.
3. Participants must solve these competitions without any external human help (e.g. asking teachers, friends or sharing data/ideas with other participants is prohibited).
4. You'll be given a chatGPT 4o or similar LLM to use. The use of any other LLM-based chat, copilot, or AI-code assistant tools
is prohibited. If you registered for NEOAI, you should've received URL and credentials (or will be given them at main offline venue).
5. Access to the internet will be restricted to the following list of websites (Contestants must not log in
to these websites, post questions, or use them to communicate with anyone outside the contest
hall):
◦ https://ext.contest.yandex.ru/reference
◦ kaggle.com - just pages of NEOAI competitions, nothing else
6. On a per-task basis, contestants may be asked to submit their code, trained models, and/or model
predictions. There will be no additional time for model training after the end of the contest.
7. Contestants cannot use cell phones or other devices during on-site competition.
8. Contestants are prohibited from engaging in any form of cheating, including tampering with the contest system, direct or indirect communication with other contestants or people outside the contest hall during the contest, bringing prohibited items into the contest hall, or attempting unauthorized access to test data.
9. The screen of each contestant’s computer may be recorded or remotely proctored.
10. Assistance staff will log any suspected cheating or rule violations observed during the contest, including discussions they had with or warnings given to contestants. This information will be reviewed by the Scientific Committee.
11. Violating any of these rules may result in disqualification or other penalties.
12. You cannot use multiple Kaggle accounts to participate in NEOAI
13. Every on-site participant will be given an access to a single Nvidia T4.
14. Usage of leaks (if accidentally left by task creators) is prohibited. You should report found leaks to organizers, so it counts towards your overall results (gives your extra points for your ML mastery).

# Scoring

To take into account difference in rules between online and on-site participation, two different final scoring tables will be assembled: one for online participants and the other one for on-site participants.

1. Each task may consist of multiple subtasks, with each subtask contributing a portion of the total score.
2. Maximum Score: The maximum score for each task is 100 points.
3. Final Score Calculation: The final score for a task is the sum of the scores of its subtasks, rounded to two decimal places.
3. Baseline Solution: A baseline solution, typically provided in the task statement, represents a simple or trivial solution that helps establish the minimum acceptable performance for the task.
4. Normalized Score Calculation: For each task, the score of a submission is normalized using the following formula:
    ```
    Norm_Score = (Submission_Score - Min_Score) / (Max_Score - Min_Score) * 100
    ```
5. Submission_Score = The raw score obtained for the submission (e.g., accuracy, AUC, F1-score, or any other metric specified in the task statement).
6. Min_Score = The score of the baseline solution.
7. Max_Score = The highest of two: unnormalized score achieved by any contestant’s submission during the contest OR the score of the authors solution (may be changed per-task).

# And as always - for everyone:
 - Don't cheat!
 - Apply yourself!
 - Have fun! :)

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

In this competition, you must predict the final rankings of participants in competitive programming competitions.

You are provided with historical data: participant results, problem data, and competition characteristics. Based on this, you must predict the placement (rank) of each participant in the test competitions.

For each contest in the test sample, a list (approximately 1000 participants) is provided. It is necessary to reconstruct the full order of participants in the final leaderboard.

The quality of the solution is determined by the accuracy of the predicted ranking compared to the actual results.

---

## Description

baseline UPDATED - https://www.kaggle.com/code/micaen/hw-baseline

####**Using pre-trained models and libraries outside of the standard Kaggle environment is prohibited.**

---

## data-description

## Description

The dataset contains historical results of competitive programming competitions from the platform. The task is to **predict the rank of a participant in an upcoming contest** based on their past performances.

For each test contest, a list of ~1000 participants is given. You need to predict their position on the final leaderboard.

## Files

* **train.parquet** — participants' results in historical contests (training set)
* **train_results.parquet** — results for each problem for each participant in each contest
* **problems.parquet** — problem metadata: maximum points, difficulty rating, thematic tags
* **contest.parquet** — contest metadata: division, scoring type, duration, start time
* **test.parquet** — test set. `(contestId, handle)` pairs for which the rank needs to be predicted
* **baseline_submission.csv** — Example submission with the required columns. To understand how it is built, check the baseline code

## Columns

### train.parquet

* `contestId` — contest identifier
* `division` — contest division: "Div1", "Div2", or "Div1+Div2"
* `handle` — anonymized participant identifier
* `rank` — participant's rank in the contest (1 = winner)
* `points` — total points for the contest
* `penalty` — total penalty time
* `successfulHackCount` — number of successful hacks during the contest
* `unsuccessfulHackCount` — number of unsuccessful hack attempts

### train_results.parquet

* `contestId` — contest identifier
* `handle` — participant identifier
* `problemIndex` — problem letter in the contest (e.g., "A", "B", "C1", "D")
* `points` — points for the problem (0 if not solved)
* `rejectedAttemptCount` — number of incorrect attempts before an accepted solution
* `bestSubmissionTimeSeconds` — time in seconds from the start of the contest to the best submission

### problems.parquet

* `contestId` — contest identifier
* `index` — problem letter (e.g., "A", "B", "C1")
* `points` — maximum points for the problem
* `rating` — difficulty rating of the problem (may be empty for new problems)
* `tags` — comma-separated thematic tags (e.g., "dp,greedy,math")

### contest.parquet

* `contestId` — contest identifier
* `division` — division: "Div1", "Div2", or "Div1+Div2"
* `type` — scoring system: "CF" (Codeforces) or "ICPC"
* `durationSeconds` — contest duration in seconds
* `startTime` — contest start date and time

### test.parquet

* `contestId` — test contest identifier (15 contests)
* `handle` — participant identifier (~1000 per contest)

### baseline_submission.csv

* `Id` — test contest and participant identifier (contestId + '_' + handle)
* `rank` — rank predictions (float or int values)

---

## Evaluation

The final score is the average across all test contests of a combined metric that evaluates two aspects of prediction quality:

$$Score = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big( 0.3 \cdot Recall@100_i + 0.7 \cdot \tau_i \Big)$$

where **N** is the number of test contests.

---

## Component 1: Recall@100

The proportion of correctly predicted participants from the actual top 100:

$$Recall@100 = \frac{| \hat{T}_{100} \cap T_{100} |}{|T_{100}|}$$

where:
- **T₁₀₀** — the set of participants who actually took places 1–100
- **T̂₁₀₀** — the set of participants that your model placed in places 1–100

**What it measures:** how well you identify the strongest participants. If you perfectly selected all top 100 (even in the wrong order) — Recall@100 = 1.0.

---

## Component 2: Kendall tau (τ)

Kendall rank correlation between the actual and predicted ranking across all 1000 participants:

$$\tau = \frac{C - D}{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}}$$

where:
- **C** — the number of **concordant** pairs (for participants *a* and *b*: both rankings agree on who is higher)
- **D** — the number of **discordant** pairs (the rankings contradict each other)
- **n** — the total number of participants

**What it measures:** how correct the overall order of participants is. If for every pair of participants you correctly determined who will perform better — τ = 1.0.

---

## rules

gl hf

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

In this competition, you need to predict the author of a text comment in a chat on a social network. You are provided with a large set of historical data: message texts and their corresponding authors. Based on this, you must determine who most likely wrote each comment in the test set.

For each text, you need to predict the 5 most probable authors. The final solution should return a list of 5 candidates.

**The data in the training and test datasets are presented in chronological order.**

**The test set contains the last 50 filtered (by length) messages for each user**

---

## data-description

## Description

The dataset represents a multiclass classification task of comments on text data. It is required to **predict the 5 most likely authors who wrote the comment**.

The training set contains comments and their corresponding authors. The test set provides only the texts, for which it is necessary to determine the most likely candidates who left the comments.

## Files

* **train_texts.csv** — training set with texts and target variable values  
* **test_texts.csv** — test set with texts without the target variable  
* **sample_submission.csv** — sample submission file in the required format  

## Columns

### train_texts.csv

* `index` — comment identifier
* `text` — comment
* `target` — commentator id

### test_texts.csv

* `index` — comment identifier
* `text` — comment

### sample_submission.csv

* `index` — comment identifier
* `target` — predicted commentators (5 items)

---

## Evaluation

## Recall@5

The final score is evaluated using the **Recall@5** metric, which measures whether the true author of a comment is among the top 5 candidates predicted by the model.

$$
Recall@5 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{1}(y_i \in \hat{Y}_i^{(5)})
$$

where:
- **N** — number of objects in the test set  
- **yᵢ** — true author of the comment  
- **Ŷᵢ⁽⁵⁾** — set of 5 authors predicted by the model for the i-th object  
- **𝟙(·)** — indicator function (1 if the condition holds, otherwise 0)

---

## rules

gl hf

---

## Baseline

https://www.kaggle.com/code/qacenn/baseline-findcomment

---

## General Rules

### Downloading NLTK corpora is allowed
### Use of external data is PROHIBITED
###  use of libraries outside the standard Kaggle environment is prohibited
###  Of the pre-trained weights, ONLY these two CAN BE USED:
- `deepvk/USER2-small` 
- `deepvk/USER2-base`


example from hugging face
```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel


def mean_pooling(model_output, attention_mask):
    token_embeddings = model_output[0]
    input_mask_expanded = (
        attention_mask.unsqueeze(-1).expand(token_embeddings.size()).float()
    )
    return torch.sum(token_embeddings * input_mask_expanded, 1) / torch.clamp(
        input_mask_expanded.sum(1), min=1e-9
    )


queries = ["search_query: Когда был спущен на воду первый миноносец «Спокойный»?"]
documents = ["search_document: Спокойный (эсминец)\nЗачислен в списки ВМФ СССР 19 августа 1952 года."]

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("deepvk/USER2-small")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("deepvk/USER2-small")

encoded_queries = tokenizer(queries, padding=True, truncation=True, return_tensors="pt")
encoded_documents = tokenizer(documents, padding=True, truncation=True, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    queries_outputs = model(**encoded_queries)
    documents_outputs = model(**encoded_documents)

query_embeddings = mean_pooling(queries_outputs, encoded_queries["attention_mask"])
query_embeddings = F.normalize(query_embeddings, p=2, dim=1)
doc_embeddings = mean_pooling(documents_outputs, encoded_documents["attention_mask"])
doc_embeddings = F.normalize(doc_embeddings, p=2, dim=1)

similarities = query_embeddings @ doc_embeddings.T
```

tasks.full_description:
## abstract You will need to train a machine learning model capable of classifying an animal's breed based solely on its text-based ASCII representation. Return the knowledge of breeds to humanity using only the power of the console and AI. --- ## Description The year is 2056. The era of graphical user interfaces is gone forever. Following the total domination of Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex, OpenCode, and other console-based AI agents, humanity has completely forgotten how to render pixels. Windows, buttons, and 4K monitors have been left to gather dust in museums - now, all of human digital life, from work to entertainment, flows exclusively through the terminal. But one thing remains unchanged: the fundamental human need to look at cats and dogs. To adapt old photo archives to this new reality, images of our furry companions were run through console converters and turned into ASCII art. Unfortunately, during the 'Great Console Transition,' almost all metadata was lost. We are left with thousands of text-based pet portraits, but we've forgotten which is a pug, which is a Maine Coon, and which is a Shiba Inu. **Example of an ASCII art image (CatBoost logo):** ![](https://www.googleapis.com/download/storage/v1/b/kaggle-user-content/o/inbox%2F10978443%2F05a837834de145d3f684e38bd9762716%2Fcatboost-ascii.png?generation=1774436867150385&alt=media)

> **Note**: This image is not from the dataset; it is simply an example of what an ASCII art image might look like. Your models will need to work with the actual data from the dataset. --- ## data-description Each object in the dataset is a former photograph converted into a **string** of **128 by 128 characters** (`\n` separates the lines). Characters of varying density convey the lighting, shadows, and contours of the animals. * **train:** A set of ASCII images with known breed labels for training your model. * **test:** A set of ASCII images for which you need to predict the animal's breed. **train.csv**: - `img`: a string containing the ASCII art of the image, 128 * 128 characters - `label`: the class of the image **test.csv**: - `id`: the image ID - `img`: a string containing the ASCII art of the image, 128 * 128 characters **sample_submission.csv**: - `id`: the image ID - `label`: the predicted class of the image --- ## Evaluation Participants' predictions will be evaluated using the **F1-micro** metric. ## Submission File id,label 2,0 5,0 6,0 etc. --- ## rules GLHF --- ## Task Limitations * **External Data:** The use of any external datasets is **strictly prohibited**. You must rely solely on the provided competition data. * **Architectures vs. Weights:** You are free to use **any** model architecture you want. However, there are strict rules regarding pre-trained weights: * **Allowed Pre-trained Models:** You may use pre-trained weights for **only** the following two models: * `google-bert/bert-base-uncased` Example of loading and using the model: ```python import torch from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("google-bert/bert-base-uncased") model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("google-bert/bert-base-uncased", num_labels=2) text = "Sample text for inference" inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt") with torch.no_grad(): outputs = model(**inputs) logits = outputs.logits print("Logits shape:", logits.shape) ``` * `gpt2` Example of loading and using the model: ```python import torch from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2") tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("gpt2", num_labels=2) model.config.pad_token_id = model.config.eos_token_id text = "Sample text for inference" inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", padding=True) with torch.no_grad(): outputs = model(**inputs) logits = outputs.logits print("Logits shape:", logits.shape) ``` * **Training from Scratch:** For **all other** algorithms, models, and architectures (e.g., `CatBoost`, `TF-IDF`, `IsotonicRegression`, `GigaChat3.1-702B-A36B`, custom neural networks, and **any predefined architectures**), **pre-trained weights and checkpoints are strictly forbidden**. These models must be initialized randomly and trained entirely from scratch using only the provided dataset. --- ## Data Each object in the dataset is a former photograph converted into a **string** of **128 by 128 characters** (with `\n` separating the rows). Characters of varying density convey the lighting, shadows, and contours of the animals. - **train**: A set of ASCII images with known breed labels for training your model. - **test**: A set of ASCII images for which you need to predict the animal's breed. It is highly recommended to check out the provided baseline notebook to get a better understanding of the data. --- ## Baseline https://www.kaggle.com/code/garifullintimur/tpets-baseline-homework

tasks.full_description:
## abstract This prestigious competition brings together school students from across Northern Eurasia who are passionate about machine learning, data analysis, and algorithmic thinking. NEOAI offers a unique opportunity not only to challenge your skills in AI but also to take your first steps toward participating in global competitions.
## Dates and Format: **Dates:** May 3–4, 2026 **Format:** - On-site at SberUniversity, Russia (On-site participation is only available for participants of the International AI Olympiad training program) - Online participation available for students from other countries, [check out the list of participants' countries](https://cu.ru/olympiad/events/neoai)
## Competition Structure: **Until May 3** – Trial round and platform introduction Participants will familiarize themselves with the competition platform and complete a practice task to get comfortable with the format and tools. They can find details about the practice task on the "Data" tab of this page. **May 3–4** – Main round: The main round will consist of 6 AI-related challenges designed by experts from Central University. These problems will cover a wide range of AI topics, including machine learning, computer vision, and data analysis, testing students’ practical and technical abilities. Tasks will be gradually released according to the following schedule: | Task | Available From | Available Until | Link | |--------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------------------| | Task 1 | May 3, 07:00 UTC | May 3, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-1-cv | | Task 2 | May 3, 07:00 UTC | May 3, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-1-nlp| | Task 3 | May 3, 07:00 UTC | May 3, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-1-kaggleforces | | Task 4 | May 4, 07:00 UTC | May 4, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-2-audio | | Task 5 | May 4, 07:00 UTC | May 4, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-2-cv| | Task 6 | May 4, 07:00 UTC | May 4, 13:00 UTC | https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/neoai-2026-day-2-cutiesnlp |
## Why Participate? The primary goal of NEOAI is to provide participants with an enriching experience in AI problem-solving and to prepare them for future international competitions, such as the International Olympiad in AI (IOAI). ✅ Gain hands-on experience in AI problem-solving ✅ Prepare for international competitions like the International Olympiad in AI (IOAI) ✅ Enhance your skills and test your knowledge with engaging and challenging tasks
**Join Northern Eurasia OAI** Take on AI challenges, improve your problem-solving skills, and prepare for future competitions. **Telegram channel for organizer contact:** [https://t.me/+40IpFt7yhd8wNDVi](https://t.me/+40IpFt7yhd8wNDVi) --- ## data-description This a test competition to help you familiarize yourself with Kaggle platform. All NEOAI competitions will be organized on Kaggle, so it's essential to know how to use the platform features. The data is quite simple: you have a set of images in `test` folder that you need to classify into 6 classes. In `train` folder there are certain examples of each class. Read "Rules" tab to learn more about NEOAI rules - they're going to be the same (minor changes are possible) for the rest 8 competitions you're going to participate in. You need to write your predictions into `sample_submission.csv` file and submit it on Kaggle. **Note that this is a competition where you have a small amount of samples and you can get the ideal accuracy of 1. Try to achieve that in the least amount of submissions possible! This is the real challenge here.** We suggest you to use Kaggle notebooks which you can create in the "Code" tab. You are allowed to use them during NEOAI as well, although note that those resources are limited. You can ask questions about the test competition and NEOAI in the "Discussion" tab or in the telegram channel https://t.me/+40IpFt7yhd8wNDVi --- ## rules These are general rules for NEOAI, which may be updated before the competition or have specific differences for certain NEOAI tasks. Note that if you compete in offline/online venue that is organized by someone in your country, they may override these rules - so ask for clarifications. The rules below apply for all russian kids participating at NEOAI on the main venue offline: Note that organizers from your country may add additional limitations for the rules or make them similar to on-site rules, which makes sense if they would like to use the results of NEOAI to select participants who form teams for IOAI. # Participation rules: 0. On the leaderboard of every competition, name yourself as "[Country] Name Surname" (e.g.: "[Russia] Alexander Guschin") 1. Reading/posting on "Discussions" tab is prohibited on the main venue - you should raise your hand and the organizer will come to you. On all other venues or online the question should be asked on "Discussions" tab of the relevant competition. 2. By default, the use of pre-trained models and additional data is not allowed, unless otherwise specified in the task statement. 3. Participants must solve these competitions without any external human help (e.g. asking teachers, friends or sharing data/ideas with other participants is prohibited). 4. You'll be given a chatGPT 4o or similar LLM to use. The use of any other LLM-based chat, copilot, or AI-code assistant tools is prohibited. If you registered for NEOAI, you should've received URL and credentials (or will be given them at main offline venue). 5. Access to the internet will be restricted to the following list of websites (Contestants must not log in to these websites, post questions, or use them to communicate with anyone outside the contest hall): ◦ https://ext.contest.yandex.ru/reference ◦ kaggle.com - just pages of NEOAI competitions, nothing else 6. On a per-task basis, contestants may be asked to submit their code, trained models, and/or model predictions. There will be no additional time for model training after the end of the contest. 7. Contestants cannot use cell phones or other devices during on-site competition. 8. Contestants are prohibited from engaging in any form of cheating, including tampering with the contest system, direct or indirect communication with other contestants or people outside the contest hall during the contest, bringing prohibited items into the contest hall, or attempting unauthorized access to test data. 9. The screen of each contestant’s computer may be recorded or remotely proctored. 10. Assistance staff will log any suspected cheating or rule violations observed during the contest, including discussions they had with or warnings given to contestants. This information will be reviewed by the Scientific Committee. 11. Violating any of these rules may result in disqualification or other penalties. 12. You cannot use multiple Kaggle accounts to participate in NEOAI 13. Every on-site participant will be given an access to a single Nvidia T4. 14. Usage of leaks (if accidentally left by task creators) is prohibited. You should report found leaks to organizers, so it counts towards your overall results (gives your extra points for your ML mastery). # Scoring To take into account difference in rules between online and on-site participation, two different final scoring tables will be assembled: one for online participants and the other one for on-site participants. 1. Each task may consist of multiple subtasks, with each subtask contributing a portion of the total score. 2. Maximum Score: The maximum score for each task is 100 points. 3. Final Score Calculation: The final score for a task is the sum of the scores of its subtasks, rounded to two decimal places. 3. Baseline Solution: A baseline solution, typically provided in the task statement, represents a simple or trivial solution that helps establish the minimum acceptable performance for the task. 4. Normalized Score Calculation: For each task, the score of a submission is normalized using the following formula: ``` Norm_Score = (Submission_Score - Min_Score) / (Max_Score - Min_Score) * 100 ``` 5. Submission_Score = The raw score obtained for the submission (e.g., accuracy, AUC, F1-score, or any other metric specified in the task statement). 6. Min_Score = The score of the baseline solution. 7. Max_Score = The highest of two: unnormalized score achieved by any contestant’s submission during the contest OR the score of the authors solution (may be changed per-task). # And as always - for everyone: - Don't cheat! - Apply yourself! - Have fun! :)

tasks.description: Две связанные подзадачи (по 50 баллов). (1) Идентификация: классификация на 5 классов «агентов» по 12 табличным признакам (числовые + категориальные), train=2000, test=1000. (2) Локализация: для каждой из 1000 surveillance-картинок (1024², 2048² или 4096²) предсказать центр шпиона; на каждого агента есть 5 референсных фото без отличительных аксессуаров. Разрешены претрейны, классический CV, zero-shot подходы. Локализация засчитывается если предсказание попадает в квадрат 200×200 вокруг истинного центра.

tasks.full_description: # HAIO 2024 🇭🇺 Magyar · 🇬🇧 English --- ## 🇭🇺 Magyar A Magyar MI Diákolimpia első kiírása. Egyetlen helyszíni forduló, az ELTE Informatikai Karán, 2024. május 30-án. A versenyről bővebben: [tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2024](https://tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2024) ### [Nyári Országos Döntő](nyari-orszagos/) - **Elméleti rész:** TensorFlow Neural Network Playground feladatok - **Gyakorlati rész:** 4 Computer Vision + 4 NLP feladat Google Colab-ban ### Feladatok | Feladat | Kategória | Megoldás | Adat | |---------|-----------|----------|------| | Elrejtett Kincs | CV | van | ❌ elveszett | | Intelligens János Gazda | CV | van | ❌ elveszett | | Transfer Tanulás | CV | van | ✅ torchvision | | Spectrogram | CV | van | ✅ [OSF](https://osf.io/drjhb/download) | | SMS | NLP | van | ⚠️ [UCI/Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/uciml/sms-spam-collection-dataset) (modell súlyok elvesztek) | | Twitter Hangulatelemzés | NLP | van | ⚠️ [Sentiment140/Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/kazanova/sentiment140) | | Gépi Fordítás | NLP | van | ❌ elveszett | | Tű a Szénakazalban | NLP | van | ✅ generált | | TF Playground 1 | Elméleti | - | - | | TF Playground 2 | Elméleti | - | - | > **Megjegyzés:** A 2024-es verseny adatainak egy része Google Drive-on volt tárolva, amely azóta törlésre került. Az egyedi versenyanyagok (Elrejtett Kincs, Intelligens János Gazda, Gépi Fordítás) nem visszaállíthatóak. Az SMS és Twitter adatok nyilvános adathalmazokból származnak, alternatív forrásokkal pótolhatóak, de az SMS feladathoz tartozó előre tanított modell súlyok elvesztek. ### Dokumentáció - [Versenyszabályzat](docs/versenyszabalyzat.pdf) --- ## 🇬🇧 English The first edition of the Hungarian AI Olympiad. A single on-site round held at the ELTE Faculty of Informatics on May 30, 2024. More details: [tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2024](https://tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2024) ### [Summer National Finals](nyari-orszagos/) - **Theory:** TensorFlow Neural Network Playground exercises - **Practice:** 4 Computer Vision + 4 NLP tasks on Google Colab ### Tasks | Task | Category | Solution | Data | |------|----------|----------|------| | Elrejtett Kincs | CV | yes | ❌ lost | | Intelligens János Gazda | CV | yes | ❌ lost | | Transfer Tanulás | CV | yes | ✅ torchvision | | Spectrogram | CV | yes | ✅ [OSF](https://osf.io/drjhb/download) | | SMS | NLP | yes | ⚠️ [UCI/Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/uciml/sms-spam-collection-dataset) (model weights lost) | | Twitter Hangulatelemzés | NLP | yes | ⚠️ [Sentiment140/Kaggle](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/kazanova/sentiment140) | | Gépi Fordítás | NLP | yes | ❌ lost | | Tű a Szénakazalban | NLP | yes | ✅ generated | | TF Playground 1 | Theory | - | - | | TF Playground 2 | Theory | - | - | > **Note:** Some 2024 competition data was hosted on Google Drive, which has since been deleted. Custom datasets (Elrejtett Kincs, Intelligens János Gazda, Gépi Fordítás) are unrecoverable. SMS and Twitter data originate from public datasets and can be obtained from alternative sources, but the pretrained model weights for the SMS task are lost. ### Documentation - [Competition rules](docs/versenyszabalyzat.pdf) (Hungarian)

tasks.full_description: # HAIO 2025 🇭🇺 Magyar · 🇬🇧 English --- ## 🇭🇺 Magyar A Magyar MI Diákolimpia második kiírása. Két forduló: online válogató (Kaggle) és országos döntő. A versenyről bővebben: [tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2025](https://tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2025) ### Időbeosztás | Esemény | Dátum | |---------|-------| | Nyári Online Válogató | 2025 tavasz | | Nyári Országos Döntő | 2025 nyár | ### Fordulók #### [Nyári Online](nyari-online/) Kaggle verseny: **Egyetemi Felvételi**, gépi tanulási modell, amely egyetemi felvételi eredményeket jósol. Értékelés: ROC-AUC. - [Kaggle verseny](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/magyar-mi-diakolimpia-online-valogato-2025) #### [Nyári Országos](nyari-orszagos/) Helyszíni verseny két részből: - **Elméleti rész:** Többválasztós és kifejtős kérdések (Deep Learning, RL, CV, NLP, ML, Optimalizáció) - **Gyakorlati rész:** 4 feladat: CV, ML, NLP, RL ### Feladatok | Feladat | Kategória | Forduló | Megoldás | |---------|-----------|---------|----------| | Egyetemi Felvételi | ML | Online | van | | Elméleti Forduló | Elmélet | Országos | van | | Petike és a Paraméteres Paletta | CV | Országos | van | | Klasszifikáló Klón | ML | Országos | van | | Epikus Emojik (Emoji Epics) | NLP | Országos | van | | Katakomba Kaland | RL | Országos | van | ### Dokumentáció - [Versenyszabályzat](docs/versenyszabalyzat.pdf) - [Adatkezelési tájékoztató](docs/adatkezelesi-tajekoztato.pdf) - [Hozzájárulási nyilatkozat](docs/hozzajarulasi-nyilatkozat.pdf) - One-pagerek: [CV](docs/one-pagerek/cv-one-pager.pdf) · [ML](docs/one-pagerek/ml-one-pager.pdf) · [NLP](docs/one-pagerek/nlp-one-pager.pdf) · [RL](docs/one-pagerek/rl-one-pager.pdf) ### Továbbjutottak és nemzetközi csapatok Az eredmények, továbbjutottak és nemzetközi csapattagok listája a [verseny weboldalán](https://tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2025) érhető el. --- ## 🇬🇧 English The second edition of the Hungarian AI Olympiad. Two rounds: online qualifier (Kaggle) and national finals. More details: [tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2025](https://tehetseg.inf.elte.hu/mi_olimpia/2025) ### Schedule | Event | Date | |-------|------| | Summer Online Qualifier | Spring 2025 | | Summer National Finals | Summer 2025 | ### Rounds #### [Summer Online](nyari-online/) Kaggle competition: **University Admissions**, an ML model predicting university admission outcomes. # IOAI TST Kazakhstan - Day 3: Code Difficulty Classification

This repository contains the solution for the third day's problem of the IOAI TST (Team Selection Test) in Kazakhstan. The task was to classify programming code snippets into one of three difficulty levels: "easy", "medium", or "hard".

**Kaggle Competition Link:** [https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/kz-tst-day-3](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/kz-tst-day-3)

## Table of Contents

  - [Problem Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23problem-description)
  - [Evaluation Metric](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23evaluation-metric)
  - [Solution Overview](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23solution-overview)
  - [Code Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23code-description)
  - [Dependencies](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23dependencies)
  - [Usage](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23usage)

## Problem Description

The challenge involved analyzing code snippets and assigning them a difficulty label (easy, medium, or hard). This is a multi-class text classification problem where the "text" is source code.

## Evaluation Metric

The primary evaluation metric for this competition was the **F1-score**.

Our solution achieved an **F1-score of 0.37750**.

## Solution Overview

The solution employs a combination of handcrafted features extracted from the code and TF-IDF representations of the code text, followed by a LightGBM classifier. Key steps include:

1.  **Data Preprocessing:** Cleaning and preparing the code snippets and mapping difficulty labels to numerical values.
2.  **Class Balancing (Oversampling):** Addressing potential class imbalance in the training data by oversampling minority classes.
3.  **Feature Engineering:**
      * **Statistical Features:** Extracting basic textual statistics (e.g., character length, word count, sentence count, number of digits).
      * **Code Structure Features:** Identifying programming constructs (e.g., number of loops, if statements, functions, comments).
      * **Algorithmic Keywords:** Detecting the presence of common algorithmic concepts (e.g., "tree", "dynamic programming", "hash", "stack", "recursive").
      * **TF-IDF Vectorization:** Converting code text into numerical TF-IDF vectors to capture important terms and their frequencies.
4.  **Feature Concatenation:** Combining the handcrafted features with the TF-IDF vectors.
5.  **Model Training:** Training a LightGBM Classifier on the combined feature set.
6.  **Evaluation:** Assessing model performance on both oversampled validation data and the original, unbalanced training data.
7.  **Prediction and Submission:** Generating predictions for the test set and formatting them for submission.

## Code Description

The provided Python script `batyr-yerdenov-3.ipynb` details the implementation:

  * **Import Libraries:** `pandas` for data handling, `numpy` for numerical operations, `re` for regular expressions, `scipy.sparse` for efficient matrix operations, `sklearn.feature_extraction.text` for TF-IDF, `sklearn.model_selection` for splitting data, `lightgbm` for the classifier, and `sklearn.metrics` for evaluation.

  * **`extract_code_features(df)` function:**

      * Calculates various lexical and structural features: `char_len`, `word_count`, `sentence_count`, `avg_token_len`, `num_digits`.
      * Counts occurrences of common programming constructs: `num_loops`, `num_if`, `num_functions`, `num_comments`.
      * Detects the presence of specific keywords or patterns indicative of algorithmic concepts: `has_O_complexity`, `contains_tree`, `contains_dp`, `contains_hash`, `contains_stack`, `contains_recursive`, `has_algo_steps`.

  * **Data Loading and Preprocessing:**

      * Loads `train.csv`, `test.csv`, and `sample_submission.csv`.
      * Fills missing 'code' values with empty strings, strips whitespace, and removes rows with empty code.
      * Maps `difficulty` labels ('easy', 'medium', 'hard') to numerical values (0, 1, 2).
      * Creat

# Image Restoration for IOAI TST in Kazakhstan

This repository contains the solution for the Image Restoration task presented at the IOAI TST in Kazakhstan. The goal is to restore original images from their filtered versions, where a specific 2x2 pixel-wise filter has been applied. The solution utilizes a custom U-Net architecture with residual blocks and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) modules, and incorporates information about the applied filter directly into the network.

## Table of Contents

  - [Problem Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23problem-description)
  - [Solution Overview](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23solution-overview)
  - [Dataset](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23dataset)
  - [Model Architecture](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23model-architecture)
  - [Loss Function](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23loss-function)
  - [Training](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23training)
  - [Inference](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23inference)
  - [Results](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23results)
  - [Dependencies](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23dependencies)
  - [Usage](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23usage)

## Problem Description

The challenge involves restoring images that have been corrupted by one of several predefined 2x2 pixel-wise filters. Each filter selectively retains a specific color channel (Red, Green, or Blue) for each of the four pixels within a 2x2 block. The task is to predict the original, unfiltered image.

## Solution Overview

The solution consists of the following key components:

1.  **Custom 2x2 Filter Application (`apply_fast_filter`):** A function to simulate the filtering process based on a given pattern.
2.  **Filter Detection (`detect_filter`):** A method to identify which of the 12 possible filters was applied to a given image. This is crucial for guiding the restoration process.
3.  **Custom Dataset (`FilteredRestoreDataset`):** A PyTorch `Dataset` that generates pairs of filtered and original images for training, along with a one-hot encoded representation of the applied filter.
4.  **Custom U-Net Model (`CustomUNet`):** A U-Net based convolutional neural network designed for image-to-image translation tasks. This model is augmented to take the filter information as an additional input, allowing it to adapt its restoration based on the specific degradation.
5.  **Combined Loss Function (`CombinedLossNoPretrained`):** A custom loss function that combines Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) to optimize for both pixel-wise accuracy and perceptual quality.
6.  **Training Loop:** Standard PyTorch training loop for optimizing the model.
7.  **Inference Pipeline:** A process for loading filtered test images, detecting the applied filter, and using the trained model to restore them.
8.  **Submission Generation:** Formatting the restored images into the required submission format.

## Dataset

The dataset consists of `real_images` (original images) and `filtered_images` (images processed by one of the 12 filters). The `FilteredRestoreDataset` dynamically applies each of the 12 filters to the original images to create a diverse training set.

**Dataset Structure:**

```
/kaggle/input/tst-day-1/
├── train/
│   └── train/
│       └── real_images/
│           ├── img_0001.png
│           ├── ...
│       └── filtered_images/ (Not directly used in training, filters are applied on the fly)
├── test/
│   └── test/
│       └── filtered_images/
│           ├── img_test_0001.png
│           ├── ...
└── sample_submission.csv
```

## Model Architecture

The core of the solution is a `CustomUNet` model.

  * **Encoder-Decoder Structure:** Follows the typical U-Net architecture with downsampling (encoder) and upsampling (decoder) paths, connected by skip connections.
  * **Residual Blocks:** Each convolutional block in the encoder and decoder is a `ResidualBlock` which helps in training deeper networks by alleviating the vanishing

# IOAI TST Kazakhstan - Day 4: Masked Word Position Prediction

This repository contains the solution for the fourth day's problem of the IOAI TST (Team Selection Test) in Kazakhstan. The task was to predict the original position of a masked word within a given sentence.

**Kaggle Competition Link:** [https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/kz-tst-day-4](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/kz-tst-day-4)

## Table of Contents

  - [Problem Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23problem-description)
  - [Evaluation Metric](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23evaluation-metric)
  - [Solution Overview](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23solution-overview)
  - [Code Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23code-description)
  - [Results](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23results)
  - [Dependencies](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23dependencies)
  - [Usage](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23usage)

## Problem Description

The challenge involved natural language processing: given a sentence with one word removed (masked), the goal was to identify the original numerical index (position) of the missing word within that sentence.

## Evaluation Metric

The primary evaluation metric for this competition was **Accuracy**.

Our solution achieved an **Accuracy of 0.53677**.

## Solution Overview

The approach leverages a pre-trained multilingual BERT model for sequence understanding and classification. Key steps include:

1.  **Text Cleaning & Filtering:** Robust cleaning of raw text data to remove invalid characters, normalize whitespace, and filter out low-quality or short sentences.
2.  **Training Data Generation:** For each clean sentence, multiple training examples are created by randomly masking one word and using its original index as the target label.
3.  **Tokenization:** Utilizing a `BertTokenizer` to convert text into token IDs suitable for BERT.
4.  **Model Architecture:** A custom PyTorch model built on top of `bert-base-multilingual-cased`, with a linear classification layer predicting the word's position.
5.  **Training:** Fine-tuning the BERT-based model using Cross-Entropy Loss.
6.  **Inference:** Predicting the word positions for the test set.
7.  **Heuristic Post-processing:** Applying a simple heuristic to refine predictions: if a masked sentence starts with a lowercase letter, the predicted position is set to 0 (implying the first word was masked and it should have been capitalized).
8.  **Submission Generation:** Formatting the predictions into the required CSV format.

## Code Description

The provided Python script `batyr-yerdenov-3.ipynb` (despite the name, it's for Day 4) details the implementation:

  * **`seed_everything(seed=42)`:** Function to ensure reproducibility across runs.
  * **Constants & Device:** `tqdm.pandas()` for progress bars, `DEVICE` set to 'cuda' if available.
  * **Text Cleaning Functions (`clean_text`, `is_valid_char`, `is_clean_sentence`):**
      * `BAD_CHARS`: A set of undesirable characters to remove.
      * `is_valid_char`: Checks if a character is valid (e.g., Cyrillic, space, punctuation).
      * `clean_text`: Removes `BAD_CHARS`, normalizes punctuation and whitespace.
      * `is_clean_sentence`: Filters sentences based on length, capitalization patterns, repeated characters, short initial words, and vowel count to ensure quality.
  * **Data Loading:** Reads `train.csv` and `public_test.csv`.
  * **Initial Sentence Preprocessing:** Applies `clean_text` and `is_clean_sentence` to extract a clean set of sentences from the raw training data.
  * **Training Example Generation (`generate_n_masks`):**
      * Takes a clean sentence and generates `n` (default 3) masked versions.
      * Each masked version has one word randomly removed, and the index of the removed word is stored as the label.
  * **Dataset Splitting:** Splits the clean sentences into `train_sentences` and `val_sentences`.
  * **Dataset Creation:** Populates `train_data` and `val_data` lists by applying `gen

# IOAI TST Kazakhstan - Day 2: Player Clustering Solution

This repository contains the solution for the second day's problem of the IOAI TST (Team Selection Test) in Kazakhstan. The objective was to cluster football (soccer) players based on their in-game attributes.

**Kaggle Competition Link:** [https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/tst-day-2](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/tst-day-2)

## Table of Contents

  - [Problem Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23problem-description)
  - [Evaluation Metric](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23evaluation-metric)
  - [Solution Overview](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23solution-overview)
  - [Code Description](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23code-description)
  - [Results](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23results)
  - [Dependencies](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23dependencies)
  - [Usage](https://www.google.com/search?q=%23usage)

## Problem Description

The task was to group football players into clusters based on their various in-game statistics (e.g., passing, shooting, defense). A key challenge was that players could have multiple positions (e.g., {CM, CAM}), and the clustering needed to reflect these overlapping positional roles.

## Evaluation Metric

The clustering performance was evaluated using the **B-Cubed F1 score for multi-positional clustering**. This metric assesses how well players with overlapping positions are grouped together.

Our solution achieved a score of **0.534** on this metric.

## Solution Overview

The approach taken involves several steps:

1.  **Data Loading:** Reading the player data and the sample submission file.
2.  **Feature Engineering (Meta-features):** Creating aggregated "meta-features" from raw player statistics to represent broader skill sets (e.g., `attacking_skill`, `passing_ability`).
3.  **Goalkeeper Identification:** Separately identifying goalkeepers, as their skill sets are distinctly different from outfield players.
4.  **Data Splitting:** Dividing the dataset into goalkeepers and outfield players.
5.  **Preprocessing:** Applying imputation for missing values and standardization to scale features.
6.  **Optimal Cluster Determination:** Using the Silhouette Score to find an optimal number of clusters for outfield players.
7.  **Clustering:** Applying Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for clustering both outfield players and goalkeepers separately.
8.  **Cluster ID Consolidation:** Assigning unique cluster IDs to both groups.
9.  **Submission Generation:** Merging the clustered data with the sample submission format.

## Code Description

The provided Python script `batyr-yerdenov-2.2.ipynb` (intended to be run in a Jupyter/Kaggle notebook environment) implements the following:

  * **Import Libraries:** Essential libraries like `pandas`, `numpy`, `sklearn.preprocessing`, `sklearn.impute`, `sklearn.mixture`, `sklearn.metrics`.

  * **Data Loading:**

    ```python
    df = pd.read_csv("/kaggle/input/tst-day-2/train.csv")
    sample = pd.read_csv("/kaggle/input/tst-day-2/sample_submission.csv")
    ```

  * **Meta-feature Engineering:** New features are created by averaging relevant raw attributes. Examples include `attacking_skill`, `passing_ability`, `dribble_mobility`, `pace`, `defense_skill`, `physicality`, `set_piece_specialist`, `goalkeeper_score`, `composure_score`, `offensive_support`, `attack_support`, and `defending_positioning`.

  * **Goalkeeper Identification (`is_gk`):** A boolean column `is_gk` is created. A player is classified as a goalkeeper if all their specific goalkeeper attributes (`gk_diving`, `gk_handling`, `gk_kicking`, `gk_positioning`, `gk_reflexes`) are above a threshold of 40.

  * **Data Splitting:** The DataFrame `df` is split into `gk_df` (goalkeepers) and `field_df` (outfield players).

  * **Feature Selection for Clustering:**

      * `features`: A list of meta-features used for clustering outfield players.
      * `gk_features`: A list containing `goalkeeper_score` for clustering g

tasks.description: Табличный кредитный скоринг с генерацией объяснений предсказаний: бинарная классификация дефолта плюс human-readable объяснение каждого решения. Train/test заданы в ноутбуке kredytobranie.ipynb (2-й этап польской OlimpiadaAI), несколько тысяч заявок с числовыми и категориальными фичами. Метрика — комбинация качества классификации и качества объяснений. Ограничения формата олимпиады: индивидуальная работа, без интернета вне разрешённой документации.
tasks.full_description:
# II Olimpiada Sztucznej Inteligencji / 2nd Polish AI Olympiad

Witamy w repozytorium z zadaniami II Olimpiady Sztucznej Inteligencji. Olimpiada ta jest skierowana do uczniów w Polsce, zarówno ze szkół średnich jak i podstawowych, którzy są zainteresowani sztuczną inteligencją. Celem jest zwiększenie zainteresowania AI i wyłonienie składów reprezentacji na olimpiady międzynarodowe [IOAI](https://ioai-official.org/) w Pekinie (2-9 sierpnia 2025) i [IAIO](https://www.iaio-official.org/) w Lublanie. ## Informacje ogólne **Strona główna:** [Olimpiada Sztucznej Inteligencji](https://oai.cs.uni.wroc.pl/edycje/ii-oai) Druga edycja Olimpiady odbędzie się w trzech etapach: - Pierwszy etap, online, 17.02 - 22.03.2025. - Drugi etap, regionalny w 4 miastach: Kraków, Poznań, Warszawa i Wrocław, 26 - 27.04.2025 - Trzeci etap, finał we Wrocławiu, 30.05 - 02.06.2025. Regulamin jest dostępny na naszej [stronie](https://oai.cs.uni.wroc.pl/regulamin). Oficjalnym kanałem komunikacji z uczestnikami będzie platforma Discord. Dostęp do niej otrzymują uczestnicy po dokonaniu rejestracji. ## Zadania W ramach pierwszego etapu Olimpiady uczestnicy zmierzyli się z następującymi wyzwaniami: 1. **Maszynka do Liczenia Monet** - Detekcja monet z wykorzystaniem modeli wizyjnych. 1. **Wykrywanie Halucynacji** - Wykrywanie halucynacji modeli językowych w odpowiedziach na pytania. 1. **Wykrywanie Zaburzeń Sygnału EKG** - Wykrywanie anomalii w danych EKG. 1. **Zaszumienie w Etykietach Danych** - Trenowanie modeli z zaszumionymi etykietami. 1. **Ukryte Podciągi** - Identyfikacja zależności w binarnych ciągach znaków. W drugim etapie uczestnicy rywalizowali rozwiązując nastepujące zadania: 1. **Rozkład Nienormalny** - Usuwanie i klasyfikacja różnych rodzajów szumu na obrazie. 1. **Kredytobranie** - Znajdowanie kierunków w przestrzeni danych najskuteczniej zmieniających decyzje klasyfikatora. 1. **Ekstrakcja Źródeł** - Dobór embeddingów użytecznych w wyszukiwaniu tekstów źródłowych. Na zawody finałowe, przygotowaliśmy następujące problemy: 1. **Inpainting** - Uzupełenianie brakujących fragmentów obrazu przy użyciu sieci INR. 1. **Oduczanie** - Usunięcie z modelu wybranych informacji. 1. **Stylizacja tłumaczeń** - Dotrenowanie modelu tłumaczenia maszynowego do specyficznego stylu. 1. **Protypy danych** - Wybór reprezentatywnych i różnorodnych przykładów ze zbioru danych. ## Sposób oddawania zadań W pierwszym etapie uczestnicy rozwiązywali zadania samodzielnie i przesyłali je do Komitetu Merytorycznego za pomocą Platformy Konkursowej. Dostęp do niej jest udostępniany uczestnikom po rejestracji. Każde zadanie wymagało przesłania pliku Jupyter Notebook z rozwiązaniem. Wszystkie prace zostały oceniane automatycznie na Platformie Konkursowej. ## Środowisko Lista dopuszczalnych pakietów znajduje się w pliku `environment.yml`, osobno dla każdego zadania. Rozwiązania były testowane przy użyciu Pythona 3.11. Na potrzeby pracy nad zadaniami, zalecamy wykorzystanie środowiska online [Google Colab](https://colab.google/). lub stworzenie środowiska lokalnego z wykorzystaniem [Anacondy](https://docs.anaconda.com/) (instrukcję instalacji środowiska z pliku YML znajdziesz [tutaj](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html#creating-an-environment-from-an-environment-yml-file)). > Uwaga: To samo rozwiązanie uruchomione w środowisku lokalnym lub Google Colab może zwracać inne wyniki niż na Platformie Konkursowej. Ostateczna ocena Twojego zadania zostanie wyliczona na Platformie Konkursowej. ## Kryteria oceny Oceny za zadania zostaną wyliczone na podstawie podanych w treściach zadań kryteriów. Za każde zadanie w pierwszym etapie uczestnicy mogą zdobyć maksymalnie 100 punktów. ## Licencje Repozytorium korzysta z następujących zasobów objętych licencjami: ### 1 etap - [**ChatGPT**](https://openai.com/policies/terms-of-use/) - obrazy w zadaniach 2, 3

tasks.description: Задача «Rozkład Nienormalny» 2-го этапа польской AI-олимпиады (II OAI, 2025): удаление шума с изображений + классификация типа шума (разные распределения). Точные параметры датасета и формулу метрики в raw README не раскрыли — там лишь общий обзор олимпиады; ожидаемые метрики — что-то вроде MSE/MAE на восстановлении + Accuracy/F1 на классификации шума. Среда: Python 3.11, пакеты из `environment.yml`, оценка автоматическая на платформе.

tasks.description: Сборник авторских решений задач IOAI-подобных олимпиад (ROAI, IAIO, NEOAI, MLCompete, Nitro AI Judge), включая две учебные задачи: классификация изображений византийской музыкальной нотации (CV) и детекция AI-сгенерированных текстов vs human-written (NLP) с BERT-подобными энкодерами. Конкретные размеры выборок зависят от исходных контестов. Метрики — accuracy/F1 в зависимости от задачи; решения нацелены на максимальный балл (100/100). Стандартные ограничения IOAI: 6 часов, без интернета и LLM, только документация.
tasks.full_description:
# AI Tasks Solutions

Here you can find my solutions for tasks from various IOAI-like contests (including many tasks from the [Nitro AI Judge](https://judge.nitro-ai.org/) platform). These come from the National AI Olympiads (regional and national rounds) as well as from various competitions and practice contests.

For context, [IOAI](https://ioai-official.org/) is the International Olympiad in AI - it's the largest AI competition for high school students. Following **its format**, many national selection olympiads have spawned, as well as practice contests. IOAI is similar to Kaggle contests, however notable differences are its 6 hours time limit per round and internet access + useful LLMs are forbidden (documentations are allowed). The 2026 IOAI syllabus: [link](https://ioai-official.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Syllabus.pdf).

Another notable, newer, international AI Olympiad is [CEOAI](https://ceoai-official.org/), which differs from IOAI by its strict point-based scoring system (0-100 instead of raw metrics) and its syllabus containing additional topics such as Reinforcement Learning and AI search. The 1st edition has not yet taken place, but there are practice contests following its format.

Useful files:
<!-- - WORK IN PROGRESS [reference tasks](./reference-tasks.md): want to learn competitive AI? I made a list of tasks to get you from zero to hero -->
- [reference solutions](./reference-solutions.md): a quick look-up of the core idea used to solve each task

Romanian websites for competitive AI (contests and practice tasks):
- Judge Nitro: https://judge.nitro-ai.org/competitions (IAIO and CEOAI national selection); [solutions](./contests/)
- MLCompete: https://platform.olimpiada-ai.ro/en/problems (IOAI national selection); [solutions](./contests/mlcompete/)

All published solutions score the maximum possible points (100 where the problem allows, otherwise as high as possible) and are written in a clean, explanatory manner.

This repository is a continuous work in progress — writing up the solutions and explanations takes time, and I publish them as soon as they’re done. If you have any suggestions about contests you want me to solve, please contact me on Discord (stefanasandei).

Repository structure:
- `contests`: smaller contests, mostly from Romania
- `national-contests`: national Olympiads that select the teams for IOAI
- `international-contests`: international Olympiads and other such contests, like NEOAI

## List of solved competitions

A complete catalog of solved tasks can also be found in [reference solutions](./reference-solutions.md).

| contest or website  | description                                                        | editions    |
| ------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------ | ----------- |
| IOAI                | International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence                  | 2024 - 2025 |
| IAIO                | International Artificial Intelligence Olympiad                     | 2024        |
| NEOAI               | international AI competition                                       | 2025        |
| APOAI               | international AI competition                                       | 2025        |
| Polish AI Olympiad  | Polish national AI olympiad                                        | 2025        |
| Romania AI Olympiad | regional, national and team selection                              | 2025-2026   |
| Georgia AI Olympiad | national team selection                                            | 2026        |
| Nitro Judge         | online [contest platform](https://judge.nitro-ai.org/competitions) | 2023-2026   |
| ML Compete          | online contest platform                                            | 2025-2026   |
| Smaller contests    | others                                                             | many        |

## Copyright

These solutions were written by [Asandei Stefan-Alexandru](https://asandei.com). The project is li

tasks.full_description:
In acest folder gasiti un ghidaj tehnic si cod refolosibil **pentru cei care doresc sa propuna probleme pentru Olimpiada de AI**.

# Problema
Documentul [Problema.md](Problema.md) contine un exemplu de problema. Structura generala a unei probleme este in [Sablon_problema.md](Sablon_problema.md), iar cateva sfaturi utile se gasesc in [Recomandari.md](Recomandari.md).

# Solutii
Folderul [Solutii](Solutii) contine 2 posibile solutii - pentru incepatori si avansati.
    
# Set de date
Pregatirea unui set de date este descrisa in folderul [Dataset si evaluator/Dataset](Dataset%20si%20evaluator/Dataset). Acolo gasiti un notebook cu pasii realizati pentru curatarea unui dataset public, transformarea lui si obtinerea a 3 seturi de date:
- `dataset_train`: folosit pentru antrenare
- `dataset_eval`: folosit pentru predictie de catre candidati (acestia nu cunosc ground-truth-ul valorilor de iesire)
- `dataset_eval_t`: la fel ca mai sus, ascuns candidatilor, contine si campurile pentru evaluare.
Nota: Fiecare candidat primeste primele doua seturi (vedeti folderul fiecaruia) si scoate ca output fisierele cerute in formatul cerut de enuntul problemei.

# Evaluator (pentru comisia de evaluare)
Notebook-ul de evaluare [Dataset si evaluator/Evaluator.ipynb](Dataset%20si%20evaluator/Evaluator.ipynb) contine cod reutilizabil care compara rezultatele candidatilor si genereaza ca iesire un tabel sortat dupa scoruri si detalierea rezultatelor.

tasks.description: Image retrieval по текстовым «карточкам каталога»: для каждого из 700 тестовых описаний (48-97 строк аннотаций по регионам изображения, возможны дубли) вернуть top-10 фотографий из галереи в 1500 кандидатов. Метрика — средний reciprocal-rank-squared (1/k²) по 700 карточкам. Жёсткое ограничение: разрешено использовать только openai/clip-vit-base-patch16 (image+text encoders), запрещены другие претрейны и внешние данные.
tasks.full_description:
A cataloguer never wrote normal captions. For every photo he filled an index card with dozens of short observations, one per small region of the image. No card says what the photo shows as a whole, and the archive holds many photos of similar scenes. For each card, find the photo it describes.

The only model you may use is **CLIP ViT-B/16** (`openai/clip-vit-base-patch16`), both its image and text encoders. No other pretrained weights and no external data. 

## Data

| File / folder | Contents |
| --- | --- |
| `train.csv` | 500 rows: `image_id, description` (cards with their correct photo) |
| `train_imgs/` | 500 training photos `<image_id>.jpg` (`trn_*`) |
| `test.csv` | 700 rows: `datapointID, description` (cards to solve) |
| `test_imgs/` | 1500 gallery photos `<image_id>.jpg` (`img_*`) |

Each `description` is one card. each description ranges from 48–97 annotation lines separated by newlines. Most lines look like `Tree: A full green tree behind a building at the top of the stairs.` (label before the colon, observation after). some are just a bare label like `stone in floor`. The same object can be annotated several times, so cards contain near-duplicate and sometimes exact duplicate lines. Every line is true of the photo. The 1500 gallery photos are the 700 answers plus 800 distractors, many showing similar scenes. Exactly one gallery photo is correct per card.

A baseline notebook (`baseline.ipynb`) that loads the data and writes a submission with a trivial solution is in the starter kit. also `train_imgs/` and `test_imgs/` are located in the starter kit zip.

## Task and submission

For each of the 700 test cards, return the 10 gallery ids that best match it, best first. Write `submission.csv` with columns `subtaskID, datapointID, answer`:

```csv
subtaskID,datapointID,answer
1,0,img_00417 img_00031 img_00582 img_00109 img_00204 img_00488 img_00006 img_00351 img_00077 img_00260
```

`subtaskID` is always 1; one row per card (700 rows); `answer` is 10 space-separated gallery ids. Only the first 10 distinct ids count.

## Scoring

Metric `M` is the **mean reciprocal-rank-squared**: if the correct photo is at rank `k` in a card's 10, that card scores `1/k²`. `M` is the mean over the 700 cards, so rank 1 is worth far more than any lower slot.

| Rank of correct photo | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 10 | not in top 10 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Card credit `1/k²` | 1.00 | 0.25 | 0.11 | 0.06 | 0.04 | 0.01 | 0 |

Score is linear from the baseline to the reference, clamped to 0–100:

$$\text{points} = 100 \times \frac{M - 0.23}{0.94 - 0.23}$$

- `M ≤ 0.23` gives 0 points (provided baseline)
- `M ≥ 0.94` gives 100 points (reference)

Train 200 / test 50, причём test — OOD (10–25 костей и суммы до 96 против 1–10 костей и сумм до 37 в train). tasks.full_description:
The board-game club at the Cup & Cards café plays every Tuesday until closing. Whoever locks up takes one photo of the dice still lying on the table and writes two numbers in the club ledger, how many dice are out and the total of all the pips facing up. For the 50 newest photos the ledger page is blank, the closer went home early. You have the photos and the old ledger. Your job is to do what the closer did, look at each new photo, count the dice and add up the pips.

## Dataset

You get 250 photos of six-sided dice on a table. Every photo is 2736 × 2736 pixels, RGB, JPG. The dice vary a lot, translucent and opaque, glossy and matte, large and small, many colors. Some photos show a few well-separated dice, others a crowded pile where dice touch and tilt.

| Split | Photos | Dice per photo | Pip total per photo | Labels             |
| ----- | ------ | -------------- | ------------------- | ------------------ |
| Train | 200    | 1 to 10        | 1 to 37             | `train_labels.csv` |
| Test  | 50     | 10 to 25       | 22 to 96            | hidden             |

Every test photo holds at least 10 dice and only one training photo is that crowded. Test pip totals reach 96 while training totals stop at 37. The test set is out of distribution by design, every photo sits past the training range.

`train_labels.csv` has one row per training photo with three columns. `image_id` is the filename without `.jpg`, `n_dice` is how many dice the photo shows, and `total_sum` is all the up-facing pips added together. 

```python
import pandas as pd
from PIL import Image

train = pd.read_csv("train_labels.csv")
row = train.iloc[0]
img = Image.open(f"train/{row.image_id}.jpg")
print(row.n_dice, row.total_sum)
```

**Note:** the starter kit zip holds the training photos, the test photos and `baseline.ipynb`. The two CSV files live in the matching sections: `train_labels.csv` under **training data** and `test.csv` under **test data**. Download them and place them next to the notebook. The baseline loads the data, trains a small CNN and writes a valid `submission.csv`.

### Rules

- Pretrained weights are not allowed.
- External datasets are forbidden. Do not add other dice photos.

## Subtasks

**Subtask 1, dice count (30 points).** For every test photo, predict how many dice it shows. The answer is one whole number. We average how far your counts sit from the truth over the 50 test photos, and the closer you are, the more points you earn.

**Subtask 2, pip sum (70 points).** For every test photo, predict the total of all up-facing pips, again one whole number, scored the same way.

## Output format

`submission.csv` carries both subtasks in one file and has three columns.

| Column        | Meaning                                                        |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `subtaskID`   | `1` for the count, `2` for the sum                             |
| `datapointID` | `count_{image_id}` for the count, `sum_{image_id}` for the sum |
| `answer`      | your predicted whole number                                    |

Here is the start of a valid file.

```csv
subtaskID,datapointID,answer
1,count_IMG_20191208_111228,12
1,count_IMG_20191208_111246,15
2,sum_IMG_20191208_111228,41
2,sum_IMG_20191208_111246,52
```

100 rows in total, one `count_` row and one `sum_` row for each of the 50 test photos.

## Scoring

Both subtasks are scored the same way. For each test photo we take the absolute difference between your answer and the truth. That error is capped, at 12 for the count and at 36 for the sum, and a photo missing from your file or carrying a non-numeric answer counts as the cap. Your metric is the mean error over the 50 test photos, the MAE, and lower is better.

Points grow linearly between fixed MAE anchors and are rounded to whole points. The zero anchor sits at what the best constant guess achieves. The two subtasks add up to 100 points.

| Count MAE   | Points               |
| ----------- | -------------------- |
| ≥ 4.0       | 0                    |
| 4.0 to 2.0  | linear from 0 to 12  |
| 2.0 to 0.25 | linear from 12 to 30 |
| ≤ 0.25      | 30                   |

| Sum MAE    | Points               |
| ---------- | -------------------- |
| ≥ 16       | 0                    |
| 16 to 6.5  | linear from 0 to 20  |
| 6.5 to 1.2 | linear from 20 to 70 |
| ≤ 1.2      | 70                   |

tasks.full_description:
A small robot is lost in a pixel world. It can only see through a tiny camera — a 64 by 64 image. The view looks like the first-person shooters from the 1990s, such as Doom or Wolfenstein 3D. The world is drawn with raycasting: walls get closer as you walk toward them, and the floor stretches out below. The robot has no map, but the camera image includes a compass at the bottom-left that points toward the target. The robot must find golden treasures while avoiding water traps.

Your job is to teach the robot two skills:
1. **Look at two camera frames and guess how much it turned.**
2. **Learn to walk to the target using only what it sees.**

This is a common problem in AI. Robots and self-driving cars use cameras to understand where they are and where to go. The robot here works the same way — it learns from pixels.

**Note:** the provided environment, along with the baseline notebook, are present in the **starter kit**. Both the train and test datasets are in the zip from **training data** (the baseline has the code required to load the data).

## Dataset Description

### Odometry Data

We give you **400 training pairs** and **100 test pairs** of camera images.

Each pair has two PNG files:

- `{pair_id}_before.png` — what the camera saw before the robot moved
- `{pair_id}_after.png` — what the camera saw after the robot moved

Both images are **64 × 64 pixels** with 3 color channels (RGB).

The labels are in `labels.csv`. Each row has four columns:

| Column     | Meaning                                              |
| ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| `pair_id`  | ID of the image pair                                 |
| `dx`       | How many cells the robot moved left/right (-3 to +3) |
| `dy`       | How many cells the robot moved up/down (-3 to +3)    |
| `dheading` | How much the robot turned                            |

The `dheading` value uses this code:

| Value | Meaning              |
| ----- | -------------------- |
| 0     | No turn              |
| 1     | Turned right (90°)   |
| 2     | Turned around (180°) |
| 3     | Turned left (90°)    |

### Navigation Environment

There is no pre-made dataset for navigation. You must train inside the environment.

The world is a **20 × 20 grid** with four terrain types:

- **Grass** — free to walk on
- **Sand** — free to walk on
- **Water** — acts like a wall (the robot cannot walk through it)
- **Golden target** — the goal the robot must reach

The robot sees a **first-person view** rendered with raycasting (like old video games). The camera image also includes a **compass** at the bottom-left that points toward the golden target.

The robot can do **4 actions**:

| Action     | Number | What it does           |
| ---------- | ------ | ---------------------- |
| Forward    | 0      | Walk one cell forward  |
| Turn left  | 1      | Turn 90° to the left   |
| Turn right | 2      | Turn 90° to the right  |
| Backward   | 3      | Walk one cell backward |

Each episode starts in a random safe cell. The target is always at least 6 cells away. The robot has **100 steps** to reach the target.

**Rewards:**
- `-1` for every step (the robot should be fast)
- `-5` for hitting water or the world edge
- `+0.5` for every cell the robot moves closer to the target
- `+100` for reaching the target

### Using the Environment

The navigation environment is registered with Gymnasium as `PixelNav-v0`. Import and create it:

```python
import pixelnav  # registers PixelNav-v0
import gymnasium as gym

env = gym.make("PixelNav-v0")
```

Interact with it:

```python
obs, info = env.reset(seed=67)   # start a new episode
obs, reward, terminated, truncated, info = env.step(0)  # do action 0 = forward
```

What you get back:

| Return       | Type            | Meaning                                                    |
| ------------ | --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `obs`        | `numpy.ndarray` | RGB image `(64, 64, 3)` — the camera view after the action |
| `reward`     | `float`         | Reward for this step                                       |
| `terminated` | `bool`          | `True` if the robot reached the target or hit water        |
| `truncated`  | `bool`          | `True` if the 100-step limit was reached                   |
| `info`       | `dict`          | Contains `episode_reward` and `steps`                      |

The camera image already includes the compass pointing to the target, so your model can read direction from the pixels.

You can also get the current frame without taking a step using `env.render()`:

```python
frame = env.render()  # returns (64, 64, 3) RGB array
```

This is useful for subtask 1 — you can collect a frame before an action and another after.

For the fast grading version (used in evaluation, not during training):

```python
from pixelnav.env import FastEvalEnv
env = FastEvalEnv()
```

`FastEvalEnv` works the same way but skips rendering. It is used during grading to replay your action sequences quickly. You do not need to use it yourself.

Close the environment when done:

```python
env.close()
```

The environment runs on CPU and can render thousands of frames per minute.

## Subtasks

You must use the `baseline.ipynb` (provided in the starter kit), it has boilerplate code such as the dataset or submission creation.

### Subtask 1 - Visual Odometry (40 points)

For every test image pair, predict how the robot moved. Your answer must be three numbers separated by semicolons:

```
dx;dy;dheading
```

For example: `0;0;1` means the robot did not move left or right, did not move up or down, but turned right.

**Evaluation:** We only score the heading prediction (`dheading`). We check if your `dheading` matches the true label. Your score depends on what percent of headings you get right (heading accuracy).

### Subtask 2 - Pixel Navigation (60 points)

Train an agent to navigate to the target in the pixel world. After training, you must predict the action list for **100 hidden test episodes**.

For each episode, give a comma-separated list of action numbers. For example:

```
0,2,0,0,1,0,0,0
```

This means: forward, turn right, forward, forward, turn left, forward, forward, forward.

The episode ends when the robot reaches the target, hits water, goes out of bounds, or uses all 100 steps.

**Evaluation:** We run your action list in the real environment for each of the 100 test episodes. We add up the rewards from all steps. Your score depends on the **mean total reward** across all 100 episodes.

## Output Format

The output file (`submission.csv`) must contain **3 columns**:

| Column        | Meaning                                                                                                 |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `subtaskID`   | `1` for odometry, `2` for navigation                                                                    |
| `datapointID` | For odometry: `odom_{pair_id}` (e.g., `odom_00000`). For navigation: `nav_{episode_id}` (e.g., `nav_0`) |
| `answer`      | For odometry: `dx;dy;dheading`. For navigation: comma-separated action numbers                          |

Example:

```csv
subtaskID,datapointID,answer
1,odom_00000,0;0;1
1,odom_00001,-1;0;3
2,nav_0,"0,2,0,0,1,0,0,0"
2,nav_1,"0,0,0,2,2,0,0"
```

You must send **only one `.csv` file** for both subtasks.

## Scoring

Your total score is the sum of both subtasks: **100 points maximum**.

### Subtask 1 — Visual Odometry (40 points)

We measure **heading accuracy** — the percent of test pairs where your `dheading` is correct.

| Heading Accuracy              | Points                                 |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------- |
| $< 30\%$                      | $0$                                    |
| $30\% \leq$ Accuracy $< 90\%$ | Linear scaling from $0$ to $40$ points |
| $\geq 90\%$                   | $40$ points                            |

The formula is:

$$
\text{points} = 40 \times \frac{\text{accuracy} - 0.30}{0.90 - 0.30}
$$

clamped between $0$ and $40$.

### Subtask 2 — Pixel Navigation (60 points)

We measure the **mean total reward** across 100 test episodes.

| Mean Reward                   | Points                                  |
| ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| $\leq -110$                   | $0$                                     |
| $-110 <$ Mean Reward $\leq 0$ | Linear scaling from $0$ to $20$ points  |
| $0 <$ Mean Reward $< 50$      | Linear scaling from $20$ to $60$ points |
| $\geq 50$                     | $60$ points                             |

The scoring uses three pieces:

- **From -110 to 0:** $20 \times \frac{\text{reward} + 110}{110}$
- **From 0 to 50:** $20 + 40 \times \frac{\text{reward}}{50}$
- **Above 50:** full $60$ points

tasks.full_description:
Pentru mai multe detalii vă recomandăm să vă alăturați comunității noastre de Discord: https://discord.gg/WSq2FnweEt

## Reguli generale

Accesul la internet este limitat doar la platforma de concurs https://judge.nitro-ai.org și la documentațiile pentru librăriile disponibile și pentru platformă: http://146.190.176.162

Trebuie să bifați, în timpul probei, submisiile care vreți să fie luate în considerare pentru clasamentul final (afișat doar după terminarea probei). În timpul probei aveți acces doar la o evaluare parțială a submisiilor.

Comunicarea cu Comisia Științifică se va face exclusiv prin intermediul platformei de concurs. Comunicarea cu Comisia Tehnică și Comisia Organizatorică se va face exclusiv prin intermediul persoanelor responsabile din centrele de concurs.

Dacă aveți probleme cu calculatorul și trebuie să vă mutați la alt calculator sau să reporniți calculatorul, anunțați supraveghetorii și insistați ca aceștia să anunțe mai departe Comisia Tehnică pentru a nu se activa sistemul anti-fraudă al platformei.

## Sistemul anti-fraudă

În momentul începerii concursului platforma vă va cere să setați sesiunea curentă. Dacă încercați să modificați sesiunea fără a anunța comisia tehnică în prealabil platforma vă va descalifica automat din concurs. 

Ce modifică sesiunea (deci **nu** aveți voie să faceți fără să anunțați înainte):
- să schimbați browserul
- să schimbați calculatorul
- să dați log out și log in din nou înainte de terminarea probei
- să intrați în cont dinafara sălii de concurs dacă ați ieșit mai devreme din probă, dar proba încă nu s-a terminat

Înainte de a ieși din sală dați log out din cont și așteptați finalizarea concursului pentru a da log in de pe alt device.

Puteți citi mai multe despre platformă și despre sistemul anti-cheat în [Ghidul de utilizare Judge Nitro AI](https://olimpiada-ai.ro/Ghidul-de-utilizare-Judge-Nitro-AI.pdf)
