tasks.full_description:
Pentru mai multe detalii vă recomandăm să vă alăturați comunității noastre de Discord: https://discord.gg/WSq2FnweEt

Se aplică [regulamentul specific](https://www.edu.ro/sites/default/files/_fi%C8%99iere/Minister/2025/olimpiade_concursuri/regulamente_actualizate/Regulament_OIA_2025.pdf) al Olimpiadei Naționale de Inteligență Artificială. 

Extras din regulament:
-

În ziua probei, elevii trebuie să aibă asupra lor un act de identitate valid cu poză (CI, pașaport sau carnet de elev vizat).

Clasamentul pe care îl vedeți în timpul probei este doar parțial. Clasamentul complet va deveni vizibil la sfârșitul probei. Trebuie să selectați, **în timpul probei**, submisiile care vreți să fie luate în considerare pentru clasamentul final. **Doar submisiile selectate de voi vor fi luate în considerare la alcătuirea clasamentului final.**

Clasamentul complet din secțiunea leaderboard este orientativ. Clasamentul oficial, anonimizat prin codurile primite în ziua concursului, în baza căruia se stabilesc premiile, medaliile și calificații la loturile de pregătire și selecție va fi afișat pe site-ul olimpiadei după competiție: https://olimpiada.nitro-ai.org/ro/2025/onia

Comunicarea cu **Comisia Științifică** se va face exclusiv prin intermediul platformei de concurs. Comunicarea cu **Comisia Tehnică și Comisia Organizatorică** se va face exclusiv prin intermediul persoanelor responsabile din centrul de concurs.

Sitemul anti-fraudă
-
Pe platformă este implementat un sistem pentru detecția automată a tentativelor de fraudă. **În cazul în care astfel de tentative au fost depistate pe contul tău în timpul concursului vei fi descalificat automat din concurs și vei pierde dreptul de a continua proba respectivă.** 

Din acest motiv, se recomandă ca, după începerea competiției, să nu te deconectezi și reconectezi pe platformă fără motive întemeiate. Dacă totuși, din motive tehnice, ai nevoie să schimbi sesiunea de lucru (ex.: te muți la alt calculator) asigură-te că supraveghetorii anunță Comisia Tehnică de acest lucru **înainte de a te muta**.

Pentru mai multe detalii despre sistemul anti-fraudă, sistemul de submit sau utilizarea platformei, uită-te în Ghidul de Utilizator

tasks.full_description:
# Română

## Detalii eveniment

Etapa națională se desfășoară fizic la [Genesis College, București](https://genesis.ro/) .

## Eligibilitate

Pot participa exclusiv elevii calificați conform etapei județene care și-au confirmat locul prin [formular](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLScbU32e5nDKgelgPeXY_h3ufIppBvU0HqJva9QspAxSPsi11Q/viewform?usp=sharing&ouid=101868508878291902444) și prin apăsarea butonului "Register" din platformă în perioada de înscriere.

## Pachete permise

**Computation & Mathematics**
numpy, scipy, sympy, mpmath

**Machine Learning & Data Science**
scikit-learn, joblib, threadpoolctl

**Deep Learning & Reinforcement Learning**
stable_baselines3, gymnasium, torch, torchvision, torchaudio, torchmetrics, numba, llvmlite, triton, Farama-Notifications

**Data Processing & Analysis**
pandas, gensim, smart_open, cloudpickle, fsspec, cloudpathlib, filelock

**Data Visualization**
matplotlib, seaborn, contourpy, cycler

**Computer Vision & Image Processing**
opencv-python, pillow, scikit-image, ImageIO, tifffile, lazy-loader

**Natural Language Processing**
nltk, spacy, spacy-legacy, spacy-loggers, regex, Unidecode, lark, catalogue, wasabi, srsly, thinc, weasel, confection

**Graph & Network Analysis**
networkx

**Jupyter & Interactive Computing**
jupyter, jupyterlab, jupyterlab_server, jupyterlab_widgets, jupyterlab_pygments, notebook, notebook_shim, jupyter_client, jupyter_core, jupyter_server, jupyter_server_terminals, jupyter-lsp, jupyter-console, jupyter-events, ipython, ipykernel, ipywidgets, ipython_pygments_lexers, widgetsnbextension, nbformat, nbclient, nbconvert, comm, nest-asyncio, traitlets, matplotlib-inline, executing, asttokens, stack-data, pure_eval, jedi, parso, debugpy, pexpect, ptyprocess, prompt_toolkit, wcwidth, pyzmq, tornado, terminado, decorator, async-lru

**Web & Networking**
httpx, httpcore, h11, anyio, requests, urllib3, certifi, charset-normalizer, idna, websocket-client, beautifulsoup4, soupsieve

**Serialization, Schemas & Data Formats**
PyYAML, json5, jsonschema, jsonschema-specifications, fastjsonschema, jsonpointer, referencing, rpds-py, attrs, annotated-types, pydantic, pydantic_core, arrow, isoduration, python-dateutil, python-json-logger, babel, markdown-it-py, mdurl, mistune, rich, bleach, webencodings, tinycss2, pandocfilters, defusedxml, fqdn, uri-template, rfc3339-validator, rfc3986-validator, rfc3987-syntax, webcolors

**Templating & Rendering**
Jinja2, MarkupSafe, Pygments

**CLI & System Utilities**
click, typer, typer-slim, shellingham, tqdm, packaging, six, platformdirs, Send2Trash, psutil, wrapt, overrides, fonttools, prometheus_client

**Security & Cryptography**
argon2-cffi, argon2-cffi-bindings

**Low-Level Dependencies**
cffi, pycparser, kiwisolver, pyparsing, cymem, murmurhash, preshed, blis, typing_extensions, typing-inspection, annotated-doc, tzdata

## Site-uri Web permise

1. Core Deep Learning Frameworks
- [PyTorch](http://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/index.html) – Core deep learning library.
- [TorchVision](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/index.html) – Datasets, models, and transforms for PyTorch.
- [Stable Baselines3](https://stable-baselines3.readthedocs.io/en/master/) – Reinforcement learning algorithms.
- [Gymnasium](https://gymnasium.farama.org/) – Reinforcement learning environments.
- [TorchMetrics](https://lightning.ai/docs/torchmetrics/stable/) – Metrics for PyTorch models.

2. Machine Learning & Classical AI
- [Scikit-learn](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/index.html) – Classical ML algorithms and utilities.
- [Pandas](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/) – Data manipulation and analysis.
- [NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/index.html) – Core numerical computing library.
- [SciPy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/) – Scientific computing and advanced math functions.

3. NLP (Natural Language Processing)
- [spaCy](https://spacy.io/usage) – Industrial NLP library.
- [NLTK](https://www.nltk.org/) – NLP toolkit for text processing.
- [Gensim](https://radimrehurek.com/gensim/auto_examples/index.html#documentation) – Topic modeling and word embeddings.
- [regex](https://pypi.org/project/regex/) – Advanced regular expressions.

4. Computer Vision & Imaging
- [OpenCV](https://docs.opencv.org/4.x/) – Computer vision algorithms.
- [Scikit-Image](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/) – Image processing in Python.
- [Pillow](https://pillow.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) – Image manipulation library.
- [ImageIO](https://imageio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) – Reading/writing images.

5. Visualization
- [Matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/index.html) – Core plotting library.
- [Seaborn](https://seaborn.pydata.org/) – Statistical visualization on top of Matplotlib.

6. Optimization & Math Libraries
- [CVXPY](https://www.cvxpy.org/) – Convex optimization in Python.
- [CVXOPT](https://cvxopt.org/) – Convex optimization library.
- [OSQP](https://osqp.org/docs/) – Quadratic programming solver.
- [Clarabel](https://clarabel.org/stable/) – Python solver for constrained optimization.
- [SymPy](https://docs.sympy.org/latest/index.html) – Symbolic mathematics.
- [MPMath](https://mpmath.org/doc/current/) – Arbitrary-precision arithmetic.
- [Patsy](https://patsy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) – Describing statistical models.
- [Polars](https://docs.pola.rs/) – Fast DataFrame library.

7. Performance & Computation Tools
- [Numba](https://numba.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) – JIT compilation for Python.
- [LLVMlite](https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) – Backend for Numba.
- [Triton](https://triton-lang.org/main/index.html) – GPU programming.
- [Joblib](https://joblib.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) – Parallel processing and caching.
- [tqdm](https://tqdm.github.io/) – Progress bars for loops.

8. Notebook & Python Ecosystem
- [Jupyter](https://docs.jupyter.org/en/latest/) – Interactive notebooks.
- [Python Docs](https://docs.python.org/3/) – Core language reference.
- [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/) – Data validation and settings management.

## Medalii

[What are the prizes, how are medals distributed, whether they receive diplomas for participation or being in the top 10. Prizes, medals, diplomas are distributed externally, at the moment the platform only gives Elo]: #

Primii 1/12 concurenți din fiecare secțiune primesc medalie de aur, următorii 2/12 primesc argint, următorii 3/12 bronz, conform clasamentului general. Toți elevii de liceu care primesc medalie conform etapei naționale din acest an se califică automat la barajul pentru lot.


## Motive de descalificare

Nu este permis accesul în sala de concurs a candidaţilor cu telefoanele mobile, mijloace electronice de calcul sau de comunicare, dispozitive de memorare externă, căşti sau orice sursă de informare (manuale, dicționare, notițe, etc.). 

Nerespectarea dispozițiilor referitoare la introducerea de materiale interzise în sala de concurs, indiferent dacă materialele interzise au fost folosite sau nu, precum și frauda sau tentativa de fraudă duc la eliminarea din concurs a candidatului de către președintele comisiei.

**Atenție!** Dacă ați părăsit sala înainte de finalizarea competiției așteptați 2 ore după finalizarea acesteia pentru a vă loga din nou. Logarea din afara spațiului de concurs în timpul competiției va duce la descalificarea automată


Mai multe informații și regulamentul complet găsiți pe site-ul oficial: https://olimpiada.nitro-ai.org/ro

Pentru mai multe detalii și anunțuri rapide înainte de probă, vă recomandăm să vă alăturați comunității noastre de Discord: https://discord.gg/WSq2FnweEt

- [Jupyter](https://docs.jupyter.org/en/latest/) – Interactive notebooks.
- [Python Docs](https://docs.python.org/3/) – Core language reference.
- [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/) – Data validation and settings management.

## Medalii

[What are the prizes, how are medals distributed, whether they receive diplomas for participation or being in the top 10. Prizes, medals, diplomas are distributed externally, at the moment the platform only gives Elo]: #

Primii 1/12 concurenți din fiecare secțiune primesc medalie de aur, următorii 2/12 primesc argint, următorii 3/12 bronz, conform clasamentului general. Toți elevii de liceu care primesc medalie conform etapei naționale din acest an se califică automat la barajul pentru lot.

Nerespectarea dispozițiilor referitoare la introducerea de materiale interzise în sala de concurs, indiferent dacă materialele interzise au fost folosite sau nu, precum și frauda sau tentativa de fraudă duc la eliminarea din concurs a candidatului de către președintele comisiei.

**Atenție!** Dacă ați părăsit sala înainte de finalizarea competiției așteptați 2 ore după finalizarea acesteia pentru a vă loga din nou. Logarea din afara spațiului de concurs în timpul competiției va duce la descalificarea automată

tasks.full_description:
# Română

## Detalii eveniment

Barajul pentru lot se desfășoară fizic la [Genesis College, București](https://genesis.ro/) .

## Eligibilitate

Pot participa exclusiv elevii calificați conform etapei naționale. Se califică la baraj elevii de liceu care au câștigat medalie la oricare secțiune a etapei naționale ROAI.

## Pachete permise

**Computation & Mathematics**
numpy, scipy, sympy, mpmath

**Machine Learning & Data Science**
scikit-learn, joblib, threadpoolctl

**Deep Learning & Reinforcement Learning**
stable_baselines3, gymnasium, torch, torchvision, torchmetrics, numba, llvmlite, triton, Farama-Notifications, transformers, huggingface_hub, safetensors, hf-xet

**Data Processing & Analysis**
pandas, gensim, smart_open, cloudpickle, fsspec, cloudpathlib, filelock

**Data Visualization**
matplotlib, seaborn, contourpy, cycler

**Computer Vision & Image Processing**
opencv-python, pillow, scikit-image, ImageIO, tifffile, lazy-loader, timm

**Natural Language Processing**
nltk, spacy, spacy-legacy, spacy-loggers, regex, Unidecode, lark, catalogue, wasabi, srsly, thinc, weasel, confection, tokenizers

**Graph & Network Analysis**

**Jupyter & Interactive Computing**
jupyter, jupyterlab, jupyterlab_server, jupyterlab_widgets, jupyterlab_pygments, notebook, notebook_shim, jupyter_client, jupyter_core, jupyter_server, jupyter_server_terminals, jupyter-lsp, jupyter-console, jupyter-events, ipython, ipykernel, ipywidgets, ipython_pygments_lexers, widgetsnbextension, nbformat, nbclient, nbconvert, comm, nest-asyncio, traitlets, matplotlib-inline, executing, asttokens, stack-data, pure_eval, jedi, parso, debugpy, pexpect, ptyprocess, prompt_toolkit, wcwidth, pyzmq, tornado, terminado, decorator, async-lru

**Web & Networking**
httpx, httpcore, h11, anyio, requests, urllib3, certifi, charset-normalizer, idna, websocket-client, beautifulsoup4, soupsieve, gdown

**Serialization, Schemas & Data Formats**
PyYAML, json5, jsonschema, jsonschema-specifications, fastjsonschema, jsonpointer, referencing, rpds-py, attrs, annotated-types, pydantic, pydantic_core, arrow, isoduration, python-dateutil, python-json-logger, babel, markdown-it-py, mdurl, mistune, rich, bleach, webencodings, tinycss2, pandocfilters, defusedxml, fqdn, uri-template, rfc3339-validator, rfc3986-validator, rfc3987-syntax, webcolors

## Site-uri Web permise

1. Core Deep Learning Frameworks

- [TorchVision](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/index.html) – Datasets, models, and transforms for PyTorch.

2. Machine Learning & Classical AI

3. NLP (Natural Language Processing)

4. Computer Vision & Imaging

5. Visualization

6. Optimization & Math Libraries
- [CVXPY](https://www.cvxpy.org/) – Convex optimization in Python.

7. Performance & Computation Tools

- [LLVMlite](https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) – Backend for Numba.
- [Triton](https://triton-lang.org/main/index.html) – GPU programming.
- [Joblib](https://joblib.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) – Parallel processing and caching.
- [tqdm](https://tqdm.github.io/) – Progress bars for loops.

8. Notebook & Python Ecosystem
- [Jupyter](https://docs.jupyter.org/en/latest/) – Interactive notebooks.
- [Python Docs](https://docs.python.org/3/) – Core language reference.
- [Pydantic](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/) – Data validation and settings management.

Conform clasamentului general, primii doi elevi cu cel mai mare scor de la fiecare clasă de liceu se califică automat în lotul național. Restul până la 24 de locuri sunt alocate conform clasamentului general.

tasks.name: Algolymp Challenge — Heart Attack Risk Prediction
tasks.description: Бинарная классификация пациентов по риску перенесённого инфаркта (HadHeartAttack) на табличных медико-демографических данных. Подзадача 1 — посчитать отношение редкого класса к частому в трейне; подзадача 2 — обучить модель и предсказать Yes/No для теста. Метрика — F1 (порог 54% на полный балл), сильный дисбаланс классов.
tasks.full_description:
Welcome to **Mission: Heartbeat**, the world’s most advanced health intelligence program.
You’ve just been recruited by **The Department of Predictive Medicine (DPM)**, a secretive organization that monitors the health of citizens across the globe to prevent medical emergencies before they happen.

Your latest assignment? A dataset containing medical history and lifestyle information from tens of thousands of individuals. The agency suspects that within this data lies a pattern that could help identify who has already suffered a heart attack and who might be at risk in the future.

Time is ticking. The DPM’s predictive systems are only as good as the models behind them, and it’s your job to make sure they’re accurate.


## Train & Test Data

The dataset consists of patient records, each containing multiple medical and lifestyle features.
The target variable, **HadHeartAttack**, indicates whether the individual has previously experienced a heart attack.


## Task

The task consists of two subtasks:

### Subtask 1 -- Label Distribution Analysis (10 points)

Calculate the **ratio between the least common and the most common label** in the training dataset for the target field **HadHeartAttack**.

### Subtask 2 -- Heart Attack Prediction (90 points)

Using the provided training data, build a model capable of predicting the value of **HadHeartAttack** for each record in the test dataset.


Every prediction brings you closer to uncovering the hidden signals that define the human heart.

The success of **Mission: Heartbeat** (and the reputation of the DPM) rests in your hands.

## Expected Output

An output.csv file that includes the following 3 columns:

- **subtaskID** – represents the subtask number (1 or 2)
- **datapointID** – refers to the id column from `test_data.csv` (for subtask 1 the datapointID should be 0)
- **answer** – the corresponding answer for that datapoint for the respective subtask

The **answer** column must contain:

- For **Subtask 1**: a **numeric value** representing the ratio between the least common and the most common label in the training dataset, with a precision of 6 decimal places.
- For **Subtask 2**: a **binary value** (`Yes` or `No`) indicating whether the patient has had a heart attack.

The evaluation metric for Subtask 2 is binary **F1 score**. The mapping from F1 to contest points is expressed in tiers as follows:

- **F1 ≥ 54%** → 90 points  
- **40% ≤ F1 < 54%** → 30–75 points  
- **F1 < 40%** → 0–30 points  

Values in the **answer** column are case-insensitive (e.g., `yes`, `YES`, or `YeS` are all accepted as `Yes`).

tasks.name: Algolymp Winter Contest — Corrupted Gift Image Classification
tasks.description: Классификация зашумлённых изображений (salt-and-pepper, glitch-артефакты) на 7 классов подарков (dog, cat, car, bicycle, boat, bird, aeroplane). Подзадача 1 — посчитать среднюю разность пиксельной яркости чётных и нечётных строк; подзадача 2 — обучить классификатор, метрика — accuracy. Доступен валидационный сет с оригинальными (чистыми) картинками.
tasks.full_description:
It's the week before Christmas and the North Pole servers are buzzing. But something awful has happened! Grinch, who wants to ruin Christmas once more, **has scrambled** the photographic catalogue of Santa’s gifts. Children from all across the globe have sent Santa dozens of images with the presents they want, but many of them are corrupted. They are scattered with **red-pepper noise**, torn by **glitch effects**, and stamped with other corruption artifacts. The Workshop needs an AI engineer to identify each gift before the sleigh departs and wishlists are lost **forever**!

## Task

The task consists of two subtasks:

### Subtask 1 -- Row Parity Bias Analysis (10 points)

For each corrupted image, compute the difference between:

- the mean pixel value of all **even-numbered rows**, and
- the mean pixel value of all **odd-numbered rows**

(using all color channels and assuming zero-based indexing).

Your task is to calculate the **average of this difference across all images** in the test dataset.

### Subtask 2 -- Gift Classification (90 points)

Once restored, each test image must be classified into one of **seven different gift categories**.

Using the labeled training data, build a model capable of recognizing the type of object present in each image.

The evaluation metric for this subtask is **classification accuracy**.

## Train & Test Data

Train Data represents the correspondence between the images in the "train" folder and their correct labels and has the following format:

ID: Represents the unique identifier of each image.

path: The path for each image in the "train" folder.

label: One of the following categories:
- dog
- cat
- car
- bicycle
- boat
- bird
- aeroplane

(I know, kids these days have some weird wishes.)

Test Data has the same format, except for the "label" field, which is missing.

## Dataset

The starter kit includes:
- data/images/train_data - train image dataset
- data/images/test_data - test image dataset
- data/images/validation_data - an image dataset which contains examples of original images (before corruption) that can be used for validating
- data/train_data.csv
- data/test_data.csv
- main.ipynb - a notebook which contains code for creating a submission and for validating

## Expected Output

You must submit an `output.csv` file containing the following columns:

- **subtaskID** — the subtask number (1 or 2)
- **datapointID** — the image identifier  
  - For Subtask 1, this value should be `1`
- **answer** — the corresponding solution for the given subtask and datapoint

The **answer** column must contain:

- For **Subtask 1**:  
  A single numeric value representing the mean row parity difference across all images, with a precision of **6 decimal places**.

- For **Subtask 2**:  
  The predicted class label for each test image.

tasks.name: Algolymp Winter Contest — Romanian Letters Region and NER
tasks.description: Двойная NLP-задача на румынских письмах из разных регионов Румынии. Task 1 — классификация региона происхождения текста (F1); Task 2 — токенный NER с метками PERSON/LOCATION/ORGANIZATION и др. (F1). Тексты с региональными диалектными особенностями.
tasks.full_description:
**Winter NLP Contest -- Lost Letters, Saved Gifts 🎄📜**

It is Christmas Eve, and the North Pole is facing an unusual situation.
Due to a cosmic accident, Santa Claus has been temporarily replaced by
another Santa, coming from a distant planet. Although he has access to
advanced technology for delivering gifts, a major problem arises: he
cannot understand the children's letters from Earth.

The letters are written in Romanian and come from different historical
regions of Romania. Each region has its own linguistic, cultural, and
geographical particularities, and without understanding them correctly,
Santa risks mixing up gifts, destinations, or even the children
themselves.

Fortunately, the original Santa Claus left behind a special dataset.
Using it, your mission is to develop models to help the new Santa save
Christmas.

### Task 1 --- Region Classification

The first step is to sort the letters into piles corresponding to
Romania regions.

For each Romanian text, the model must predict the region of origin
based on linguistic and contextual features.

**Evaluation metric:**

- F1 score

### 🔍 Task 2 --- 
After sorting the letters, Santa needs to understand their content.

Children often mention:

- names of people,

- cities and villages,

- schools,

- institutions,

- other relevant places or entities.

The second task consists of developing a model that correctly identifies
and labels entities in the texts by tokens, a 0 means nothing relevant and another number has a specific meaning.

**Example labels:**

- PERSON

- LOCATION

- ORGANIZATION

- other types defined in the dataset

<!-- -->

- correct delivery of gifts.

**Evaluation metric:**

- F1 score

### 📁 Dataset

The data includes:

- train_texts -- labeled texts for training

- test_texts -- unlabeled texts (for evaluation)

### 📤 Expected Output

Participants must submit an output.csv file containing:

- taskID --- 1 or 2

- datapointID --- the letter ID

- answer --- the corresponding solution

**Content of the answer field:**

- **Task 1:** the predicted region

- **Task 2:** the list of identified entities, in the required format

tasks.name: Algolymp PreONIA IX-X 2026 — Cartoon vs Photo Medium and Content
tasks.description: Две задачи компьютерного зрения по личной коллекции Gabi. Task 1 — бинарная классификация cartoon/photo по медиуму (метрика accuracy, порог 0.99 на полный балл); Task 2 — кластеризация изображений на elephant/house без меток (метрика ARI, порог 0.9). Разрешён предобученный ResNet18.
tasks.full_description:
Gabi has always seen the world in two ways. As a cartoon artist, he fills sketchbooks with hand-drawn illustrations. As a photographer, he captures real-world moments that inspire his art. Over time, he built a collection that blends both sides of his creative life  cartoons he drew, and photos he took.

This project grew from that collection. The goal is to build systems that can look at an image and understand it on two levels:

1.  Medium Classification: Tell whether an image was drawn or photographed.
2.   Understanding data: Figure out what the image actually shows (elephant or house).

## What's in the Collection

The images come straight from Gabi's own work:

- **Cartoon Illustrations:** Hand-drawn by Gabi.
- **Real Photographs:** Taken by Gabi as visual references.

## The Tasks

### Task 1: Medium Classification (20 points)

Look at an image and decide whether it's a cartoon or a photograph.

Gabi is pretty confident his art style stands out. If your model gets it right at least 99% of the time, you'll earn full points. If you're slightly below that, you'll still score well. Anything under 95% accuracy is a good sign you might want to try a different approach.

| Metric | Score |
| :--- | :--- |
| Accuracy < 0.95 | 3  points |
| 0.95 ≤ Accuracy < 0.99 | Linear normalization between 10 and 90 points |
| 0.99 ≤ Accuracy | 100 points |

### Task 2: Understanding data (80 points)

 Your job is to figure out which ones show elephants and which ones show houses — purely by looking.

| Metric | Score |
| :--- | :--- |
| ARI < 0.5 | 3 points |
| 0.5 ≤ ARI < 0.9 | Linear normalization between 10 and 90 points |
| 0.9 ≤ ARI | 100 points |

## How to Submit Your Results

You'll need to submit a single file in CSV format with three columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| The task ID (1 or 2) | The image ID from the test set | Your prediction (`cartoon`/`photo` for Task 1, `elephant`/`house` for Task 2) |

Only one file is needed for both tasks. See `sample_output.csv` for the correct format.
## Observation:
You are allowed to use a pretrained model: RESNET 18.
## Scoring Summary

- **Task 1:** Worth 20 points total, based on accuracy.
- **Task 2:** Worth 80 points total, based on how well your groupings match the real categories.
- **Total Score:** Combined across both tasks (up to 100 points total).
- Submissions that don't run successfully receive 0 points and won't count toward your submission limit.

tasks.name: Algolymp PreONIA IX-X 2026 — RNA Reactivity Profile Prediction
tasks.description: Регрессия последовательность-в-последовательность: для каждой синтетической РНК (длина 64–256 нуклеотидов A/U/G/C) предсказать вектор реактивности по позициям. Метрика — Mean Pearson Correlation Coefficient (порог 0.9 на полный балл). Сигнал зависит от локальных мотивов, нуклеотидной идентичности и сглаживания соседей плюс шум.
tasks.full_description:
Predicting RNA structural properties is a fundamental challenge in computational biology. The stability and reactivity of an RNA molecule are dictated by its primary sequence and the resulting secondary structures, such as hairpin loops and motifs. Accurate models for these profiles enable the design of synthetic RNAs for therapeutics and vaccines. This task requires predicting a continuous target profile for synthetic RNA sequences based on local nucleotide properties and structural motifs.

## Dataset Description

The dataset consists of synthetic RNA sequences composed of four nucleotides: Adenine (A), Uracil (U), Guanine (G), and Cytosine (C). Each sequence has a length between 64 and 256 bases. The `target_profile` represents a simulated reactivity or stability value for each position in the sequence. These values are influenced by:
1.  **Base Identity:** Individual nucleotides have different baseline propensities.
2.  **Local Motifs:** Specific sequences (e.g., `GCGC`, `UAAA`) act as stabilizers or destabilizers.
3.  **Structural Neighborhoods:** Values are smoothed to simulate the correlation between adjacent bases in a folded structure.
4.  **Noise:** Stochastic variance is added to simulate experimental conditions.

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - Target Profile Prediction (100 points)

Predict the numerical `target_profile` for each sequence in the test set. For every nucleotide in a sequence of length, you must provide a corresponding float value.

The evaluation metric is the **Mean Pearson Correlation Coefficient (MPCC)**. For each sequence, the Pearson correlation between the predicted vector and the ground truth vector is computed. The final score is the average of these correlations across all sequences.

Any prediction that does not match the sequence length, contains non-numeric values will receive a correlation of 0.0 for that specific sequence.

## Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Represents the subtask id (1) | The IDs from the test data (e.g., `RNA_00000`) | Space-separated floats representing the profile |

Check the `sample_submission.csv` for a valid submission file.

## Scoring

### Subtask 1

Scoring is based on the Mean Pearson Correlation Coefficient (MPCC), with the baseline threshold set at 0.3 (10 points) and the best score at 0.9 (100 points). Any metric obtained between these values will be linearly scaled as a score between 10 and 90. Failed submissions or those with errors get 0 points.

| Metric | Score |
| :--- | :--- |
| MPCC $ < $ 0.3 | 0 points |
| 0.3 $ \leq $ MPCC $ < $ 0.9 | Linear normalization between 10 and 90 points |
| 0.9 $ \leq $ MPCC | 100 points |

tasks.name: Algolymp PreONIA XI-XII 2026 — Music Composer Classification
tasks.description: Классификация автора композиции по последовательности нот (duration в секундах, pitch в MIDI). Каждая песня — упорядоченный набор нот, нужно предсказать композитора. Метрика — weighted F1 (порог 0.9 на полный балл).
tasks.full_description:
Deep in the ocean, in Bikini Bottom, Spongebob borrowed Squidward's mp3 player, but while messing with Patrick, the deleted all of his mp3s. Although Squidward is sad, he remembers he knows all of the notes for each song, but he forgot the names of some of the composers. Help Squidward!
![sad squidward](https://media1.tenor.com/m/XHf0aprrlx0AAAAC/meme.gif)
<!--![spongebob](https://media1.tenor.com/m/ry7vDwNkJs0AAAAd/spongebob-ascend.gif)-->

### Dataset Description

The input consists of three files: `composers.csv` and `train_data.csv` and `test_data.csv`.

- `composers.csv` contains three columns: `subtaskID` and `datapointID` and 'answer'. `subtaskID` is equal to 1 and `answer` is the composer of the song with the id equal to `datapointID`.
- `train_data.csv` and `test_data.csv` contain three columns:  `id`, `duration` and `pitch`. Each row represents a note in the song with the given id. The row order represents note order. By `duration`(float) we refer how many seconds a note is held out for, and `pitch`(int) meaning what note is played, in standard midi notation.

### Task 1 - 100 points

Find out who composed each song present in `test_data.csv`.

### Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
1 | the `id` from `test_data.csv` for each song| the compositor

You must only send one `.csv` file.


### Scoring

Scoring is based on weighted F1, with the baseline threshold set at 0.3 (10 points) and best score at 0.9 (100 points). 

Metric | Score
-|-
F1 $<$ 0.3 | 3 points
0.3 $\leq$ F1 $<$ 0.85 | Linear normalization between 10 and 90 points
0.9 $\leq$ F1 | 100 points

tasks.name: Algolymp PreONIA XI-XII 2026 — Gridworld Value and Policy
tasks.description: Классический RL на стохастической сетке 6x6 (вероятности 0.8/0.1/0.1, gamma=0.9, штраф -0.01, цель +1.0). Subtask 1 — посчитать V(s) для всех клеток (метрика MSE); Subtask 2 — определить оптимальное действие pi*(s) для непреградных клеток (метрика accuracy). Известная модель среды — решается через Value Iteration.
tasks.full_description:
In many real-world robotics applications, movement is not deterministic. Factors such as wheel slippage, sensor noise, or environmental interference introduce uncertainty into an agent's trajectory. To navigate successfully, the agent must model the environment as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and solve for a policy that maximizes the expected cumulative discounted reward. This task requires implementing fundamental Reinforcement Learning algorithms to compute the value of specific states and the optimal actions to take within a stochastic 6x6 grid.

## Dataset Description

The environment is a 6x6 grid representing a maze. Each cell is indexed from 0 to 35, starting from the top-left (0) to the bottom-right (35) in row-major order.
- **Start Cell**: The robot begins at cell 0.
- **Terminal State**: The goal is located at cell 35.
- **Obstacles**: Certain cells are impassable; if a robot attempts to move into an obstacle or a boundary, it remains in its current cell.
- **Dynamics**: Movement is stochastic. When an action is taken:
    - Probability 0.8: Move in the intended direction.
    - Probability 0.1: Move 90° clockwise from the intended direction.
    - Probability 0.1: Move 90° counterclockwise from the intended direction.

The `test_data.csv` file provides the layout, identifying which cells are empty, obstacles, or terminal points. Types of cells: normal (0), obstacle (1), start (2), finish (3).

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - Value Function Estimation (50 points)

Compute the value $ V(s) $ for every cell in the grid using a discount factor of $ \gamma = 0.9 $. The terminal state (cell 35) provides a reward of $ +1.0 $. To ensure a unique solution, every movement that does not result in entering the terminal state incurs a small step penalty of $ -0.01 $. Values should be calculated until convergence.

**Metric**: Mean Squared Error (MSE).

### Subtask 2 - Optimal Policy Mapping (50 points)

Determine the optimal action $ \pi_*(s) $ for each non-terminal and non-obstacle cell. Actions are encoded as:
- **0**: Up
- **1**: Right
- **2**: Down
- **3**: Left

The policy must maximize the expected discounted reward. 

**Metric**: Accuracy.

## Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID                          | datapointID                       | answer                                                      |
| ---------------------------------- | --------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| represents the subtask id (1 or 2) | the IDs from the test data (0-35) | answer for each ID (float for Subtask 1, int for Subtask 2) |

You must only send one `.csv` file for both subtasks.

## Scoring

### Subtask 1

Scoring is based on MSE. Since lower MSE is better, the score is calculated based on the proximity to zero.

| Metric                           | Score                                         |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| MSE $ > 0.25 $                  | 0 points                                      |
| $ 0.01 < $ MSE $ \leq 0.25 $ | Linear normalization between 10 and 40 points |
| MSE $ \leq 0.001 $             | 50 points                                    |

### Subtask 2

Scoring is based on the Accuracy of the predicted optimal actions.

| Metric                               | Score                                         |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------- |
| Accuracy $ < 0.5 $                 | 0 points                                      |
| $ 0.5 \leq $ Accuracy $ < 0.95 $ | Linear normalization between 10 and 40 points |
| Accuracy $ \geq 0.95 $             | 50 points                                    |

tasks.name: Algolymp PreOJIA IX-X — Pirate Ship Arrival Delay Regression
tasks.description: Табличная задача о задержках пиратских кораблей: предсказать ARRIVAL_DELAY по фичам (день, экипаж, порты, расписание, дистанция). Subtask 1 — процент пропусков в DAY_OF_WEEK теста; Subtask 2 — мода часа отправления по каждому экипажу (формат времени — линейные сотые часа); основная — регрессия задержки прибытия.
tasks.full_description:
The High Seas Syndicate is losing doubloons, and it is not because of the Royal Navy! Pirate vessels carrying precious loot (ranging from cursed Aztec gold to barrels of premium spiced rum) are completely unpredictable. Storms, naval blockades, mutinies, and the occasional kraken are throwing the Syndicate’s ledgers into absolute chaos. 

The Grand Quartermaster cannot plan fencing operations or grog rations if the ships keep arriving days late. You, a rogue Data Sorcerer, have been contracted to analyze the Syndicate's historical shipping logs. Before you can predict the future, however, you must clean up the Quartermaster's sloppy record-keeping and understand the operational habits of the individual pirate factions. Your ultimate mission is to predict exactly how delayed a ship will be upon reaching its destination so the Syndicate can optimize its illicit supply chain.

## Dataset Description

The dataset consists of thousands of recorded voyages across the treacherous waters of the Caribbean. Each log entry contains details about the ship, the crew, and the route. 

* **DAY_OF_WEEK:** The day the anchor was weighed.
* **PIRATE_CREW:** The specific syndicate faction manning the rigging.
* **SHIP_NAME:** The vessel braving the high seas.
* **ORIGIN_PORT:** The smuggler's cove where the voyage began (e.g., *Tortuga*, *Nassau*).
* **DESTINATION_PORT:** The safe haven where the loot is expected to be unloaded.
* **SCHEDULED_DEPARTURE:** The hour the captain *swore* they would set sail.
* **DISTANCE:** The length of the journey in nautical leagues.
* **SCHEDULED_ARRIVAL:** The expected time the crow's nest lookout should spot land.
* **ARRIVAL_DELAY (Target Variable):** The actual delay of the ship upon dropping anchor, measured in hours. A negative value means the crew had favorable winds and arrived early!

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - The Lost Logs (10 points)

The Quartermaster spilled grog all over the recent ledgers, and some of the departure days have been washed away completely! 

Your first task is to calculate the **percentage of missing (null) values** in the `DAY_OF_WEEK` column of the test dataset.

### Subtask 2 - The Pirate's Schedule (10 points)

Different crews have different habits: some prefer setting sail under the cover of night, while others leave at high noon after nursing a hangover. The Syndicate needs to know the most common departure hour for every crew.

Your task is to find the **mode (most frequent value) of the scheduled departure hour** for each unique `PIRATE_CREW`. 

**Understanding the Time Format:**
The `SCHEDULED_DEPARTURE` is logged as a float number ranging from `0.0` to `2400.0`. This does *not* represent standard HHMM format, but rather a **linear normalization of hours**, where a value of `100.0` equates to exactly one hour. 
* For example, a value of `1250.0` represents 12:30 (since 50 is exactly halfway between 1200 and 1300). 
* A value of `825.0` represents 08:15 (a quarter of the way through the 8th hour).
* A value of `75.0` represents 00:45 (three-quarters of the way through the 0th hour).

For this task, you must extract **only the hour** from this float (e.g., hour `12` from `1250.0`, hour `8` from `825.0`, hour `0` from `75.0`). Calculate the mode of these extracted hours for every crew.

### Subtask 3 - Plunder Punctuality Prediction (80 points)

Now that you understand the data, it is time for the main event: build a regression model to predict the "Arrival Delay" for each voyage. Accurately forecasting this temporal drift allows the Syndicate to hide their contraband before the authorities arrive.

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**.

* Full points (80) are awarded for MAE ≤ 5.0. 
* Zero points are awarded for MAE ≥ 12.0. 
* Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

## Output Format

The output file (`submission.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| --- | --- | --- | 
| 1 | `1` | The percentage of missing values |
| 2 | The name of the `PIRATE_CREW` (e.g., *Salty Dogs*) | The mode of the departure hour (integer) |
| 3 | The `SampleID` from the test data | The predicted delay (continuous value) |

You must only send one `.csv` file containing the answers for all three subtasks.

## Scoring

Total scoring is the sum of the points obtained across all three subtasks.

### Subtask 1 (The Lost Logs)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
| Exact match (up to 3 decimal places) | 10 points |
| Incorrect | 0 points |

### Subtask 2 (The Pirate's Schedule)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
| Accuracy across all unique crews | Scaled between 0 and 10 points |

### Subtask 3 (Plunder Punctuality Prediction)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
| MAE ≤ 5.0 | 80 points |
| 5.0 < MAE < 12.0 | Linear normalization between 0 and 80 points | 
| MAE ≥ 12.0 | 0 points |

tasks.name: Algolymp PreOJIA IX-X — Harbor Property Clustering and Pricing
tasks.description: Мультимодальная задача по объявлениям о недвижимости: структурные фичи плюс AI-сгенерированные тексты с галлюцинациями. Subtask 1 — кластеризация на скрытые рыночные сегменты (метрика ARI, порог 0.9); Subtask 2 — регрессия sale price (метрика R², порог 0.79). Требуется работа и с табличной частью, и с шумным текстом.
tasks.full_description:
After months at sea, the pirates finally found a safe harbor a quiet port city where no one asked questions and gold could change hands without raising suspicion. Their chests overflowed with stolen coins, but hiding treasure underground was no longer enough. The modern world demanded something smarter. In this bustling port, property listings were generated by an ambitious AI-driven system that described houses in grand, exaggerated detail.
## Dataset Description

The dataset contains residential property records from multiple partners. Each data point includes:

- **Structured features:** Numerical and categorical attributes describing physical and location characteristics.
- **Text description:** AI-generated property descriptions prone to hallucinations and inconsistencies.
- **Sale price:** Actual transaction price (only available in training data).

The dataset exhibits high variability, with some descriptions containing spurious details, exaggerations, or missing information. Realistic modeling requires careful handling of both structured and unstructured features.

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 – Harbor Crews Discovery (20 points)

Beneath the AI’s exaggerated property tales lies a hidden structure of the harbor’s housing market. Your mission is to uncover the natural market segments within the dataset.


**Task:** Group properties into meaningful groups using the data. Then assign each test property a cluster label.The evaluation metric for this subject is the **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)**.
* Full points (20) are awarded for $\text{ARI} \geq 0.9$. 
* Zero points are awarded for $\text{ARI} < 0.3$ . 
* Scores are linearly scaled between two even thresholds: (0.3, 0.8) and (0.8, 0.9).

### Subtask 2 – Unmask the Gold (80 points)

The AI’s grandiose property tales hide the true value of each home.

**Task:** Predict the sale price for each property using any combination of data, despite noise and exaggeration.
The evaluation metric for this subject is the **Coefficient of Determination (R²)**.
* Full points (80) are awarded for $\text{R²} \geq 0.79$. 
* Zero points are awarded for $\text{R²} < 0.2$ . 
* Scores are linearly scaled between two even thresholds: (0.2, 0.6) and (0.6, 0.79).

## Output Format

The output file (`submission.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID               | answer                                                   |
| --------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1 or 2    | IDs from the test dataset | answer for each ID |

You must only send one `.csv` file for all three subtasks.

## Scoring

The total score is the sum of points obtained from Subtask 1 and Subtask 2. Each subtask is evaluated independently using its corresponding metric.

### Subtask 1 (Harbor Crews Discovery)

| Metric  | Points                      |
| ---------- | --------------------------- |
|$\text{ARI} \geq 0.90$| 20 points |
|$0.80 \leq \text{ARI} < 0.90$ | Linear normalization between 10 and 20 points |
|$0.30 \leq \text{ARI} < 0.80$ | Linear normalization between 0 and 10 points |
|$\text{ARI} < 0.30$ | 0 points |

### Subtask 2 (Unmask the Gold)

| Metric  | Points                      |
| ---------- | --------------------------- |
|$\text{R²} \geq 0.79$| 80 points |
|$0.60 \leq \text{R²} < 0.79$ | Linear normalization between 40 and 80 points |
|$0.20 \leq \text{R²} < 0.60$ | Linear normalization between 0 and 40 points |
|$\text{R²} < 0.20$ | 0 points |

tasks.name: Algolymp PreOJIA XI-XII — SETI Signal Filtering and Classification
tasks.description: Табличный SETI-датасет с 50 спектральными фичами и шумными «ghost echoes», валидные сигналы лежат на низкоразмерных многообразиях. Три подзадачи: бинарная фильтрация шум/сигнал (F1, порог 0.98), регрессия Contact Probability 0–100 (MSE ≤ 2.5) и классификация источника (F1 ≥ 0.92).
tasks.full_description:
The search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI) involves isolating structured patterns from cosmic background radiation. Automated analysis of spectral data is essential for distinguishing potential technosignatures from random interference and classifying their origins. This task involves processing high-dimensional radio telescope data from the Kepler Sector. Participants must develop models to filter noise, quantify signal probability, and classify the source of the transmission.

## Dataset Description

The dataset consists of thousands of candidate signal samples. Each data point is composed of **50 spectral features** representing frequency modulation, amplitude shifts, and temporal decay. The data contains "Ghost Echoes"—high-dimensional random radiation spikes that mimic structure but lack coherence. Notably, valid transmissions do not occupy the entire 50-dimensional spectrum; they reside on specific, lower-dimensional manifolds (hypersurfaces) embedded within the noise.

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - Signal Filtering (30 points)

Build a binary classifier to separate "Space Noise" from "Candidate Signals."

* **0:** Space Noise (Cosmic background radiation, sensor glitches). 
* **1:** Candidate Signals (Potentially structured data).

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the **F1 Score**.

* Full points (30) are awarded for $\text{F1} \geq 0.98$. 
* Zero points are awarded for $\text{F1} < 0.7$ . 
* Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

### Subtask 2 - Probability Assessment (30 points)

Predict the "Contact Probability," representing the authenticity of the signal. The target is a continuous value ranging from 0.0 (likely a pulsar/natural phenomenon) to 100.0 (definite communication attempt).

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**.

* Full points (30) are awarded for $\text{MSE} \leq 2.5$ . 
* Zero points are awarded for $\text{MSE} \geq 5.0$. 
* Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

### Subtask 3 - Source Origin Classification (40 points)

Classify all signals into one of two distinct sources based on their structural patterns.

* **0:** Scout Beacons or Space Noise (Passive, automated navigational buoys). 
* **1:** Active Transmissions (Complex, intentional communication).

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the **F1 Score**.

* Full points (40) are awarded for $\text{F1} \geq 0.92$. 
* Zero points are awarded for $\text{F1} < 0.66$ . 
* Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

## Output Format

The output file (`submission.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| --- | --- | --- | 
| represents the subtask id (1, 2, or 3) | the IDs from the test data | answer for each ID |

You must only send one `.csv` file for all three subtasks.

## Scoring

Total scoring is the sum of the points obtained in Subtasks 1, 2, and 3.

### Subtask 1 (Signal Filtering)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
|$\text{F1} \geq 0.98$| 30 points |
|$0.7 \leq \text{F1} < 0.98$ | Linear normalization between 0 and 30 points | 
|$\text{F1} < 0.7$ | 0 points |

### Subtask 2 (Probability Assessment)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
|$\text{MSE} \leq 2.5$| 30 points |
|$2.5 < \text{MSE} < 5.0$| Linear normalization between 0 and 30 points | 
|$\text{MSE} \geq 5.0$| 0 points |

### Subtask 3 (Source Origin Classification)

| Metric | Score |
| --- | --- | 
|$\text{F1} \geq 0.92$| 40 points |
|$0.66 \leq \text{F1} < 0.92$ | Linear normalization between 0 and 40 points | 
|$\text{F1} < 0.66$ | 0 points |

tasks.name: Algolymp PreOJIA XI-XII — Lunar Crater Counting and Age
tasks.description: Компьютерное зрение по 1000 синтетическим 128x128 grayscale изображениям лунной поверхности (700 train / 300 test). Subtask 1 — регрессия числа кратеров (3–8) по изображению, метрика MAE ≤ 1.0; Subtask 2 — оценка глобального возраста поверхности 1.0–5.0 (степень эрозии), метрика MAE ≤ 0.3.
tasks.full_description:
The lunar surface serves as a chronological record of the solar system's history. Automated analysis of impact craters is essential for planetary geology, specifically for dating surface units and understanding bombardment rates. This task involves processing synthetic 128x128 grayscale images that simulate lunar terrain under varying lighting conditions. Participants must develop models to identify the frequency of craters and estimate the degree of surface erosion, which serves as a proxy for geological age.

## Dataset Description

The dataset consists of 1000 synthetic images (700 for training, 300 for testing). Each image represents a 128x128 top-down view of a lunar-like surface. The terrain is generated using fractal noise, and crater profiles are modeled with specific geomorphological features including rims, floors, and walls. The visual appearance is influenced by simulated sun azimuth and elevation. Labels include the exact number of craters and a global age parameter (1.0 to 5.0) that dictates the level of erosion and structural degradation of the craters.

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - Crater Counting (20 points)

Predict the total number of distinct craters present in each image. In this dataset, images contain between 3 and 8 craters. The output must be an integer.

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the Mean Absolute Error (MAE). 
- Full points (20) are awarded for $\text{MAE} \leq 1.0$.
- Zero points are awarded for $\text{MAE} \geq 4.0$.
- Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

### Subtask 2 - Age Estimation (80 points)

Estimate the global age of the surface unit. The age is a normalized floating-point value ranging from 1.0 (young/sharp features) to 5.0 (old/highly eroded features). Age affects the rim height, depth, and sharpness of the crater profiles, as well as the surface noise.

The evaluation metric for this subtask is the Mean Absolute Error (MAE).
- Full points (80) are awarded for $\text{MAE} \leq 0.3$.
- Zero points are awarded for $\text{MAE} \geq 1.5$.
- Scores between these thresholds are linearly scaled.

## Output Format

The output file (`submission.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| ---------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ------------------ |
| represents the subtask id (1 or 2) | the IDs from the test data | answer for each ID |

You must only send one `.csv` file for both subtasks.

## Scoring

Total scoring is the sum of the points obtained in Subtask 1 and Subtask 2.

### Subtask 1 (Crater Counting)
| Metric | Score |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| $\text{MAE} \leq 1.0$ | 20 points |
| $1.0 < \text{MAE} < 4.0$ | Linear normalization between 20 and 0 points |
| $\text{MAE} \geq 4.0$ | 0 points |

### Subtask 2 (Age Estimation)

| Metric | Score |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| $\text{MAE} \leq 0.3$ | 80 points |
| $0.3 < \text{MAE} < 1.5$ | Linear normalization between 80 and 0 points |
| $\text{MAE} \geq 1.5$ | 0 points |

tasks.name: CEOAI 2026 - Practice Round 1 — Atmospheric Star Flux Calibration
tasks.description: Мультимодальная регрессия истинного звёздного потока по изображению (128x128, одна звезда на кадр) и метеометаданным (airmass, fried parameter). 1000 train + 300 test изображений; PSF зависит от атмосферных параметров. Нужно совместить CNN-фичи картинки с табличной метой и предсказать экспоненциальный target flux.
tasks.full_description:
**Central European Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence**

Ground-based astronomy faces a fundamental challenge: the Earth's atmosphere. While space telescopes enjoy a pristine view of the cosmos, terrestrial observatories must contend with turbulent air masses that distort incoming light. This turbulence causes stars to twinkle, a phenomenon known as scintillation, and blurs celestial images, spreading the light of a point source over a larger area. This degradation limits the precision with which astronomers can measure the brightness of stars.

In this task, you act as a data scientist for the Star Observatory conducting a survey to measure the intrinsic brightness (flux) of thousands of stars. Your measurements are heavily affected by varying atmospheric conditions. The amount of atmosphere the light passes through (airmass) and the severity of atmospheric turbulence (seeing) change constantly throughout the night.

Your goal is to build a machine learning model that recovers the **calibrated target flux** of a star. You are provided with the captured image of the star and meteorological metadata recorded at the moment of observation. You must learn the complex, non-linear relationship between atmospheric parameters, the distorted image, and the true underlying flux.

### Physics Background

In astronomy, brightness is described by **Flux** $ F $, representing energy received per unit area per unit time. Historically, brightness is denoted by **Magnitude** $ m $, a logarithmic scale related by:

$$ m = -2.5 \log_{10}(F) + C $$

where $ C $ is a zero-point constant. Conversely, flux is proportional to $ 10^{-0.4m} $ (**clarification**: the target flux is exponential).

Atmospheric state is defined by two primary values:
- **Airmass $ X $**: Amount of air light passes through. $ X=1 $ at zenith, increasing toward the horizon. More airmass means more extinction.
- **Fried Parameter $ r_0 $**: Measures "seeing" quality. Higher $ r_0 $ indicates calmer air and better images; lower $ r_0 $ means more blurring.

**Image Formation**: Ideally a star is a point source, but diffraction and turbulence spread light into a **Point Spread Function (PSF)**. The observed image $ I(x,y) $ is modeled as:

$$ I(x,y) = (S * \text{PSF}_{r_0, X})(x,y) + N $$

where $ S $ is the true star flux and $ N $ is background noise. PSF shape depends on $ r_0 $ and $ X $, with additional Poisson and readout noise from the sensor. 

You **don't need to use these formulas** in your solution, the takeaway point is that the image is a function of the star flux, atmospheric state and noise - you need the **metadata** for the best flux predictions.

## Dataset Description

The dataset comprises 1,000 training images and 300 test images, each $ 128 \times 128 $ pixels with precisely one star per image. Training data includes true target flux values.

**Provided files:**
- `train_images/00000.png` through `00999.png`: Training images
- `train.csv`: Metadata with `image_id`, `fried_parameter`, `airmass`, `target_flux`
- `test_images/00000.png` through `00299.png`: Test images
- `test.csv`: Test metadata with `image_id` only

## Subtasks

### Subtask 1 - Star Center Prediction (20 points)

Compute the center coordinates of each star within its image. The answer must be a tuple with 2 elements, e.g., `(69, 66)`.

**Evaluation Metric:** Mean Absolute Error (MAE) calculated as the average Manhattan distance between predicted and true centers.

**Scoring ranges:**
- MAE $ < $ 5: 20 points
- MAE = 5 to 10: 10 to 0 points (linear interpolation)
- MAE $ > $ 10: 0 points

### Subtask 2 - Flux Prediction (80 points)

Predict the true flux (continuous value) of each star given its image and atmospheric parameters. The test set contains only images.

**Evaluation Metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) between predicted and true flux values.

**Scoring ranges:**
- RMSE $ \leq $ 280: 80 points
- RMSE = 280 to 400: 80 to 40 points (linear interpolation)
- RMSE = 400 to 880: 40 to 0 points (linear interpolation)
- RMSE $ \geq $ 880: 0 points

## Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain exactly 600 samples with 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| --------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| 1 or 2 | Image filename (e.g., `00001.png`) | Tuple `(x, y)` for subtask 1, float for subtask 2 |

**Example submission:**

```csv
subtaskID,datapointID,answer
1,00001.png,"(69.0, 66.0)"
1,00002.png,"(70.5, 68.0)"
2,00001.png,1542.3
2,00002.png,893.7
...
```

## Scoring

### Subtask 1

| Metric | Score |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| MAE $ < $ 5 | 20 points |
| 5 $ \leq $ MAE $ \leq $ 10 | Linear interpolation from 10 to 0 points |
| MAE $ > $ 10 | 0 points |

### Subtask 2

| Metric | Score |
| ---------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| RMSE $ \leq $ 280 | 80 points |
| 280 $ < $ RMSE $ \leq $ 400 | Linear interpolation from 80 to 40 points |
| 400 $ < $ RMSE $ \leq $ 880 | Linear interpolation from 40 to 0 points |
| RMSE $ > $ 880 | 0 points |

**Total Score:** Sum of points from both subtasks (maximum 100 points).

## Constraints

- Maximum 45 minutes notebook runtime (training + inference) on free 16GB GPU (T4 on Google Colab or P100 on Kaggle)
- No usage of LLMs for code writing or submission generation
- No pre-trained models (ResNet, ViT, etc.)
- Forbidden to use any data not included in the provided dataset

tasks.name: CEOAI 2026 - Practice Round 1 — Offline RL Value Estimation
tasks.description: Offline Reinforcement Learning: оценить V*(s) для запрошенных состояний MDP по статическому логу ~6000 переходов (current_state, action, reward, next_state) с шумом, gamma=0.99. Действия и состояния дискретны; динамика и награды стохастичны. Требуется off-policy оценка оптимальной политики без взаимодействия со средой.
tasks.full_description:
**Central European Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence** ## 1. The Mission > *"Probability is not a constant. Here, it is a predator."* Deep within the Cronus Hyper-Grid, **Sector 7** has destabilized. The topology of the network has fractured into a stochastic mess of quantum states. We need to extract a stranded kernel from the core, but active navigation is impossible; the radiation lag is too high for real-time control. You have been tasked with programming the **Navigator Oracle**. Since we cannot simulate the environment without collapsing the wave function, your only source of information is the **"Black Box" Flight Log** recovered from a previous, failed reconnaissance drone. This drone moved blindly through the sector. It recorded its transitions, its actions, and the energy fluctuations (rewards) it encountered. It did not survive. > **Note:** For a visual representation of the topology and a baseline implementation, please refer to the **PDF** or the **Jupyter Notebook (`.ipynb`)** included in the starter kit. **Your Objective:** Using *only* this static, historical dataset, you must determine the **True Optimal Value** of specific starting states. ## 2. The Environment Sector 7 is modeled as a **Stochastic Markov Decision Process (MDP)**: $\mathcal{M} = \langle S, A, P, R, \gamma \rangle$. * **States ($S$):** A discrete set of nodes, identified by integers $0 \dots N-1$. * **Actions ($A$):** A discrete set of 4 navigational impulses, identified by integers $0 \dots 3$. * **Dynamics ($P$):** The transition function $P(s' | s, a)$ is unknown and highly stochastic. * **Reward ($R$):** The reward function is noisy. * **Discount ($\gamma$):** The temporal discount factor is exactly **0.99**. Your task is to estimate the value of the optimal policy $\pi^*$ for a set of query states: $$ V^*(s) = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t \mid s_0 = s \right] $$ ## 3. Dataset Description You are provided with three files. ### File 1: `sector_logs.csv` This file contains approximately **6,000** recorded transitions from the failed drone. The data is non-sequential (shuffled) and sparse relative to the state space. ```csv current_state,action,reward,next_state 42,0,-1.05,43 105,2,300.0,999 105,2,-100.0,0 ... ``` *Note: As observed in the snippet, the same state-action pair $(105, 2)$ resulted in vastly different outcomes. The log contains the raw history, not the averages. Rewards may contain sensor noise.* ### File 2: `query_states.csv` This file lists the states for which the Central Command requires value estimates. ```csv id,state_id 0,14 1,256 2,0 ... ``` ### File 3: `env.py` A Python definition of the environment class. * It defines the valid state space size, action space, and API structure. * It contains a `step()` method allowing for local simulation. ## 4. Evaluation & Scoring You will be evaluated against a **Ground Truth Solver** that has access to the perfect underlying probability matrix of the simulation. We compute the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** between your predicted values $\hat{V}$ and the true optimal values $V^*$: $$ \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} (\hat{V}(s_i) - V^*(s_i))^2 $$ Your score is calculated based on two constants: * $\text{MSE}_{\text{baseline}} = \mathbf{4000.0}$ (Upper Bound) * $\text{MSE}_{\text{optimal}} = \mathbf{205.0}$ (Lower Bound) The scoring follows a **quartic curve**: $$ \text{Points} = 100 \times \left( \frac{\text{MSE}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{MSE}_{\text{yours}}}{\text{MSE}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{MSE}_{\text{optimal}}} \right)^4 $$ **Constraints:** * If $\text{MSE}_{\text{yours}} \ge \text{MSE}_{\text{baseline}}$, the term becomes negative (or zero) and you receive **0 points**. * If $\text{MSE}_{\text{yours}} \le \text{MSE}_{\text{optimal}}$, the term exceeds 1 and is capped at **100 points**. **Scoring Note:** The Baseline Threshold corresponds to the performance of a Naive Agent that blindly trusts the sparse data statistics or the provided simulation code. To score points, you must significantly outperform these heuristic approaches. ## 5. Submission Format Submit a single CSV file named `predictions.csv` containing exactly the number of rows as `query_states.csv`. ```csv subtaskID,datapointID,answer 1,0,24.5012 1,1,-5.100 1,2,0.000 ... ``` * `subtaskID`: One for all rows. * `datapointID`: Matches the `id` from `query_states.csv`. * `answer`: A float representing your estimate of $V^*(s)$. ## 6. Constraints * **Inference Constraint:** Your code should be able to produce the solution in under **30 minutes** on a standard single-core CPU. * **External Data:** Strictly prohibited.
The void listens. Calculate wisely.

tasks.name: CEOAI 2026 - Practice Round 1 — Hyperdimensional Signal Reconstruction
tasks.description: Реконструкция латентного состояния 5-мерных «гипер-сущностей» по искажённой проекции с неравномерным временным шагом и непрерывным фоновым шумом. По сути — denoising и нелинейная регрессия с временного ряда на исходные физические параметры объекта на псевдо-римановом многообразии.
tasks.full_description:
**Central European Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence**

## Preamble:  
  
**"We thought the void was silent. We were wrong. It is not that the universe does not speak; it is that our ears were never designed to hear the frequency of its screaming."**  
— *Dr. Aris Thorne, Lead Astrometric Specialist, Deep Space Interferometry Array (DSIA)*  
  
### Context  
At the edge of the known sector lies X-99, a Supermassive Singularity that defies the standard model of cosmic evolution. It is not merely a gravity well; it is a wound in the skin of reality. For the past decade, the Deep Space Interferometry Array (DSIA) has orbited this abyss, listening to the static of the accretion disk, searching for patterns in the chaos.  
  
Last week, the patterns changed.  
  
The singularity has entered an unstable resonance phase. The event horizon is no longer a static boundary but a shifting, semi-permeable membrane. Through this membrane, something is pushing back. We are detecting high-dimensional geometric anomalies—structures that possess mass and trajectory but defy Euclidean description. We call them "Leviathan-Class" hyper-entities.  
  
They exist natively in a five-dimensional bulk ($\mathbb{R}^5$), but as they brush against our four-dimensional brane, they cast shadows. These shadows are not visual; they are gravitational, distorting the very flow of time and space around our sensors.  
  
### The Challenge  
You are the architect of the KRAKEN Protocol, an advanced AI system designed to interpret these impossible signals. The raw data streaming from the DSIA is fragmented, warped by relativistic time dilation, and corrupted by the quantum noise of Hawking radiation. Human intuition fails here. The geometry is non-orientable, timelines are looped, and the logic is alien.   
  
Only a machine learning model can stare into this abyss and reconstruct the shape of the things rising from the dark.    
  
## Part I: Mathematical Formulation  
  
**Intuitive Explanation:**  
In simple terms, imagine trying to reconstruct the exact shape and trajectory of a complex 3D object just by looking at its distorted 2D shadow cast on an uneven, moving surface. You do not have direct access to the original object. The data you receive is a lower-dimensional projection that has been heavily distorted: the timeline is warped (meaning the intervals between sensor readings are not constant), and the signal is corrupted by continuous, fluctuating background noise. Your objective is to take this noisy, warped projection and mathematically map it back to the original properties of the object.  
  
**More formally**, the data generation process and the physical state of the entities are defined by the following framework:  
  
The raw data exists on a pseudo-Riemannian manifold $\mathcal{M}$ with a shifting metric tensor $g_{\mu\nu}$. Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^5$ denote the latent state of the entity. As it moves, the entity undergoes a continuous transformation $\mathcal{T} \in SO(1,4)$, corresponding to the de Sitter group acting on a hyperboloid bundle.   
  
The sensor output, $S_{obs}$, is a lossy projection $\Pi: \mathbb{R}^5 \to \mathbb{C}^2$ integrated over a proper time interval $\tau$. Therefore, the signal streaming to your model is defined by the integral transform over the manifold:  
  
$$ S_{obs}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{\Delta \tau} \sqrt{-g} \left[ \mathcal{F}(\Psi(x^\mu)) \cdot e^{-i \omega t} + \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \mathcal{L}_{\text{interaction}}^{(n)} \right] dt + \eta(t) $$  
  
**Where:**  
* $g = \det(g_{\mu\nu})$ is the determinant of the metric tensor, representing the curvature of local space.  
* $\mathcal{F}: L^2(\mathbb{R}) \to L^2(\mathbb{R})$ is the Fourier transform operator acting on the entity's wavefunction $\Psi$.  
* $\mathcal{L}_{\text{interaction}}$ represents the Lagrangian density of the holographic interference.  
* $\eta(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2(t))$ represents non-Gaussian shot noise induced by Hawking radiation, where variance $\sigma^2(t)$ is time-dependent.  
  
  
## Part II: Telemetry & Dataset Specification  
**WARNING:** The dataset contains raw interferometric data. Caution is advised when parsing the dimensional arrays.  
  
### 1. The Holographic Biopsies (Slices)  
These arrays represent 2D slices of the 5D object as it passes through our sensor array.  
* **Files:** `train_slices.npy`, `test_slices.npy`  
* **Dimensions:** $(N, 3, 128, 128)$  
* **Channels (Dimensional properties):**  
 1. **Real component ($\Re$):** Gravimetric potential (The "weight" of the shadow).  
 2. **Imaginary component ($\Im$):** Frame-dragging angular momentum (The "spin" of space itself).  
 3. **Spin-tensor ($\sigma$):** Normalized vorticity field (The turbulence of the void).  
  
### 2. The Song of the Accretion Disk (Echoes)  
Gravitational wave readings capturing the strain on spacetime.  
* **Files:** `train_echoes.npy`, `test_echoes.npy`  
* **Dimensions:** $(N, 1024, 2)$  
* **Signal Structure:** $h(t) = [h_+, h_\times]$ (Plus and Cross polarizations).  
* **Corruption:** The series contains "Quantum Gaps"—intervals where the sensor recorded Absolute Zero, indicating a momentary erasure of existence.  
  
### 3. The Syntax of Madness (Glyphs)  
Encoded telemetry data that appears to follow an alien, recursive grammar.  
* **Files:** `train_glyphs.csv`, `test_glyphs.csv`  
* **Parsing Constraint:** Grammar interpretation depends on the eigenvalues of Image Channel 0 (Gravimetric potential). *(See Appendix B)*  
  
### 4. Ground Truth  
* **File:** `train_targets.csv` (Contains the target labels for all three subtasks).  
  
## Part III: The Mission Objectives  
  
### Subtask 1: Geodesic Rectification (30 pts)  
> **"Time is a straight line only to those with limited perspective."**  
  
The immense gravity of the Leviathan warps the sensor timestamps. You must predict the 10 spline coefficients $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{10}$ of the warping function $\Omega(t)$. This acts as a map, restoring the twisted signal to a canonical, linear reference frame.  
* **Output:** 10 Spline Coefficients (float).  
* **Metric:** **MSE** (Minimize the difference between your predicted coefficients and the true parameters).  
* **Scoring:** 30 pts for MSE $\le$ 0.00085 | 0 pts for MSE $\ge$ 0.020.  
  
### Subtask 2: Non-Euclidean Topological Classification (35 pts)  
> **"It has no face, yet it stares at you. It has no shape, yet it casts a shadow."**  
  
Predict the topological class $y$ of the entity based on its homological features (holes, voids, twists).  
* **Stable Forms:** Classes $y \in \{0, \dots, 49\}$. These are previously cataloged geometries.  
* **Abyssal Forms:** If the entity does not match known geometries, it is an unprecedented anomaly and must be labeled as $y = -1$.  
* **Output:** A single integer (0-49, or -1).  
* **Metric:** **Macro F1 with Abyssal Penalty** *(See Appendix A)*.  
* **Scoring:** 35 pts for F1 $\ge$ 0.85 | 0 pts for F1 $\le$ 0.55.  
  
### Subtask 3: The Heisenberg Stability Limit (35 pts)  
> **"The fabric is tearing. We need to know when to run."**  
  
Predict the stability index $s \in [0, 1]$ of the local sector.   
* $s=0$: Perfect vacuum stability.  
* $s=1$: Total collapse of the local manifold (Event Horizon breach).  
Your prediction dictates whether the station adjusts its shields or initiates immediate evacuation.  
* **Output:** Stability index $s$ (float).  
* **Metric:** **RMSE** (Lower is better).  
* **Scoring:** 35 pts for RMSE $\le$ 0.035 | 0 pts for RMSE $\ge$ 0.100.  
  
*(Note: Scores for all subtasks are calculated using linear interpolation between the 0 pt threshold and the Maximum pt threshold).*  
  
## Part IV: Submission Format  
Your submission must be a single CSV file containing your model's predictions. The order of rows does not matter, but every test datapoint must be present.  
  
```csv  
subtaskID,datapointID,answer  
1,test_00000,"0.123;0.456;0.789;...;0.999"  
2,test_00000,-1  
3,test_00000,0.884  
...  
```  
  
**Formatting Requirements:**  
* **Subtask 1:** 10 float values separated by semicolons (`;`) enclosed in double quotes.  
* **Subtask 2:** A single integer (0-49 or -1).  
* **Subtask 3:** A single float value between 0.0 and 1.0.  
  
  
## Appendix A: Metric Details (Macro F1 with Penalty)  
Due to the catastrophic threat posed by Abyssal Entities, the standard F1 score is heavily modified to punish false alarms regarding the $y = -1$ class.  
  
For each class $c$:  
1. **Precision:**  
 $$ \text{Precision}_c = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FP'_c} $$  
 * **Penalty rule:** If $c = -1$ (Abyssal): $FP'_c = 2 \times FP_c$. For all other stable classes ($c \neq -1$), $FP'_c = FP_c$.  
  
2. **Recall:**  
 $$ \text{Recall}_c = \frac{TP_c}{TP_c + FN_c} $$  
  
3. **F1 Score:**  
 $$ F1_c = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision}_c \cdot \text{Recall}_c}{\text{Precision}_c + \text{Recall}_c} $$  
  
4. **Macro F1:**  
 $$ \text{Macro F1} = \frac{1}{|C|} \sum_{c \in C} F1_c $$  
  
  
## Appendix B: Technical Context  
  
**Warping Function Mechanics (Subtask 1)**  
The spline coefficients parameterize $\beta(t)$ in the relativistic transformation:  
$$ \beta = \frac{v}{c}, \quad t' = \frac{t - vx}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^2}} $$  
  
**Glyph Interpretation Mechanics (Telemetry)**  
When processing the `glyphs.csv` telemetry sequences, note the following recursive operators:  
* $\nabla$: Differentiates the next token in the sequence.  
* $\int$: Integrates the next token.  
* $\Phi$: Check the phase variance $\bar{\phi}$ derived from the Holographic Biopsy slices. If $\bar{\phi} > \frac{\pi}{2}$, the subsequent syntax must be parsed in reverse.

tasks.name: Cram School Beginner 1 — Fitness Status Prediction
tasks.description: Учебная табличная классификация is_fit (0/1) по антропометрии, ЧСС, давлению, сну, питанию, активности, курению и полу. Subtask 1 — детерминированный расчёт BMI и порог >25 (all-or-nothing); Subtask 2 — обучение классификатора, метрика F1 (порог 0.76 на полный балл). Есть пропуски в sleep_hours.
tasks.full_description:
Nowadays, knowing the fitness status of individuals is important for prevention and personalized recommendations. The goal of this problem is to develop a machine learning model that predicts whether a person is fit (is_fit = 1) or not (is_fit = 0), based on the given data.

## Dataset Description

The training dataset (train_data.csv) contains the following columns:

**id** (natural number): unique identifier of the person.

**age** : age of the person.

**height_cm** : height in centimeters.

**weight_kg** : weight in kilograms.

**heart_ratec**: average resting heart rate.

**blood_pressure** : average blood pressure.

**sleep_hours** : number of hours of sleep per day (may be missing for some records).

**nutrition_quality** : nutrition quality score.

**activity_index** : activity level score.

**smokes** : smoking habit (categorical, may be yes/no or 0/1).

**gender** : sex (M or F).

**is_fit** : binary variable (1 – the person is fit, 0 – the person is not fit).

The test dataset (test_data.csv) contains the same features, but without the is_fit column.

## Subtasks

- **Subtask 1**:

On the test dataset, calculate the Body Mass Index (BMI):
**BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2**
and classify each person into one of the categories:

**answer = 1** if BMI > 25

**answer = 0** otherwise

- **Subtask 2** :

On the test dataset, use a classification model trained on the training data to predict whether a person is fit (is_fit = 1) or not (is_fit = 0).

## Expected Result

A file (output.csv) that includes the following 3 columns:

subtaskID – the subtask number (1 or 2).

datapointID – which refers to the id column from test_data.csv.

answer – the corresponding answer for the datapoint for the given subtask (is_overweight or is_fit).


## Scoring strategy

**Subtask1**:

For Subtask 1, the evaluation is binary and all-or-nothing.

**Correct** → 30 points

**Incorrect** → 3 points

**Subtask2**

Scoring for the second subtask is done using the f1 metric.

**f1_score > 0.76** : 70 / 70 points

**0.65 < f1_score < 0.76** : 25 - 60 / 70 points

**35 < f1_score < 65** : 15 - 25 / 70 points

**25 < f1_score < 35** : 5 - 15 / 70 points

**f1_score < 25** : 0 / 70 points

tasks.name: Cram School Beginner 1 — Powerlifting Bench Press Regression
tasks.description: Табличная задача по данным пауэрлифтинга (playerId, Sex, Equipment, Age, BodyweightKg, BestSquatKg, BestDeadliftKg, BestBenchKg). Подзадача 1 — посчитать число атлетов с Total/BodyweightKg > 5; подзадача 2 — регрессия для предсказания BestBenchKg. Рекомендуется только удалять NaN, остальное препроцессить.
tasks.full_description:
Athletes’ performances in powerlifting depend on factors such as body weight, age, sex, and type of equipment used. The goal of this task is to develop a machine learning model that estimates athletes’ performance, both through simple rules and predictive models.

## Dataset Description
The training dataset (**train_data.csv**) contains the following columns:

- **playerId** (integer): unique identifier of the athlete.

- **Name**: the athlete’s name.

- **Sex**: the athlete’s sex (M/F).

- **Equipment**: type of equipment used.

- **Age**: the athlete’s age.

- **BodyweightKg**: body weight in kilograms.

- **BestSquatKg**: best squat performance (kg).

- **BestDeadliftKg**: best deadlift performance (kg).

- **BestBenchKg**: best bench press performance (kg) – the target variable for Subtask 2.

The test dataset (**test_data.csv**) contains the same features, but without the BestBenchKg column.

- **! It is recommended that only the Nan values be removed and the rest preprocessed !**

## Subtasks

- **Subtask 1:**
On the training dataset, calculate the powerlifting total:

Total=BestSquatKg+BestDeadliftKg+BestBenchKg

answer = How many athletes have Total / BodyWeightKg > 5

- **Subtask 2:**
Build a regression model trained on the training dataset to predict BestBenchKg (bench press performance) based on the other features.

## Expected Output
An output.csv file that includes the following 3 columns:

- **subtaskID** – represents the subtask number (1 or 2).

- **datapointID** – refers to the playerId column from **test_data.csv**.

- **answer** – the corresponding answer for that datapoint for the respective subtask (binary value for Subtask 1, numeric value for Subtask 2).

tasks.name: Christmas Training Special — Mnemon Dreams Multimodal Decoding
tasks.description: Мультимодальная задача декодирования "Mnemon-Dreams": непрерывный поток символов Oneiric Language плюс абстрактные голограммы. Жёсткие ограничения: запрещены предобученные веса (BERT, ResNet и т.п.) и готовые токенизаторы, лимит обучаемых параметров 60M, всё обучается с нуля. Подзадача 1 — эвристическая классификация фрагментов.
tasks.full_description: # IMPORTANT The use of **large language models (LLMs) designed for open-ended or instruction-based natural language generation** (e.g., chat-based or conversational models such as GPT-4, Claude, Llama) is strictly **forbidden**. This prohibition **explicitly includes** importing, loading, calling, or otherwise using such models in the environment (local or remote), using hosted LLM APIs, or leveraging **any** generative or pre-trained system **Furthermore**, to ensure the evaluation focuses on architectural ingenuity rather than the use of pre-existing foundation models, the following **Strict Technical Constraints** apply to **all parts** of the solution: 1. **No Pre-trained Weights:** The use of **any** pre-trained model weights is prohibited. You may **not** use models like **BERT, T5, BART, RoBERTa, ResNet, or VGG** initialized with weights from external sources (e.g., HuggingFace `from_pretrained`).
