tasks.name: Slovak OAI 2026 — Digit vs Letter Anomaly Detection with VAE
tasks.description: Среди изображений цифр в датасете подмешаны буквы — необходимо определить, какие изображения являются буквами (аномалиями), используя предоставленный вариационный автоэнкодер цифр. Подзадачи включают расчёт доли бинарных изображений, ошибку реконструкции, бинарную классификацию буква/цифра и распознавание самих букв (A–Z). Классическая задача anomaly detection через reconstruction error автоэнкодера.
tasks.full_description:
# English Version

When the royal ball was being prepared, the stepmother said:
"Cinderella, I have mixed data samples of letters into the data samples of written digits. If you sort them out within two minutes, you may come with us."
The girl went out the back door into the garden and called:
"Doves, turtledoves, and all the little birds under the sky, fly to me and help me sort:
leave the digits be and take the letters in your beaks."
Then two doves flew to the window, followed by turtledoves, and finally all the birds of the sky gathered. They settled around the stepmother's mixture and began picking out the letters with their beaks. Since they already had access to an autoencoder of the stepmother's digits, before two minutes had passed, everything was sorted. Lucky thing the stepmother hadn't mixed in letters like O, which would have been indistinguishable from digits like 0.

### Dataset

Contains 10,000 images in `PNG` format numbered from 0000 to 9999.

### Starter Kit

Contains a variational encoder and decoder of the stepmother's digits. How to use them should be clear from the accompanying code in `anomaly.py`.

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 - Binariness (5 points)

Determine what percentage of images in the stepmother's mixture are binary (all their pixels take only one of the two extreme values of the range). Round the value to two decimal places.

Scoring: 5 points for an exact result, one point deducted for each percentage point of difference. (Negative points are not awarded.)

#### Subtask 2 - Reconstruction (10 points)

Compute the maximum reconstruction error of the provided autoencoder applied to the symbols in the stepmother's mixture. The error for a symbol (in which pixels have values from 0 to 1) is computed as the mean of the squared differences between the values of corresponding pixels in the input and output of the autoencoder. Report it to five decimal places.

Scoring: 10 points for an exact result, one point deducted for each percent of relative error.

#### Subtask 3 - Where Are the Letters (60 points)

Determine for each element of the stepmother's mixture whether it is a digit (category 0) or a letter (category 1).

Scoring: For each correctly identified letter you receive a proportional share of 60 points. For each digit that you classify as a letter, you lose one point. (Negative points are not awarded.)

#### Subtask 4 - Number of Letters (5 points)

Report the number of letters in the stepmother's mixture.

Scoring: Up to 5 points depending on the deviation from the true count; some points can be earned even for an estimate, so even if you do not solve Subtask 3, it is worth providing an answer.

#### Subtask 5 - What Are the Letters (20 points)

Determine for each letter in the stepmother's mixture what letter it is (categories 'A' ... 'Z'). For digits, use the category space (' ').

Scoring: Up to 20 points depending on the length of the longest common subsequence of letters with the correct answer.

### Out

tasks.name: Slovak OAI 2026 — Slovak LLM-Generated Text Detection
tasks.description: Операция Parrot: анализ словацких текстов для отличия человеческих от сгенерированных LLM (GPT-5.4, Claude, Gemini, Gemma). Подзадачи включают статистический анализ корпуса, бинарную классификацию human vs machine, атрибуцию модели-источника и детекцию перефразированных машинных текстов. Классическая NLP-задача на классификацию текстов.
tasks.full_description:
# English Version

A Slovak media watchdog has identified a worrying trend. In recent months, texts that nobody wrote have been appearing on news portals, in parliamentary summaries, and even on Wikipedia. Well, someone did write them — it just appears that someone was a machine. Large language models (LLMs) can already generate Slovak text that is nearly indistinguishable from human writing. And as it turns out, this is being widely abused.

The operation to detect these texts was given the code name **Parrot** — because after all, those [stochastic parrots](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3442188.3445922), and so...

Your task is to become an analyst for Operation Parrot. You will receive a dataset of Slovak texts — some written by humans, others generated by four different language models. Your tasks are to:

1. Analyze the dataset and determine its statistical properties
2. Distinguish human texts from machine-generated ones
3. Identify which model generated which text
4. Detect texts that were rewritten to appear human

---

## Task Overview

| Subtask | Name | Points | Description |
|----------|-------|------|-------|
| 1 | Exploration | 10 | Statistical analysis of the test dataset |
| 2 | Identification | 15 | Binary classification: human vs. machine-generated text |
| 3 | Attribution | 45 | Identifying the source model for machine-generated texts |
| 4 | Counterintelligence | 30 | Detection of paraphrased machine-generated texts |

**Common input:** Slovak texts from parliamentary speeches, news articles, and Wikipedia.

**Sources of human texts:**
- Slovak parliament (transcripts from NRSR)
- Slovak news articles
- Slovak Wikipedia

**Machine-generated texts were produced by four models:**
- GPT-5.4 (OpenAI)
- Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic)
- Gemini 3.1 Pro (Google)
- Gemma 4 31B (Google, open-source)

---

## Subtask 1 -- Exploration (10 points)

Compute the following statistics on the **entire test dataset** (file `test_s2.jsonl`). Each statistic is one row in the output CSV.

| Row | Statistic | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------|
| 0 | Percentage of machine-generated texts | Round to 2 decimal places |
| 1 | Total number of texts | Integer |
| 2 | Average length (words) | Round to 1 decimal place |
| 3 | Median length (words) | Round to 1 decimal place |
| 4 | Average length (characters) | Round to 1 decimal place |
| 5 | Average number of tokens | Use the tiktoken tokenizer `o200k_base`. Round to 1 decimal place |
| 6 | Average number of words per sentence | Round to 1 decimal place |
| 7 | Average word length (characters) | Round to 1 decimal place |
| 8 | Average type-token ratio | Unique words / total words. Round to 4 decimal places |
| 9 | Freq. of most common bigram | Average across texts. Round to 4 decimal places |
| 10 | Freq. of most common trigram | Average across texts. Round to 4 decimal places |
| 11 | Average number of punctuation marks | Per text. Round to 1 decimal place |
| 12 | Q1 length (words) | 25th per

tasks.name: Slovak OAI 2026 — Dry Bean Variety Tabular Classification
tasks.description: Классификация 7 сортов фасоли по 16 числовым признакам формы и размера зёрен, извлечённых из изображений (площадь, периметр, эксцентриситет, shape factors и др.). Требуется обучить модель классификации, например на основе решающих деревьев, на 9527 образцах. Типичная задача табличной классификации.
tasks.full_description:
# English Version
Imagine you have thousands of bean grains in front of you. At first glance, they may look very similar. For farmers, food manufacturers, or automated sorting lines, it is very important to be able to distinguish them quickly and reliably. In practice, image-based or geometric characteristics of objects obtained from cameras and sensors are often used for this purpose. Your task will be to train a model that, based on numerical data about the shape and size of grains, can classify 7 varieties of bean grains. You will demonstrate that even a simple model can uncover hidden patterns in data and reliably distinguish between very similar objects.

### Dataset description

In this task, you will work with a real dataset containing 9,527 samples (grains) of seven varieties of dry bean. For each grain, 16 numerical features are available, derived from image analysis of seed shape and size. The dataset was created from images captured by a high-resolution camera.

Information about the dataset attributes:

p.č.| Column name|Meaning
-|-|-
1| Area ($\texttt{A}$)| Area of the bean region, i.e., the number of pixels within its boundary
2|Perimeter ($\texttt{P}$)|Perimeter of the bean, defined as the length of its boundary
3|Major axis length ($\texttt{L}$)|Length of the major axis — the distance between the endpoints of the longest line that can be drawn through the bean
4|Minor axis length ($\texttt{l}$)|Length of the minor axis — the longest line that can be drawn through the bean perpendicular to the major axis
5|Aspect ratio ($\texttt{K}$)|Axis ratio $\frac{\texttt{L}}{\texttt{l}}$
6|Eccentricity ($\texttt{Ec}$)|Eccentricity of the ellipse that has the same moments as the given region
7|Convex area ($\texttt{C}$)|Area of the smallest convex polygon that completely encloses the seed
8|Equivalent diameter ($\texttt{Ed}$)|Diameter of a circle having the same area as the bean seed region
9|Extent ($\texttt{Ex}$)|Ratio of the pixels in the bounding rectangle to the area of the bean
10|Solidity ($\texttt{S}$)|Ratio of the bean area to the area of its convex hull
11|Roundness ($\texttt{R}$)|$\frac{4\pi \cdot \texttt{A}}{\texttt{P}^2}$
12|Compactness ($\texttt{CO}$)|$\frac{\texttt{Ed}}{\texttt{L}}$
13|ShapeFactor1 ($\texttt{SF1}$)|Shape factor 1 — ratio of the major axis length to the area $\frac{\texttt{L}}{\texttt{A}}$
14|ShapeFactor2 ($\texttt{SF2}$)|Shape factor 2
15|ShapeFactor3 ($\texttt{SF3}$)|Shape factor 3 $\frac{\texttt{A}}{{\left(\frac{\texttt{L}}{2}\right)}^2 \cdot \pi}$
16|ShapeFactor4 ($\texttt{SF4}$)|Shape factor 4 $\frac{\texttt{A}}{{\frac{\texttt{L}}{2}}\cdot {\frac{\texttt{l}}{2}} \cdot \pi}$
17|Class|Bean variety (`SEKER`, `BARBUNYA`, `BOMBAY`, `CALI`, `DERMOSAN`, `HOROZ` and `SIRA`)

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 - Bean variety classification (100 points)

Your task is to train a classification model, for example using decision tree-based methods, on the provided training set. You may further split this training set for valida

tasks.name: AI Olympiad 2026 First Round (cpfed) — Cross-Lingual Code Retrieval Python to C++
tasks.description: Задача information retrieval: по запросу на Python найти соответствующий фрагмент кода на C++. Метрика — Mean Reciprocal Rank@1. Решается через эмбеддинги кода и поиск ближайших соседей (code retrieval / RAG-подобный сценарий).
tasks.full_description:
James and Aibek are brothers. They like to prank each other and they generally have a lot of fun together. One day, Jimmy - a deep learning engineer knowing only Python - has slept early and Aibek - a deep learning engineer knowing Python and C++ - decided to prank him and rewrote all of Jimmys code in C++. Now Jimmy is in shambles and cant find anything in his old code, because its all C++! Help create Jimmy a good retriever - one that can match C++ code given a Python code snippet

### Dataset Description

You are given dataset of pairs - Python source and C++ source - this is some logbook that Jimmy once stole from Aibek.

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1

Given a query $q$ your job is to find the relevant candidates that matches $q$ in another programming language.

### Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
represents the subtask id (Always 1 in your case) | the IDs from the test data | answer for each ID

You must only send one `.csv` file (see `sample_output.csv`)

### Scoring

#### Subtask 1

Metric that is going to be used to score your solution is Mean Reciprocal Rank@1

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{1}{|Q|} \sum_{i=1}^{|Q|} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_i}$$

Where:

- $|Q|$ is the total number of queries
- $\text{rank}_i$ is the rank position of the first relevant result for the $i$-th query

---

Джеймс и Айбек — братья. Они любят разыгрывать друг друга и в целом отлично проводят время вместе. Однажды Джимми — инженер глубокого обучения, знающий только Python — лёг спать рано, и Айбек — инженер глубокого обучения, знающий Python и C++ — решил его разыграть и переписал весь код Джимми на C++. Теперь Джимми в отчаянии и не может ничего найти в своём старом коде, потому что всё на C++! Помогите создать для Джимми хороший поисковик — такой, который сможет находить соответствующий код на C++ по фрагменту кода на Python.
### Описание набора данных
Вам дан набор данных из пар — исходный код на Python и исходный код на C++ — это некий журнал, который Джимми однажды украл у Айбека.
### Подзадачи
#### Подзадача 1
По заданному запросу $q$ ваша задача — найти подходящих кандидатов, соответствующих $q$ на другом языке программирования.
### Формат вывода
Выходной файл (`.csv`) должен содержать 3 столбца:
subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
представляет идентификатор подзадачи (в вашем случае всегда 1) | идентификаторы из тестовых данных | ответ для каждого идентификатора

Вы должны отправить только один файл `.csv` (см. `sample_output.csv`)
### Оценивание
#### Подзадача 1
Метрика, которая будет использоваться для оценки вашего решения — Mean Reciprocal Rank@1
$$\text{MRR} = \frac{1}{|Q|} \sum_{i=1}^{|Q|} \frac{1}{\text{rank}_i}$$
Где:
- $|Q|$ — общее количество запросов
- $\text{rank}_i$ — позиция в ранжировании первого релевантного результата для $i$-го запроса

---

Джеймс пен Айбек — бір туған ағайынды. Олар бір-бірін қалжыңдап жазғыруды ұнатады және жалпы бірге көп қызықты уақыт өткізеді. Бір күні

tasks.name: AI Olympiad 2026 Second Round (cpfed) — Object Detection with Pseudo-Labels
tasks.description: Построение детектора объектов на смешанном датасете: 50 изображений с полноценными bounding box разметками и 200 изображений только с метками классификатора. Метрика — mAP@[.50:.95] по 10 классам. Требует использования псевдо-разметки для увеличения обучающей выборки.
tasks.full_description:
Junichiro loves to take pictures and wants to start labelling his pictures with a detector. Help him build a detector!

### Dataset Description

Junichiro assembled a small dataset of annotated pictures - 50 pictures with bounding boxes and classes and then got really lazy and sleepy and labeled other 200 with a simple classifier label

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 - Building a good detector!

Your task is to build a good detector for Junichiro to label his pictures based on his dataset

### Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
represents the subtask id (in your case always 1) | the IDs from the test data | answer for each ID

You must only send one `.csv` file (see `sample_output.csv`)


### Scoring

#### Subtask 1

Junichiro wants to judge you based on mean Average Precision @ IoU = 50:95 averaged across classes

$$\text{AP} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{t \in \{0.50,\, 0.55,\, 0.60,\, \ldots,\, 0.95\}} \text{AP}_t$$

where each $\text{AP}_t$ is computed as:

$$\text{AP}_t = \int_0^1 p_t(r)\, dr$$

and $p_t(r)$ is the precision at recall $r$ using IoU threshold $t$ and then average for 10 classes

Simply put,
When your model detects an object, we need to decide if that detection is "correct." We do this by measuring how much the predicted box overlaps with the ground-truth box — that overlap ratio is called **IoU** (Intersection over Union). A higher IoU means a tighter, more accurate box.

Instead of picking just one IoU threshold (like "count it as correct if overlap ≥ 50%"), AP@[.50:.95] evaluates your model at **ten thresholds**: 50%, 55%, 60%, … all the way up to 95%. At each threshold, you compute Average Precision (the area under the precision-recall curve). Then you simply **average those ten AP values**.

This gives a single number that rewards models not just for *finding* the right objects, but for *localizing* them precisely. A model that produces loose, sloppy boxes will do fine at the easy 50% threshold but will fail at the strict 90%+ thresholds, dragging its average down.

---

Джуничиро любит фотографировать и хочет начать размечать свои фотографии с помощью детектора. Помогите ему создать детектор!
### Описание набора данных
Джуничиро собрал небольшой набор данных с аннотированными фотографиями — 50 фотографий с ограничивающими рамками и классами, а потом стал очень ленивым и сонным и разметил оставшиеся 200 простой меткой классификатора.
### Подзадачи
#### Подзадача 1 — Создание хорошего детектора!
Ваша задача — создать хороший детектор для Джуничиро, чтобы он мог размечать свои фотографии на основе его набора данных.
### Формат вывода
Выходной файл (`.csv`) должен содержать 3 столбца:
subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
представляет идентификатор подзадачи (в вашем случае всегда 1) | идентификаторы из тестовых данных | ответ для каждого идентификатора

Вы должны отправить только один файл `.csv` (см. `sample_output.csv`)
### Оценивание
#### Подзадача 1
Джуничиро хо

tasks.name: AI Olympiad 2026 Second Round (cpfed) — Code Author Clustering by Style Features
tasks.description: Группировка авторов кода по стилю написания на основе 29 признаков (camelCase vs snake_case, табы vs пробелы и т.д.). На тесте — совершенно новые авторы с неизвестным количеством кластеров, метрика — Adjusted Rand Index. Классическая задача unsupervised кластеризации табличных признаков.
tasks.full_description:
Zhuldyzzhan runs a detective agency that stalks hackers. Hackers are very picky and very proud of their work, so they obey to their own style, but they often switch accounts and they have thousands of them. Zhuldyzzhan's firm managed to find hideout of hackers and get hands on their codes, but its all shuffled with some other source codes from GitHub, however agency staff managed to extract some features and you are gonna act as helpers for Zhuldyzzhan agency and group authors of codes by their style.

### Dataset Description

You are given a dataset of 29 features of different code writing patterns (whether author uses camelcase or snakecase, how consistent is author in putting tabs or spaces and etc.) all of the features are deanonymized and you can read what they mean by reading the name of subsequent columns. Training data also includes author_id that Zhuldyzzhan managed to uncover

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 - Grouping Authors together

Zhuldyzzhan's agency wants you to design an algorithm, that would be given features of how code is written by a certain user would allow you to group it to the same author. In other words, you would need to detect from the sea of features and embeddings who are just the same person running multiple accounts.

Remember, Zhuldyzzhan wants a robust algorithm, you wont classify the same users on testing data, testing data would involve completely new users and whats worse is unknown real number of them.

### Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

subtaskID | datapointID | answer
-|-|-
represents the subtask id (always 1 in your case) | the IDs from the test data | answer for each ID

You must only send one `.csv` file (see `sample_output.csv`)

### Scoring

#### Subtask 1

Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) is defined as:

$$ARI = \frac{\sum_{ij}\binom{n_{ij}}{2} - \left[\sum_i\binom{a_i}{2}\cdot\sum_j\binom{b_j}{2}\right] / \binom{n}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}\left[\sum_i\binom{a_i}{2} + \sum_j\binom{b_j}{2}\right] - \left[\sum_i\binom{a_i}{2}\cdot\sum_j\binom{b_j}{2}\right] / \binom{n}{2}}$$

Where, given a contingency table of two clusterings $U$ and $V$:

- $n_{ij}$ is the number of objects in both cluster $i$ of $U$ and cluster $j$ of $V$
- $a_i = \sum_j n_{ij}$ is the row sum (size of cluster $i$ in $U$)
- $b_j = \sum_i n_{ij}$ is the column sum (size of cluster $j$ in $V$)
- $n$ is the total number of objects

The ARI ranges from −1 to 1, where 1 indicates perfect agreement, 0 indicates agreement expected by chance, and negative values indicate less agreement than expected by chance.

There is an easy explanation to ARI without too much scare in the formula:

Take every **pair of points** in your dataset. For each pair, two things can happen in your clustering and in the true labels:

1. **Same-same:** the pair is together in your clustering *and* together in the true labels
2. **Apart-apart:** the pair is separated in your clustering *and* separated in the true labels
3. **They disagree:** togethe

tasks.name: Nitro AI Warmup Round — Steam Game Price Regression
tasks.description: Предсказание цены игр в Steam по табличным признакам (Metacritic score, жанры, издатели, оценки владельцев, лайки/дизлайки) с метрикой MAE. Подзадачи включают парсинг диапазона Estimated Owners в среднее значение и построение регрессионной модели. Требуется обработка списковых и категориальных признаков.
tasks.full_description:
## Enunț
Implementați un model de AI pentru a prezice prețurile din jocurile de pe Steam având la dispoziție un set de antrenare `train_data.csv` și unul pe care trebuie să faceți doar predicția `test_data.csv`.

Setul de date conține următoarele câmpuri având semnificațiile:

- `Name`: nume aplicație
- `AppID`: identificatorul jocului
- `Metacritic score`: nota medie a jocului de pe platforma de recenzii Metacritic
- `Genres`: o lista separata prin virgule conținând tag-uri referitoare la tipul de joc
- `Publishers`: numele producătorilor
- `Estimated Owners`: de forma min – max, însemnând așteptările (producătorilor jocului) minime și maxime de jocuri vândute
- `Negatives`: numărul de dislike-uri
- `Positives`: numărul de like-uri
- `Recommendations`: câte persoane recomandă acest joc

## Note despre setul de date:

- Câmpul-țintă este „Price”. Date fiind celelalte feature-uri, scopul este de a prezice „Price”. Setul de date de evaluare vizibil pentru candidați nu conține acest câmp. Metrica de evaluare folosita este MAE
- Câmpul Genres, fiind o listă, trebuie transformat în date numerice într-un fel sau altul pentru a putea fi folosit in algoritmii de ML.
- Asemănător pentru câmpul Estimated Owners
- Unele câmpuri pot fi inutile in predicția câmpului-țintă. Încercați să analizați datele și să selectați doar ce este nevoie sau ar putea explica predicția.

## Cerințe

**Subtask 1 (40p)**. 
"Avg owners": pentru fiecare camp din coloana "Estimated Owners" de forma min – max, extrageți media valorilor considerând pentru fiecare linie int ((min + max) / 2).

**Subtask 2(60p)** 
"Price": Implementați un model de AI și rulați predicția pentru fiecare rând din `test_data.csv`. 

## Format de ieșire

Fișierul de ieșire încărcat de tip `.csv` trebuie să conțină 3 coloane:

- `subtaskID` - reprezintă numărul subtaskului (1 sau 2)
- `datapointID` - care se referă la coloana `id` din `test_data.csv`
- `answer` - răspunsul corespunzător datapointului pentru subtaskul respectiv

Trimiteți un singur csv care să conțină răspunsurile pentru toate subtask-urile pe care le-ați rezolvat. Pentru a vedea un exemplu, descărcați fișierul `sample_output.csv`

tasks.name: ROAI Selection Camp - CPU Practical Round — Too Easy Fairy 1-Shot Segmentation
tasks.description: 1-shot semantic segmentation с использованием DINOv2-фич: для каждой картинки даны замороженные 16×16×384 patch features + один foreground-пиксель + один background-пиксель. Нужно вырастить бинарную маску по 256 патчам из этих двух «семян». Метрика — IoU. Без обучения сегментатора (zero/1-shot, frozen DINOv2).
tasks.full_description:
It is a quiet afternoon at the national AI selection camp. You are currently training a massive U-Net architecture on a dataset of 100,000 perfectly segmented images. 

"Too easy, isn't it?" a voice echoes from your cooling fans. Your terminal goes black. You have summoned the **Too Easy Fairy**.

"You think learning to segment objects is impressive when I hand you millions of labeled pixels?" she laughs. With a flick of her digital wand, your entire hard drive is wiped. 

"Let's play a game of extreme scarcity. I have given you a dataset of images. But I deleted the masks. For each image, I will give you exactly **one pixel** that belongs to the object, and **one pixel** that belongs to the background. That is your entire training signal."

She leaves you with one tool: a pre-extracted feature map from a frozen Foundation Model (DINOv2). 

"The Foundation Model has already looked at the images and extracted deep semantic concepts into a $16 \times 16$ grid. Your job is to mathematically grow my two tiny seeds into a perfect, full silhouette. Good luck."

### Problem Description:
You are tasked with **Zero-Shot / 1-Shot Semantic Segmentation**.
For each image, you are given a tensor of shape `(16, 16, 384)`. These are the DINOv2 patch features. You are also given the exact $(X, Y)$ coordinates of one Foreground pixel and one Background pixel.

You must output a binary mask indicating which of the 256 patches belong to the foreground object. 

### Dataset Description:
*   `features/`: A folder containing `.npy` files of shape `(16, 16, 384)` for each image.
*   `seeds.csv`: A file containing `image_id`, `fg_x`, `fg_y`, `bg_x`, `bg_y`. (Coordinates are in $224 \times 224$ space).

### Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain 3 columns:

| subtaskID | datapointID | answer |
| 1 | 1 | 0,0,...,0 |
| Represents the subtask ID (1) | The image id | The predictions for each patch |

You must only send one `.csv` file containing predictions for both subtasks.

### Scoring

The scoring is based on the dice score. See in the starter_kit.py the exact implementation used for the evaluation function.

| Metric                   | Score                                       |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------- |
| Dice `< 0.40`        | 5 points                                    |
| `0.40 ≤ Dice < 0.78` | Linearly normalized between 5 and 100 points |
| `0.78 ≤ Dice`        | 100 points                                   |

tasks.name: ROAI Selection Camp - GPU Practical Round — Multi-Caption Image Captioning
tasks.description: Image captioning ассистента: по фото сгенерировать короткое и понятное описание. Одна картинка может иметь несколько валидных подписей (на румынском). Train: пары (image, captions) с несколькими ground-truth подписями на картинку. Метрика — какая-то вариация BLEU/CIDEr/семантика с учётом множественных эталонов.
tasks.full_description:
Imagine that you are building a small assistant that helps people understand images automatically.

A user uploads a photo, and the assistant must describe what is visible in that image using short and clear sentences. However, an image does not have only one correct description. The same image can often be described in several valid ways.

For example, an image could be described as:

- `51 este numar prim?`
- `6 rate sunt in poza`
- `100 de puncte`
- `o bulaneala pentru calificare la inter`
- `ratele nu stiu matematica`

All these descriptions are examples of possible textual outputs. In the actual task, the generated descriptions should be relevant to the image and should describe its visual content.

Your task in this competition is to generate multiple textual descriptions for each image.

### Dataset Description

The dataset consists of images paired with multiple short textual descriptions.

Each image is associated with several alternative captions that describe the same visual content using slightly different wording. The annotations are simple, concise, and semantically similar.

This means that there may be multiple correct ways to describe the same image.


### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 - Image Description Generation

For each image in the test set, participants must generate exactly **5 textual descriptions**.

Each description must be a string written in natural language. The descriptions should accurately reflect the content of the image.

The expected output for each image is a list of exactly **5 strings**.

Each string represents one possible valid description of the image.

Example output:

```json
[
  "Is 51 a prime number?",
  "There are 6 ducks in the picture",
  "100 points",
  "A lucky break for qualifying to the international stage",
  "Ducks do not know mathematics"
]
````

### Evaluation Metric

The evaluation metric is based on the average of **BLEU-1** and **BLEU-2**.

Let:

```text
M = (BLEU_1 + BLEU_2) / 2
```

BLEU is a text similarity metric. It compares the generated descriptions with the reference descriptions from the dataset.


#### BLEU-1

BLEU-1 measures the overlap of individual words between a generated caption and the reference captions.

For example, if the reference caption is:

```text
There are 6 ducks in the picture
```

and the generated caption is:

```text
The ducks in the picture
```

then BLEU-1 rewards the matching words:

```text
 ducks, in, the, picture
```


#### BLEU-2

BLEU-2 measures the overlap of consecutive pairs of words, also called bigrams.

For example, in the sentence:

```text
There are 6 ducks in the picture
```

the bigrams are:

```text
There are
are 6
6 ducks
ducks in
in the
the picture
```

### Scoring

The final metric is computed as:

```text
M = (BLEU_1 + BLEU_2) / 2
```

|      Metric value M |                                             Score |
| ------------------: | ------------------------------------------------: |
|          `M < 0.11` |                                        `5 points` |
|   `0.11 ≤ M ≤ 0.62` |  Linear interpolation between `5` and `60` points |
|  `0.62 < M ≤ 0.665` | Linear interpolation between `60` and `75` points |
| `0.665 < M ≤ 0.685` | Linear interpolation between `75` and `90` points |
| `0.685 < M ≤ 0.710` | Linear interpolation between `90` and `95` points |
|         `M > 0.710` |                                      `100 points` |

tasks.name: ROAI Selection Camp - GPU Practical Round — Perceptual Image Similarity (Inception Features)
tasks.description: Перцептивное сравнение изображений: pixel-RMSE не работает при сдвиге/повороте/цветовых сдвигах. Нужно сравнивать в feature space pretrained InceptionV3 (ImageNet) — feature maps инвариантны к мелким геометрическим трансформациям. Метрика — какая-то forms of perceptual distance/MSE в feature space.
tasks.full_description:
## Theory

### Perceptual comparison

Pixel-level RMSE (root mean square error) fails as soon as images are not perfectly aligned: a small translation, rotation, or color shift inflates the error even though the content is unchanged. RMSE also has no notion of *what* the image depicts – it treats every pixel as an independent scalar. The fix is to compare images in the feature space of a pretrained CNN. `InceptionV3`, trained on ImageNet, produces representations that are progressively invariant to low-level perturbations and progressively sensitive to semantic content.

The key hyperparameter is the cut depth: how many layers of `InceptionV3` the image is passed through before its activations are read out. Cutting after only a few layers leaves the representation close to raw pixels – edges and textures – so it inherits most of pixel-space sensitivity to jitter. Cutting near the end of the network gives abstract class-oriented features that discard the detail distinguishing instances within a class, collapsing similar images together. The useful regime is an intermediate cut: deep enough to abstract away pixel-level jitter, shallow enough to preserve instance-level detail. *Practical notes*: `InceptionV3` expects ImageNet-normalized inputs (provided in the starter kit) and comparisons use cosine similarity or L2 distance on the flattened or pooled activations.

### Bipartite matching
Once features and images live in a common representation, recovering the pairing reduces to matching each feature to exactly one image so that the total perceptual distance across all pairs is minimized. Concretely, with N features and N images, build an N × N cost matrix C where C[i, j] is the perceptual distance between the i-th decoded feature and the j-th image, and find the one-to-one assignment that minimizes the sum of selected entries.

A greedy nearest-neighbor strategy does not produce a valid pairing: several features compete for the same image, and ad hoc tie-breaking is suboptimal. The Hungarian algorithm solves this problem and should be treated as a black box: `scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment(C)` returns the optimal pairing. The result depends almost entirely on the quality of the cost matrix, i.e. on the perceptual representation used to build it.


## Problem Statement

A trained image autoencoder has been corrupted. The decoder weights were truncated to one decimal place, the stored feature vectors carry added Gaussian noise, the corresponding images were lightly jittered, and the image folder was shuffled. The decoder still produces recognizable but degraded outputs, and the pairing between features and images has been lost. Your task is to recover that pairing and then repair the decoder well enough to reconstruct a held-out set of test features.

Perceptual comparisons should be made with `InceptionV3` features; pixel-level RMSE will not work well because of the image jitter.

### Dataset Description

The images are 160×160 RGB. They were all encoded through the same hidden encoder → 128-d feature → decoder pipeline. You receive:

- **Train Data (problem_data)**:
  - **Subtask 1 data**:
    - `task1/unordered/features.npy` + `task1/unordered/img_*.jpg` – `N=1500` noisy features and `N=1500` augmented images, **shuffled relative to each other**. Recovering the pairing is Subtask 1.
    - `task1/ordered/features.npy` + `task1/ordered/img_*.jpg` – a self-check pool of 500 noisy features paired **by index** with their augmented images, for local validation.
  - **Subtask 2 data**:
    - `task2/train/features.npy` + `task2/train/img_*.jpg` – `4000` features paired **by index** with `4000` clean images. Training data for Subtask 2.
    - `task2/test/features.npy` – `M=500` clean test features. Decode each with your fine-tuned decoder and submit the resulting perceptual features.

- **Starter Kit (starter_kit)**:
  - `model.py` – model architecture and a `load_decoder()` helper.
  - `submission.py` – `submit_task1(answers)` and `submit_task2(images)` helpers that build the CSV rows for the submissions.
  - `data_loader.py` — `load_data(root_dir, task, split)` helper that returns the latent features `X` (shape `(N, 128)`) and the corresponding images `y` (shape `(N, 3, 160, 160)`, `None` for `subtask2/test`) for any task/split pair:
    - `load_data(DATA_DIR, task="subtask1", split={"unordered"/"ordered"})`
    - `load_data(DATA_DIR, task="subtask2, split={"train"/"test"})`
  - `decoder.pt` – the quantized decoder (fp16, weights truncated to one decimal).
  - `load_utility.py` — InceptionV3 loaders that work offline:
    - `load_base_inception(weights_path)` builds InceptionV3 and loads the pre-trained weights with all parameters frozen
    -  `load_inception_transforms()` returns a module that resizes inputs to 299×299 and applies the ImageNet mean/std normalization
  - `inception_v3.pth` — the pretrained InceptionV3 weights.

### Subtasks

#### Subtask 1 – Feature ↔ Image Pairing (50 points)

For each feature in `task1/unordered/features.npy`, identify the image in `task1/unordered/` it came from.

- Answer space: integer image index in `[0, N)`.
- Output: `subtaskID = 1`, `answer = matched image index`.
- Metric: exact-match accuracy of the recovered permutation.

**Approach.** Decode each feature through the shipped decoder, compare the result to every gallery image in Inception feature space, and recover the pairing with `scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment`. The choice of Inception cut depth materially affects accuracy and should be validated against `task1/ordered/`. Remember to apply the `torchvision` ImageNet normalization before passing images to Inception.

A direct implementation against the corrupted decoder typically yields **~30/50 points**. Reaching the 50/50 ceiling would require fine-tuning the decoder first (*Subtask 2*) and re-running the matching.

#### Subtask 2 – Decoder Repair (50 points)

For each feature in `task2/test/features.npy`, decode it with your fine-tuned decoder and submit a perceptual-feature vector for the result.

- Compute features with **InceptionV3, using the same cut depth chosen in Subtask 1**.
- Pass them to `submit_task2(features)` from `submission.py`. The helper avg-pools spatially, so 4-D tensors of shape `(N, C, H, W)` are accepted directly, and the resulting 1-D vector per image is base64-encoded.
- Output: `subtaskID = 2`, `answer = base64-encoded float32 feature bytes`.
- Metric: mean cosine similarity between your features and the hidden reference features.

The reference is computed by running the *original* (uncorrupted) decoder on each test feature, applying the same Inception cut, and storing the pooled activations.

**Approach.** The shipped decoder gives noticeable error when used directly. A short fine-tune on the `(feature, clean_image)` pairs from `task2/train/`, with a regularizer pulling weights toward the shipped initialization, recovers most of the lost quality and yields **50/50 points** when well-tuned. The same fine-tuned decoder is what lifts Subtask 1 to its ceiling.

### Output Format

Submit a single `submission.csv` with columns `subtaskID`, `datapointID`, `answer`, assembled via the helpers in `submission.py`:

```python
import pandas as pd
from submission import submit_task1, submit_task2

# Subtask 1: list of N integer image indices
df1 = submit_task1(predicted_image_indices)

# Subtask 2: perceptual features for your decoded test images.
# Use the Inception cut chosen for Subtask 1; the helper avg-pools spatially.
df2 = submit_task2(decoded_image_features)     # (M, C, H, W) or (M, C) array

pd.concat([df1, df2]).to_csv("submission.csv", index=False)
```

A `sample_output.csv` is provided as a format reference.

### Scoring

A format-valid submission with metrics below the baseline still earns a small floor score (5 points per subtask) — `sample_output.csv` therefore yields 10 points overall.

#### Subtask 1

| Metric | Score |
|--------|-------|
| Accuracy < `0.30` | 5 points |
| `0.30` ≤ Accuracy < `0.90` | linear, 5 → 15 points |
| `0.90` ≤ Accuracy ≤ `0.93` | linear, 15 → 30 points |
| `0.93` < Accuracy ≤ `0.97` | linear, 30 → 40 points |
| `0.93` < Accuracy ≤ `0.98` | linear, 30 → 45 points |
| `0.98` < Accuracy | 50 points |

The 0.93 → 0.98 segment is steeper: getting past the knee at 0.93 typically requires improving the decoder (Subtask 2 first) and re-running the matching.

#### Subtask 2

The submitted features are spatially avg-pooled by `submit_task2` and compared to the hidden reference features by cosine similarity. Use the same Inception cut you found optimal for Subtask 1.

| Metric | Score |
|--------|-------|
| Cosine < `0.80` | 0 points |
| `0.80` ≤ Cosine ≤ `0.94` | linear, 0 → 45 points |
| `0.94` < Cosine | 50 points |

topics: Encoder-Decoder Models (e.g. for Machine Translation or Vision-Language Modeling)
Loss Functions (MSE, MAE, Cross Entropy, etc.)
Model Ensembles (Gradient Boosting, Bagging, Random Forest)
Model Evaluation Metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, etc.)
NumPy and Pandas for Data Handling
Pre-trained Language Models (open-source and API-based ones)
Regularization: Dropout, Early Stopping, Weight Decay
Training Models on CPU and GPU
Transformers (theory needed only for text and image)
